- The **script engine ↔ database round-trip** (steps 9–13) is the critical latency source.
- The **template engine** (steps 14–15) is what produces the final HTML at runtime.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a static website. List any two of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A static website is a collection of web pages whose content is **pre-built and stored as files** (HTML, CSS, JS) on the web server. When a user requests a page, the server delivers the file **without any server-side processing or database interaction**.

**Key Characteristics (any 2):**
1. **Fixed Content** — Every visitor receives identical HTML; the page does not change per user or per request.
2. **No Database / No Server-Side Scripting** — Content is hard-coded; updates require manual file editing.
3. **Fast Delivery** — Suitable for CDN caching; minimal server load.
4. **Example Use Case** — Portfolios, brochures, documentation, landing pages.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + 2 Characteristics (1 each) = **3 Marks**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a dynamic website? Give two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A dynamic website generates web pages **on-the-fly at the moment of a user's request** using server-side scripting languages and database queries. The content **varies per user, per session, and per request**, allowing personalization and real-time data display.

**Two Real-World Examples:**
1. **Amazon.com** — Product listings, prices, and recommendations change based on user profile, location, and browsing history.
2. **Facebook.com** — News feed is dynamically generated from user connections, likes, and recent activity.
3. **(Alt)** **Gmail.com** — Inbox content is fetched from the database per user session.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + 2 Examples (1 each) = **3 Marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **static website** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the architecture of a **dynamic website** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — Static Website Architecture (7 Marks)**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
A static website delivers **pre-rendered HTML files** stored on the web server. There is **no server-side processing, no database, and no personalization**.

**2. Block Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
┌────────────┐    HTTP GET    ┌────────────┐   File Read   ┌─────────────┐
│   Browser  │ ─────────────► │ Web Server │ ────────────► │  .html File │
│            │ ◄───────────── │ (Apache/   │ ◄──────────── │  on Disk    │
└────────────┘   HTTP 200     │   Nginx)   │   File Bytes  └─────────────┘
                              └────────────┘
        │
        │  + Static assets: .css, .js, .png, .jpg
        ▼
   (No Database, No Script Engine)
```

**3. Working (1 Mark):**
- User types URL → DNS resolves → browser sends HTTP request → server reads matching file → file returned in HTTP response → browser renders.

**4. Advantages (1.5 Marks):**
- **Speed** — Minimal server processing; fast page load.
- **Low Cost** — Can be hosted on free tiers (GitHub Pages, Netlify).
- **High Security** — No user input is processed, so SQLi/XSS attack surface is minimal.
- **CDN-Friendly** — Easily cached and distributed globally.
- **SEO Advantage** — Search engines can index quickly.

**5. Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
- **Difficult to Maintain** — Updating content requires editing each HTML file manually.
- **No Personalization** — Cannot show user-specific content.
- **Limited Interactivity** — No real-time data, no user login, no forms (beyond client-side).
- **Scalability Bottleneck at Updates** — Large sites (1000+ pages) become unmanageable.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — Dynamic Website Architecture (7 Marks)**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
A dynamic website generates HTML pages **at runtime** using server-side scripts, databases, and templates. The response is **tailored per user, per session, and per request**.

**2. Block Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
┌────────────┐  HTTP Request  ┌────────────┐  Forward    ┌──────────────┐
│   Browser  │ ────────────► │ Web Server │ ──────────► │ Script Engine│
│            │ ◄──────────── │ (Apache)   │ ◄────────── │ (PHP/Node)   │
└────────────┘   HTTP 200    └────────────┘  HTML       └──────┬───────┘
                                       ▲                       │
                                       │                       │ Query
                                       │                       ▼
                                  ┌────┴─────┐         ┌──────────────┐
                                  │ Template │ ◄───────│   Database   │
                                  │  Engine  │  Render │   (MySQL)    │
                                  └──────────┘         └──────────────┘
```

**3. Working (1 Mark):**
- Browser sends request → server routes to script → script reads parameters/session → queries database → applies business logic → renders template → returns HTML.

**4. Advantages (1.5 Marks):**
- **Personalization** — Content adapts to user identity, location, and preferences.
- **Easy Content Management** — Admin panels (CMS like WordPress) allow non-technical updates.
- **Real-Time Data** — Stock prices, sports scores, news feeds are always current.
- **User Interaction** — Login, comments, e-commerce transactions, form processing.
- **Database-Driven** — Centralized data management.

**5. Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
- **Higher Cost** — Requires server runtime + database licensing + DevOps.
- **Slower Performance** — Database round-trips and script execution add latency.
- **Security Risks** — SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, session hijacking must be mitigated.
- **Complex Deployment** — Requires server configuration, database setup, environment management.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare and contrast **static and dynamic websites** across any **seven** dimensions in a tabular format. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable real-world example, explain how a **dynamic e-commerce website** processes a product search query step-by-step from URL entry to page render. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)**

| # | Parameter | Static Website | Dynamic Website |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Content Source** | Pre-built files on disk | Generated at runtime via scripts + DB |
| 2 | **Server-Side Processing** | None | Script execution (PHP, Node, JSP) |
| 3 | **Database** | Not used | Mandatory (MySQL, MongoDB) |
| 4 | **Personalization** | Not possible | Full (user-specific content) |
| 5 | **Update Mechanism** | Manual HTML edit & upload | Admin panel / CMS |
| 6 | **Performance** | Very fast (< 100 ms) | Slower (200 ms – 2 s) |
| 7 | **Hosting Cost** | Very low (free tiers) | Higher (server + DB + DevOps) |
| 8 | **Security Surface** | Small | Large (SQLi, XSS, CSRF) |
| 9 | **Examples** | Portfolio, docs site | Amazon, Facebook, Gmail |
| 10 | **Technologies** | HTML, CSS, JS, Hugo, Jekyll | LAMP, MERN, MEAN, Django |

**Mark Distribution:** 7 dimensions × 1 mark each = **7 Marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — E-commerce Product Search Workflow (7 Marks)**

**Scenario:** User searches for *"laptop under 50000"* on `www.shopkart.com`.

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** User enters `www.shopkart.com/search?q=laptop&max_price=50000` in the browser. DNS resolves the domain to the server IP.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Browser sends an HTTP GET request with the query string. The web server (Nginx) receives the request and routes it to the appropriate controller (e.g., `ProductController` in Node.js).

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** The script parses the query parameters: `q = "laptop"`, `max_price = 50000`. It also reads any user session cookies to fetch personalization data (location, currency).

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** The script queries the database (MongoDB / MySQL) with a parameterized query:
```
db.products.find({ name: /laptop/i, price: { $lte: 50000 } }).sort({ rating: -1 }).limit(20)
```

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** The database returns the top 20 matching products. The script applies additional business logic: filter out-of-stock items, apply user-specific discounts, sort by relevance and rating.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** The result set is passed to the template engine (EJS / Handlebars), which injects the product cards into an HTML layout, including CSS classes, image URLs, and JavaScript event handlers for the "Add to Cart" button.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** The final HTML is sent back to the browser with HTTP 200 OK. The browser parses, applies CSS, runs client-side JS (lazy-loading images, infinite scroll), and displays the search results page within ~600 ms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in the KTU 2024 board exam (and how to avoid them):**

1. **Writing "dynamic website is interactive, static is not"** — This is **oversimplified** and loses 1 mark. A static site *can* have interactivity via client-side JavaScript. The correct distinction is **server-side processing**, not interactivity alone.

2. **Confusing "dynamic" with "animated"** — Many students write about CSS animations and hover effects. **Wrong.** A dynamic website refers to **server-side content generation**, not visual motion. A website can be static and full of animations.

3. **Forgetting the database in the dynamic diagram** — In the block diagram question, students often show Browser → Server → HTML and skip the database and script engine. This is a **2-mark deduction** in the KTU scheme.

4. **Not giving examples** — KTU board examiners expect **at least one example** for static (e.g., `wikipedia.org/maps`) and one for dynamic (e.g., `amazon.com`). Skipping examples costs 1 mark.

5. **Mixing up terms "web page" and "website"** — A **web page** is a single document; a **website** is a collection. Use the correct term.

6. **Ignoring security in dynamic disadvantages** — Always mention **SQL injection and XSS** as risks in dynamic sites. Examiners specifically test this.

7. **Not stating the HTTP status code** — When explaining server responses, mention `200 OK` for static and dynamic success cases. This is a favorite 1-mark "extra" question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1, Topic: Types of Websites**

### 🔑 Key Definitions to Memorize
- **Static Website** → Fixed content, served as-is, no server-side processing, no database.
- **Dynamic Website** → Content generated at request time, uses server-side scripts + database.
- **Web Page** vs **Website** → Single document vs. collection of interlinked pages.
- **HTTP** → HyperText Transfer Protocol (port 80); **HTTPS** → port 443.

### 🔑 Key Technical Terms
- **LAMP Stack** → Linux, Apache, MySQL, PHP (dynamic).
- **MERN Stack** → MongoDB, Express.js, React.js, Node.js (dynamic).
- **Jamstack** → JavaScript, APIs, Markup (modern hybrid).
- **CDN** → Content Delivery Network (best for static assets).
- **CMS** → Content Management System (e.g., WordPress — dynamic).

### 🔑 7 Standard Comparison Points
1. Content Source
2. Server-Side Processing
3. Database Usage
4. Personalization
5. Update Mechanism
6. Performance / Speed
7. Security Surface

### 🔑 3 Real-World Examples Each
- **Static:** GitHub Pages portfolio, Wikipedia articles, news landing pages.
- **Dynamic:** Amazon product pages, Facebook feed, Gmail inbox.

### 🔑 Must-Use Phrases in Answers
- "Server-side script execution" (not just "interactive")
- "Database-driven content" (not just "changes often")
- "Pre-rendered HTML" (for static) and "Runtime HTML generation" (for dynamic)
- "Per-request personalization" (for dynamic)
- "CDN cacheable" (for static)

### 🔑 Numbers to Remember
- **HTTP port:** `80` (plain) / `443` (secure)
- **Static response time:** `< 100 ms`
- **Dynamic response time:** `200 ms – 2 s`
- **Cache hit ratio (static):** `> 90%`
- **LAMP port for MySQL:** `3306` (bonus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Give an example of a static website," **do not** say "Facebook is dynamic and Instagram is static." Instagram is fully dynamic. Safe static examples: **Wikipedia, a personal blog built with Jekyll, an online resume hosted on GitHub Pages.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

It is the foundational layer of all web documents and is maintained by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** and the **WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **Modern Web Applications (PECBT633)**, HTML is the **first pillar** of the front-end triad (HTML + CSS + JavaScript). The current standard taught is **HTML5**, which introduced semantic elements, native multimedia support (`

topics.title: JSON
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** JSON # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** is a lightweight, text-based, language-independent data interchange format derived from the object literal syntax of JavaScript (as defined in the ECMA-262 standard). It is formally specified in two Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) Requests for Comments: - **RFC 8259** — *The JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) Data Interchange Format* (published by T. Bray in December 2017, obsoletes RFC 7159). - **RFC 6907** — Specifies the `application/json` Media Type for JSON. JSON is standardized as **ECMA-404 (December 2017)** by Ecma International. It is **not** a document format (like XML/HTML), but a pure **data-serialization format** used to transport and store structured information across heterogeneous systems (web servers, mobile clients, IoT devices, REST APIs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** JSON is a foundational building block of modern web development. It is the de-facto data format for **RESTful APIs** (covered later in this course), AJAX calls, configuration files, NoSQL databases (MongoDB), and front-end frameworks (React, Angular, Vue). Expect direct 3-mark definitions and applied 14-mark questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you run a multi-cuisine restaurant chain where the **Head Chef (Server)** needs to send a daily recipe list to **Branch Managers (Clients)** located in different cities, each of whom speaks a different native language (Java, Python, JavaScript, C#, PHP). The Head Chef cannot send a recipe in a complex legal contract format (that would be like **XML** — heavy, verbose, full of tags). Instead, the chef writes the recipe on a **single, universal index card** using a simple, structured shorthand: - Ingredients on the left, quantities on the right. - A list of cooking steps. - A nested box containing a sub-recipe for the sauce. **JSON is that universal index card for computers.** Every modern programming language can read it, write it, and translate it into its own native data structures. The card itself is just plain text (UTF-8), so it travels through any network, email, or storage medium without corruption. | Real-World Object | JSON Equivalent | |---|---| | A recipe card | A JSON document | | Ingredient: Quantity | `"flour": "500g"` (key:value) | | A numbered cooking step | An array element `[step1, step2]` | | A sub-recipe box | A nested object `{ "sauce": { ... } }` | ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties JSON's design enforces the following measurable characteristics, frequently tested in KTU exams: - **Media Type:** `application/json` - **Default Encoding:** **UTF-8** (UTF-16 or UTF-32 are permitted but never emitted by default). - **MIME Compatibility:** `text/json` is sometimes seen but **non-standard**. - **File Extension:** `.json` - **Schema Definition Language:** **JSON Schema** (Draft 2020-12, IETF Draft). - **Self-describing:** No external DTD/XSD required — structure is embedded in the document. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Key Takeaway** > **JSON** = a language-independent, human-readable, text-based **data interchange format** that represents data as **attribute–value pairs** (objects) and **ordered lists** (arrays), making it the universal translator of the modern web. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Structural Rules of Valid JSON A KTU examiner can award or deduct marks based on whether a student can identify a *valid* vs *invalid* JSON string. A document is **syntactically valid JSON** if and only if **all six** rules below are satisfied simultaneously: 1. **Data is in name/value pairs** → written as `"name" : value` 2. **Data is separated by commas** → between every pair/element 3. **Curly braces `{}` hold objects** → unordered collection of key:value pairs 4. **Square brackets `[]` hold arrays** → ordered list of values 5. **Strings MUST be enclosed in double quotes** → single quotes are illegal 6. **Whitespace, tabs, and newlines are ignored** → purely for human readability > [!WARNING] > Common KTU Pitfall: Trailing commas (e.g., `[1, 2, 3, ]`) are **forbidden** in JSON, unlike JavaScript object literals. A single trailing comma makes the entire document invalid. ## 2.2 The Seven Permissible Data Types JSON supports a strictly closed, type-safe set of values. A JSON value MUST be **exactly one** of the following primitive or structural types: | # | Type | Syntax Example | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **String** | `"Hello KTU"` | Must use **double quotes**; supports `\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\"`, `\/`, `\b`, `\f`, `\uXXXX` escape sequences | | 2 | **Number** | `42`, `-17`, `3.1415`, `2.5e10` | No distinction between int/float; **NaN** and **Infinity** are **forbidden** | | 3 | **Boolean** | `true` or `false` | Lowercase only — `True`/`FALSE` are invalid | | 4 | **Null** | `null` | Represents intentional absence of value (lowercase) | | 5 | **Object** | `{ "key": value, ... }` | Unordered collection; keys MUST be strings | | 6 | **Array** | `[ value, value, ... ]` | Ordered list; can be heterogeneous | | 7 | *(none)* | — | **No** `undefined`, `date`, `function`, or `regex` types exist in JSON | ## 2.3 The Two Compound Structures ### 2.3.1 JSON Object - Enclosed in `{}`. - An **unordered** set of `name/value` pairs. - Each name is a **string**, and values can be **any** of the 6 types (including another object/array — enabling recursion). - A key is **unique within its object** (duplicates are implementation-defined; some parsers keep the last, others throw). ### 2.3.2 JSON Array - Enclosed in `[]`. - An **ordered** collection of values. - Values can be of **mixed types** (e.g., `[42, "text", true, null, {"x":1}]` is valid). - Indexed from `0` after parsing into a host-language array. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet: JSON vs XML This is the **single most-tested comparison** in KTU 2024 exams. Memorize the table: | Comparison Axis | **JSON** | **XML** | |---|---|---| | Full Form | JavaScript Object Notation | eXtensible Markup Language | | Primary Use | Data interchange (lightweight) | Document markup & data (verbose) | | Verbosity | **Compact** — minimal syntax | **Verbose** — opening + closing tags | | Data Model | **Key/Value** + ordered arrays | Tree of elements + attributes | | Type Support | **Native** (string, number, bool, null) | **All data is text** (manual casting) | | Metadata | No native support | Built-in via attributes | | Comments | **NOT allowed** | Allowed (``) | | Namespaces | **No** | Yes (`xmlns`) | | Schema | JSON Schema (separate) | DTD / XSD (built-in) | | Parsing Cost | **Faster** (native `JSON.parse`) | Slower (DOM/SAX parsers) | | Array Support | **Native** arrays | Requires convention | | Trailing Commas | **Forbidden** | N/A (tag-based) | | Binary Data | Base64 inside string | Base64 or external | | Standardization | ECMA-404, RFC 8259 | W3C Recommendation | | Best For | APIs, mobile, microservices | Documents, RSS, SOAP, config | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In real-world REST API design (covered in Module 4 of this course), JSON has displaced XML primarily because of its **~30–40% lower payload size** and **faster client-side parse time**, both critical for mobile and low-bandwidth IoT contexts. ## 2.5 JSON Operations: Parse & Stringify Every host language (JavaScript, Java, Python, etc.) provides two symmetric operations: $$ \text{JSON String} \xrightarrow{\text{Parse / Deserialize}} \text{Native Object} $$ $$ \text{Native Object} \xrightarrow{\text{Stringify / Serialize}} \text{JSON String} $$ **In JavaScript (the native language of JSON):** ```javascript // Stringify: Object -> JSON string const student = { name: "Anu", cgpa: 8.9, active: true }; const jsonText = JSON.stringify(student); // Result: '{"name":"Anu","cgpa":8.9,"active":true}' // Parse: JSON string -> Object const obj = JSON.parse(jsonText); console.log(obj.cgpa); // 8.9 ``` ## 2.6 JSON Schema (Validation Layer) **JSON Schema** is a declarative vocabulary (IETF Draft 2020-12) that lets you describe and validate the **structure** of a JSON document — its required keys, expected types, value ranges, regex patterns, and references to other schemas. A schema answers four questions about a JSON document: 1. *What* properties exist? (object) 2. *What type* is each property? (type validation) 3. *What range/format* is acceptable? (constraints) 4. *What else* is required? (composition: `allOf`, `anyOf`, `oneOf`) # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Constructing a Nested JSON Object Step-by-Step **Problem (KTU-style):** Represent the following real-world entity as a valid JSON document: > A student named *Rahul* (roll number 21, branch CSE, CGPA 8.7) has taken 3 subjects — *Web* (marks 85), *DBMS* (marks 78), *Maths* (marks 92). He is *active* and has no backlog (`null`). ### Step 1 — Identify the Top-Level Structure A single student entity → **Object** (curly braces). ```json { } ``` ### Step 2 — Add Scalar Key–Value Pairs For each attribute, follow the rule: `"key" : value` with a comma between pairs. ```json { "rollNo": 21, "name": "Rahul", "branch": "CSE", "cgpa": 8.7, "isActive": true } ``` **Logic:** `rollNo` and `cgpa` are numbers (no quotes). `name` and `branch` are strings (double quotes). `isActive` is a boolean. ### Step 3 — Add the Subject List as an Array Subjects are an *ordered* list → **Array** (square brackets). Each subject is itself an object. ```json { "rollNo": 21, "name": "Rahul", "branch": "CSE", "cgpa": 8.7, "isActive": true, "subjects": [ { "name": "Web", "marks": 85 }, { "name": "DBMS", "marks": 78 }, { "name": "Maths", "marks": 92 } ] } ``` **Logic:** The array `subjects` is keyed as a single value. Each element is a JSON object (nested object inside an array — a perfectly valid recursive composition). ### Step 4 — Add the Optional Field with `null` A backlog count that is currently absent is represented by `null` (lowercase). ```json { "rollNo": 21, "name": "Rahul", "branch": "CSE", "cgpa": 8.7, "isActive": true, "backlogCount": null, "subjects": [ { "name": "Web", "marks": 85 }, { "name": "DBMS", "marks": 78 }, { "name": "Maths", "marks": 92 } ] } ``` ### Final Validation Checklist (Six Rules) | Rule | Verification | ✓ | |---|---|---| | All keys in double quotes | `"rollNo"`, `"name"`, … | ✓ | | No trailing commas | Last item in each object/array has no comma | ✓ | | Curly braces for object | `{}` around student & each subject | ✓ | | Square brackets for array | `[]` around `subjects` list | ✓ | | Strings in double quotes | `"Rahul"`, `"CSE"`, etc. | ✓ | | Booleans / null lowercase | `true`, `null` | ✓ | > [!NOTE] > **Result:** The document above is a **syntactically valid JSON** that can be parsed by `JSON.parse()` in JavaScript, `json.loads()` in Python, or `ObjectMapper` in Java without error. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Parse–Process–Stringify Round Trip **Problem:** Given a JSON string received from a server, show how to (a) parse it, (b) access the third subject's marks, (c) update the CGPA, and (d) serialize it back to JSON. ### Input JSON String ```json { "rollNo": 21, "name": "Rahul", "cgpa": 8.7, "subjects": [ { "name": "Web", "marks": 85 }, { "name": "DBMS", "marks": 78 }, { "name": "Maths", "marks": 92 } ] } ``` ### Step (a) — Parse (String → Object) ```javascript const jsonStr = '{ "rollNo": 21, ... }'; const student = JSON.parse(jsonStr); ``` **Logic:** The parser tokenizes the string character-by-character, builds an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** according to the JSON grammar, and instantiates native data structures (objects/arrays/numbers). ### Step (b) — Access Nested Value $$ \text{Value} = \text{student.subjects}[2].\text{marks} $$ ```javascript console.log(student.subjects[2].marks); // 92 ``` **Logic:** Traverse object → array (index `2` = third element) → property `marks`. Result: **92**. ### Step (c) — Mutate the In-Memory Object ```javascript student.cgpa = 9.1; ``` **Logic:** Direct property assignment. The in-memory object is now updated, but the original JSON string is **untouched** (immutable string). ### Step (d) — Stringify (Object → String) ```javascript const updatedJson = JSON.stringify(student); ``` **Logic:** The serializer walks the object graph depth-first, emitting tokens per the JSON grammar. The result is a single-line string (or pretty-printed if the third argument `indent` is provided). ### Pretty-Printed Output ```javascript const pretty = JSON.stringify(student, null, 2); ``` $$ \text{Result} = \begin{cases} \texttt{"{ rollNo: 21, name: \\"Rahul\\", cgpa: 9.1, subjects: [ ... ] }"} & \text{default} \\[6pt] \texttt{Indented, 2-space human-readable text} & \text{with indent=2} \end{cases} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Validate a Document Against a JSON Schema **Schema Definition:** ```json { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "type": "object", "required": ["rollNo", "name", "cgpa"], "properties": { "rollNo": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1 }, "name": { "type": "string", "minLength": 1 }, "cgpa": { "type": "number", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 10 }, "branch": { "type": "string", "enum": ["CSE", "ECE", "ME", "CE"] } }, "additionalProperties": false } ``` ### Validation Decision Table | Document Property | Schema Constraint | Document Value | Valid? | Reason | |---|---|---|---|---| | `rollNo` | integer, ≥ 1 | `21` | ✓ | Integer, ≥ 1 | | `name` | string, len ≥ 1 | `"Rahul"` | ✓ | Non-empty string | | `cgpa` | number, 0–10 | `8.7` | ✓ | Within range | | `branch` | string, enum | `"CSE"` | ✓ | Member of allowed set | | `unknownField` | forbidden | `"x"` | ✗ | `additionalProperties: false` rejects extras | **Logic:** Validation is purely structural — it cannot enforce business rules (e.g., "CGPA must be updated only once per semester"), but it eliminates ~80% of common integration bugs caused by malformed payloads. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use JSON | Engineering Scenario | Recommended Format | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | REST API response payload | **JSON** | Compact, native browser support | | WebSocket message body | **JSON** | Low-latency parsing on mobile | | Configuration file (npm, VSCode) | **JSON / YAML** | JSON is machine-friendly | | Document with rich metadata | XML | Native attribute support | | RSS / Atom feed | XML | Industry standard | | NoSQL document (MongoDB) | **BSON** (binary JSON superset) | Faster indexing | | Cross-language data exchange | **JSON** | Universal parsers | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of a JSON Document The following Mermaid tree diagram maps the internal structure of a typical student record JSON document, illustrating the recursive nature of objects and arrays. ```mermaid graph TD A[Root Document] --> B[Object curly braces] B --> C1["key: rollNo"] B --> C2["key: name"] B --> C3["key: cgpa"] B --> C4["key: isActive"] B --> C5["key: subjects"] B --> C6["key: backlogCount"] C1 --> C1a["value: Number 21"] C2 --> C2a["value: String Rahul"] C3 --> C3a["value: Number 8.7"] C4 --> C4a["value: Boolean true"] C5 --> D[Array square brackets] D --> D1["Index 0 Object"] D --> D2["Index 1 Object"] D --> D3["Index 2 Object"] D1 --> D1a["name: Web"] D1 --> D1b["marks: 85"] D2 --> D2a["name: DBMS"] D2 --> D2b["marks: 78"] D3 --> D3a["name: Maths"] D3 --> D3b["marks: 92"] C6 --> C6a["value: Null"] style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#A0E7E5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFAEBC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C1a fill:#B4F8C8,stroke:#333 style C2a fill:#B4F8C8,stroke:#333 style C3a fill:#B4F8C8,stroke:#333 style C4a fill:#FBE7C6,stroke:#333 style C6a fill:#D5A6BD,stroke:#333 style D1a fill:#B4F8C8,stroke:#333 style D1b fill:#B4F8C8,stroke:#333 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **Root** is a JSON object (cyan node). - Leaves colored **green** are scalar values (string/number). - The **pink node** is an array; its elements are themselves objects. - The **peach-colored leaf** is a boolean. - The **mauve leaf** is the explicit `null` value. --- ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: JSON Data Exchange in a Web Application This diagram shows how JSON flows between the **Browser (Client)** and **Node.js Server** during a typical AJAX call — a scenario directly linked to Module 1 of your syllabus. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant JS as JavaScript Engine participant API as REST API Server participant DB as Database U->>JS: 1 User clicks Get Profile button JS->>JS: 2 Build request payload as JS object JS->>JS: 3 Call JSON.stringify to serialize JS->>API: 4 HTTP POST application/json header API->>API: 5 Server reads request body API->>API: 6 JSON.parse into native object API->>DB: 7 Query SELECT FROM users DB-->>API: 8 Return raw row data API->>API: 9 Build response object API->>API: 10 JSON.stringify to serialize API-->>JS: 11 HTTP 200 application/json JS->>JS: 12 JSON.parse into JS object JS->>U: 13 Render profile in DOM note over JS,API: Content-Type: application/json
Encoding: UTF-8 ``` **Key Insights from the Diagram:** - The JSON string is sent as the **request body** (step 4) and the **response body** (step 11). - The HTTP header `Content-Type: application/json` is **mandatory** for both directions. - The same payload undergoes **two parse** and **two stringify** operations in a single round trip. --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: JSON Schema Validation Pipeline This diagram illustrates how a JSON Schema enforces a contract between a producer (mobile app) and a consumer (backend service). ```mermaid graph LR A[Producer App] --> B[Generate JSON Payload] B --> C{Schema Validator} C --> D[Valid Payload] C --> E[Invalid Payload] D --> F[Network Transmission] E --> G[Return 400 Bad Request] F --> H[Consumer Service] H --> I[Parse to Native Object] I --> J[Business Logic] subgraph Validation Rules R1[Required keys present] R2[Types match schema] R3[Values within range] R4[Format valid email date uri] end C -.uses.-> Validation Rules style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333 style C fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#77DD77,stroke:#333 style E fill:#FF6961,stroke:#333 style G fill:#FF6961,stroke:#333 style J fill:#77DD77,stroke:#333 ``` **Interpretation:** - Validation acts as a **gatekeeper** at the network boundary. - The orange `Schema Validator` block is the focal point — it reduces the need for defensive coding in `J` (business logic). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define JSON. List any four data types supported by JSON.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, text-based, language-independent data interchange format standardized in RFC 8259 and ECMA-404. It represents structured data using attribute–value pairs and ordered arrays, making it the de-facto format for REST APIs and modern web applications. **Four Data Types (1.5 marks):** 1. **String** — `"Hello"` (double-quoted Unicode) 2. **Number** — `42`, `3.14`, `-7`, `2.5e3` 3. **Boolean** — `true` / `false` 4. **Null** — `null` (intentional absence of value) *(Other valid types: Object, Array)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between JSON and XML in terms of verbosity, comments, native array support, and parsing speed.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | JSON | XML | |---|---|---| | **Verbosity** (1m) | Compact — no closing tags | Verbose — opening + closing tags | | **Comments** (1m) | **Not allowed** | Allowed via `` | | **Native Array Support** (0.5m) | **Yes** — first-class `[ ]` | Not native; requires conventions | | **Parsing Speed** (0.5m) | Faster — `JSON.parse()` is highly optimized in JS engines | Slower — DOM/SAX parsers are heavier | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) > *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one** of the two internal-choice questions.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the structure of a JSON document with a suitable example. Discuss any **six** rules that make a JSON document syntactically valid. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare JSON and XML in detail across **eight** different parameters. Why is JSON preferred over XML in modern RESTful web services? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution A(a) — Structure & Six Rules (7 marks)** **Structure of a JSON document (3 marks):** A JSON document has two structural building blocks: - **Object** → an unordered collection of key–value pairs enclosed in `{}` - **Array** → an ordered list of values enclosed in `[]` These two structures can be **nested recursively** to model arbitrarily complex data trees. **Example (2 marks):** ```json { "university": "KTU Kerala", "course": "Modern Web Applications", "credits": 4, "active": true, "tags": ["web", "json", "rest"], "instructor": { "name": "Dr. Asha", "email": "asha@ktu.in" } } ``` **Six Syntax Rules (2 marks):** 1. Data is represented as `name/value` pairs separated by `:`. 2. Pairs within an object are separated by commas (no trailing comma allowed). 3. Objects use curly braces `{}`; arrays use square brackets `[]`. 4. Strings MUST be enclosed in **double quotes** (single quotes are illegal). 5. Booleans are written as lowercase `true` or `false`; null as `null`. 6. JSON has **no support for comments** (`//` or `/* */` are illegal). --- #### **Solution A(b) — JSON vs XML (7 marks)** **Detailed Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | JSON | XML | |---|---|---| | Syntax | Key-value pairs | Custom tags | | Verbosity | Low | High | | Comments | Not allowed | Allowed | | Data Types | Native (string, number, bool, null) | All string (manual casting) | | Arrays | Native support | Not native | | Schema | JSON Schema (external) | DTD / XSD (built-in) | | Parsing | `JSON.parse()` — fast, O(n) | DOM/SAX parsers — slower | | Namespace | No | Yes | | Metadata | Not supported | Attributes supported | | Best Use Case | APIs, mobile, IoT | Documents, SOAP, RSS | **Why JSON is preferred in RESTful services (3 marks):** 1. **Lower payload size** — fewer characters mean reduced bandwidth and faster delivery, critical for mobile clients. 2. **Faster client-side parsing** — browsers have built-in native `JSON.parse()`, requiring no third-party library. 3. **Direct mapping to native data structures** — JavaScript objects can be obtained in a single `parse()` call, removing DOM-walking logic needed for XML. 4. **Language-agnostic** — every modern language (Python, Java, Go, Rust, PHP) has a first-class JSON parser. 5. **Easier human readability** for the simple data structures that APIs typically exchange. 6. **Native array support** eliminates the verbose repeated-tag patterns required in XML. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** What is JSON Schema? Write a JSON Schema to validate the following object: a user with a required `email` (string, valid email format), an `age` (integer between 18 and 100), and an optional `role` (string, default `"student"`). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **parse** and **stringify** operations in JSON with suitable JavaScript examples. What is the role of the `Content-Type: application/json` HTTP header? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution B(a) — JSON Schema (7 marks)** **What is JSON Schema (2 marks):** JSON Schema is a declarative, vocabulary-based specification (currently Draft 2020-12) that describes the structure, content, and constraints of a JSON document. It allows machines to **validate** that a JSON payload conforms to a pre-agreed **contract** — e.g., required keys, expected types, value ranges, and string formats like `email`, `uri`, `date-time`. It is the JSON equivalent of XML's XSD/DTD. **Required Schema (5 marks):** ```json { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "type": "object", "required": ["email", "age"], "properties": { "email": { "type": "string", "format": "email", "minLength": 5 }, "age": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 18, "maximum": 100 }, "role": { "type": "string", "default": "student", "enum": ["student", "faculty", "admin"] } }, "additionalProperties": false } ``` **Explanation of Key Keywords:** | Keyword | Meaning | |---|---| | `$schema` | Declares the schema draft version | | `type` | Validates the JSON data type | | `required` | Lists keys that MUST be present | | `minimum` / `maximum` | Numeric range constraint | | `format` | Pre-defined semantic format (`email`, `date`, `uri`) | | `default` | Value used when key is absent | | `enum` | Restricts to a fixed set of allowed values | | `additionalProperties: false` | Rejects unknown keys | --- #### **Solution B(b) — Parse, Stringify & Content-Type (7 marks)** **Stringify (Serialization) — Object → JSON String (2 marks):** The `stringify` operation converts a native in-memory object (e.g., a JavaScript object literal) into a JSON-formatted text string. It walks the object graph depth-first and emits tokens per the JSON grammar. ```javascript const user = { name: "Rahul", age: 21, active: true }; const jsonString = JSON.stringify(user); // Output: '{"name":"Rahul","age":21,"active":true}' ``` **Parse (Deserialization) — JSON String → Object (2 marks):** The `parse` operation reads a JSON string, validates its syntax, and produces the corresponding native data structure. It throws a `SyntaxError` on invalid input. ```javascript const obj = JSON.parse('{"name":"Rahul","age":21,"active":true}'); console.log(obj.name); // "Rahul" console.log(obj.age); // 21 console.log(obj.active); // true ``` **Pretty-Print with Indentation (1 mark):** ```javascript const pretty = JSON.stringify(user, null, 2); // Output: indented 2-space human-readable form ``` **Role of `Content-Type: application/json` (2 marks):** The `Content-Type` HTTP header tells the **receiver** how to interpret the bytes in the message body. When set to `application/json`: 1. The browser/client knows to invoke its **JSON parser** (not an XML parser, form decoder, or plain text reader). 2. The server-side framework (e.g., Express.js `express.json()` middleware) automatically **parses** the body into a native object and attaches it to `req.body`. 3. Without this header, the body is treated as a raw string and must be manually parsed, leading to bugs. **CURL example:** ```bash curl -X POST https://api.ktu.in/users \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"name":"Rahul","age":21}' ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Single-quoted strings** — `"name":'Rahul'` is invalid; always use **double quotes** for JSON keys and string values. > 2. **Unquoted keys** — `{name:"x"}` is valid JavaScript but **invalid JSON**. > 3. **Trailing commas** — `{ "a":1, "b":2, }` is invalid JSON (unlike JS object literals). > 4. **Confusing JSON with JS object literal syntax** — JSON has no `undefined`, no functions, no `Date` objects, no comments, no `NaN`, no `Infinity`. > 5. **Wrong-case booleans/null** — `True`, `NULL`, `False` are **invalid**; must be lowercase. > 6. **Confusing the term "JSON Object" with "JavaScript Object"** — a "JSON object" refers to the **text** `{}` structure, not a JS runtime object. > 7. **Forgetting `Content-Type: application/json`** in fetch/AJAX calls — causes the server to return the raw string instead of a parsed object. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **JSON** = JavaScript Object Notation — text-based, language-independent, RFC 8259 / ECMA-404. - ✅ It is a **data interchange format**, not a document format like XML/HTML. - ✅ Two structural types: **Object** `{}` and **Array** `[]` — both can be nested recursively. - ✅ Six valid value types: **String, Number, Boolean, Null, Object, Array**. - ✅ All **strings** (keys and values) MUST be in **double quotes**. - ✅ **No comments** are allowed in JSON. - ✅ **No trailing commas** — strict syntax. - ✅ `Content-Type: application/json` header is mandatory in HTTP APIs. - ✅ `JSON.parse(str)` → string-to-object; `JSON.stringify(obj)` → object-to-string. - ✅ JSON is **preferred over XML** in REST APIs due to lower payload, faster parsing, and native data types. - ✅ **JSON Schema** (Draft 2020-12) is the standard way to validate JSON structure. - ✅ File extension: **`.json`**, MIME type: **`application/json`**, default encoding: **UTF-8**. - ✅ JSON supports only **plain text**; binary data must be Base64-encoded inside a string. - ✅ BSON (Binary JSON) extends JSON with extra types and is used by MongoDB.

topics.title: Web Browsers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Web Browsers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Web Browser** (often simply called a *browser*) is a distributed client-server application software that fetches, interprets, renders, and executes resources — typically web documents written in **HTML**, styled with **CSS**, and made interactive via **JavaScript** — retrieved from a web server using the **HTTP/HTTPS** protocol over the Internet. The browser acts as a **Universal Interpreter** between human-readable web content and the underlying operating system, translating declarative markup and scripting into pixels rendered on the screen.

Formally, as per the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** and **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** specifications, a browser is a **User Agent (UA)** that:

- Initiates a network request to a resource identified by a **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)**.
- Parses the response payload.
- Applies a rendering pipeline to produce a visual and interactive representation.
- Persists state (cookies, cache, local storage) for performance and personalization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PECBT633)**
> A web browser is the entry point of every modern web application. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically expects students to understand not just *what* a browser is, but *how* it functions internally — because modern frameworks like React, Angular, and Vue all rely on browser internals (DOM, V8 engine, BOM).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Waiter"

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and order a dish. You do not enter the kitchen, you do not cook, you do not plate. Instead:

- **You (User)** → place an order.
- **The Waiter (Browser)** → takes the order, communicates to the kitchen, brings back the dish, and presents it nicely.
- **The Kitchen (Web Server)** → prepares the dish (HTML/CSS/JS payload).
- **The Recipe Book (HTTP Protocol)** → defines the language of communication.
- **The Plate (Rendered Page)** → what you finally see and interact with.

In this analogy:
- If the recipe is in a language the waiter does not understand → error.
- If the waiter is slow → poor user experience.
- If the waiter caches the dish → faster service next time (**Browser Caching**).

This is exactly how a browser works. It is the **translator, presenter, and executor** of web content.

---

## 1.3 Core Functions of a Modern Browser

A modern browser must perform **seven** core functions as defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Resource Retrieval** — fetch resources using HTTP/HTTPS.
2. **Parsing** — convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into structured data structures (DOM, CSSOM).
3. **Rendering** — convert parsed data into visual pixels.
4. **Script Execution** — execute JavaScript via a JS engine.
5. **User Interaction** — handle input events (clicks, keypresses, touch).
6. **State Persistence** — maintain cookies, localStorage, IndexedDB, and cache.
7. **Security Enforcement** — sandboxing, same-origin policy, SSL/TLS validation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards Compliance**
> Modern browsers conform to the following open standards:
> - **HTML5, CSS3, ECMAScript (ES2024+)** — maintained by **W3C** and **ECMA International**.
> - **HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3** — defined in **RFC 9110, RFC 9114**.
> - **TLS 1.3** — defined in **RFC 8446**.
> Browsers undergo a *standards arms race* — each vendor races to be the first to implement the latest specification.

---

## 1.4 Popular Web Browsers (as of KTU 2024 syllabus cycle)

| Browser         | Vendor                | Engine            | Market Role         |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Google Chrome   | Google LLC           | Blink + V8        | Dominant (~65%)     |
| Mozilla Firefox | Mozilla Foundation   | Gecko + SpiderMonkey | Privacy-focused  |
| Safari          | Apple Inc.           | WebKit + Nitro    | Default on macOS/iOS|
| Microsoft Edge  | Microsoft Corp.      | Blink + V8        | Default on Windows  |
| Opera           | Opera Norway         | Blink + V8        | Niche (built-in VPN)|

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Architecture Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered Architecture of a Web Browser

A modern browser is not a monolithic program. It is a **multi-layered, modular software stack**. As per **"How Browsers Work"** (the canonical KTU reference material derived from the WebKit and Gecko documentation), every browser consists of **eight cooperating layers**:

### Layer 1 — User Interface (UI Shell)
- The "chrome" — address bar, back/forward buttons, bookmarks, tabs.
- Purely visual. Does not interact with rendered content.

### Layer 2 — Browser Engine
- The **conductor**. Marshals actions between the UI and the rendering engine.
- Translates user actions (e.g., URL entry, refresh) into low-level commands.
- Manages tab-to-process mapping.

### Layer 3 — Rendering Engine
- The **painter**. Parses HTML + CSS, builds DOM + CSSOM, performs layout, paint, and compositing.
- Examples: **Blink** (Chrome/Edge), **Gecko** (Firefox), **WebKit** (Safari).

### Layer 4 — Networking Layer
- Handles **HTTP/HTTPS, DNS resolution, TCP/UDP sockets, TLS handshake, HTTP/2 multiplexing, HTTP/3 (QUIC)**.
- Implements caching directives (`Cache-Control`, `ETag`).

### Layer 5 — JavaScript Engine (Interpreter/Compiler)
- Parses, compiles, and executes JavaScript.
- Uses **JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation** for performance.
- Examples: **V8** (Chrome), **SpiderMonkey** (Firefox), **JavaScriptCore** (Safari).

### Layer 6 — UI Backend
- A thin abstraction layer using the host OS's GUI toolkit (e.g., Cocoa on macOS, Win32 on Windows, GTK on Linux).
- Draws basic widgets: combo boxes, scrollbars, window frames.

### Layer 7 — Data Storage / Persistence Layer
- Manages **Cookies**, **LocalStorage**, **SessionStorage**, **IndexedDB**, **Service Workers**, **Cache API**.
- Enforces storage quotas and eviction policies (e.g., LRU).

### Layer 8 — Security Sandbox
- Isolates web content from the host system.
- Implements **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)**, **Content Security Policy (CSP)**, **CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Almost every 14-mark question in Module 1 of PECBT633 asks you to "Explain the architecture of a web browser" or "Explain the role of the rendering engine." Memorize these **eight layers in order** — examiners award step-marks for each correctly identified component.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Architecture Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU 2024 exam preparation on this topic.** It is the equivalent of a formula sheet for a non-mathematical subject.

| # | Layer / Component | Primary Function | Standards / Specs | Example Implementation |
|---|-------------------|------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | User Interface    | Renders browser chrome (tabs, address bar) | HTML, OS GUI toolkits | Chrome's Views system |
| 2 | Browser Engine    | Coordinates UI + Rendering engine actions | Custom IPC protocols | Blink's Content layer |
| 3 | Rendering Engine  | Parses HTML/CSS, builds render tree, paints | HTML5, CSS3, SVG | Blink, Gecko, WebKit |
| 4 | Networking        | DNS, TCP/TLS, HTTP request/response | HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, RFC 9114 | Cronet, libcurl |
| 5 | JS Engine         | Parses, JIT-compiles, executes JavaScript | ECMAScript (ES2024) | V8, SpiderMonkey, JSC |
| 6 | UI Backend        | Abstracts OS-level drawing primitives | OS GUI APIs | Skia, CoreGraphics |
| 7 | Data Storage      | Cookies, localStorage, IndexedDB, Cache | Web Storage API, W3C | LevelDB, SQLite |
| 8 | Security Sandbox  | SOP, CSP, CORS, process isolation | RFC 6454, W3C CSP | Chromium sandbox |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding browser internals is **not academic trivia**. It directly enables:

- **Performance Engineering** — knowing the critical rendering path helps reduce Time-to-Interactive (TTI) and First Contentful Paint (FCP).
- **Frontend Framework Design** — React's Virtual DOM, Vue's reactivity, and Svelte's compilation are all built *around* browser limitations.
- **Web Security Auditing** — penetration testers and security engineers exploit browser sandboxing models to test SOP, CORS, and CSRF protections.
- **DevTools Mastery** — the **Chrome DevTools** are essentially a GUI exposing the browser's internal components. Knowing the architecture unlocks DevTools.
- **Server-Side Optimization** — HTTP/2 server push, cache headers, and content negotiation all depend on browser behavior.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Workflow Analysis: From URL to Pixels

## 3.1 The End-to-End Browser Request Lifecycle

When a user types a URL (e.g., `https://www.ktu.edu.in/index.html`) and presses **Enter**, the browser executes a precise, well-orchestrated sequence of **eight** high-level stages. This is the **most important conceptual walkthrough** for the KTU 2024 exam, and is frequently worth **7 to 14 marks**.

---

### Stage 1 — URL Parsing & Validation

The browser parses the URL into its constituent components:

$$
\text{URL} = \underbrace{\text{https://}}_{\text{Scheme}} \underbrace{\text{www.ktu.edu.in}}_{\text{Host}} \underbrace{:443}_{\text{Default Port}} \underbrace{\text{/index.html}}_{\text{Path}}
$$

- **Scheme** (`https`) → tells browser to use TLS.
- **Host** (`www.ktu.edu.in`) → domain name to resolve.
- **Path** (`/index.html`) → resource identifier on the server.

> **Reasoning:** The browser must first understand the *type* of request (HTTP, FTP, file://, mailto://) before it can dispatch the correct protocol handler.

---

### Stage 2 — DNS Resolution (Domain Name System Lookup)

The browser needs to convert the human-readable hostname into a routable **IP address** (typically IPv4 or IPv6). This is done via DNS.

The resolution order is:

1. **Browser DNS Cache** — does the browser remember this hostname?
2. **Operating System DNS Cache** — does the OS remember it?
3. **Router DNS Cache** — does the ISP router have it?
4. **ISP Recursive Resolver** — typically a recursive DNS server (e.g., 8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1).
5. **Root, TLD, and Authoritative Name Servers** — full hierarchical lookup.

The result is a mapping:

$$
\text{www.ktu.edu.in} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{A or AAAA record} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{IP address (e.g., 103.251.43.50)}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Network hardware (routers, switches) cannot route domain names — only numerical IP addresses. DNS acts as the Internet's *phonebook*.

---

### Stage 3 — TCP Connection Establishment (Three-Way Handshake)

Once the IP is known, the browser initiates a **TCP connection** on port **443** (default for HTTPS). This requires a **three-way handshake**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client} &\xrightarrow{\text{SYN, seq}=x} \text{Server} \\
\text{Server} &\xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK, seq}=y, ack=x+1} \text{Client} \\
\text{Client} &\xrightarrow{\text{ACK, seq}=x+1, ack=y+1} \text{Server}
\end{aligned}
$$

After this exchange, a reliable full-duplex connection is established.

> **Reasoning:** TCP provides *reliable, ordered, error-checked* delivery of bytes. The handshake ensures both sides are ready to communicate.

---

### Stage 4 — TLS Handshake (for HTTPS)

Since the URL uses `https://`, the browser performs a **TLS 1.3 handshake** to encrypt the channel. This involves:

1. **ClientHello** — browser sends supported cipher suites and a random nonce.
2. **ServerHello + Certificate** — server presents its **X.509 SSL certificate** issued by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.
3. **Key Exchange** — using **X25519** or **ECDHE**, both parties derive a shared symmetric session key.
4. **Finished** — both sides confirm the handshake.

$$
\text{Session Key} = \text{HKDF}(\text{Shared Secret}, \text{Transcript Hash})
$$

> **Reasoning:** TLS prevents eavesdropping, tampering, and message forgery. The certificate binds a public key to the domain name, preventing **man-in-the-middle (MITM)** attacks.

---

### Stage 5 — HTTP Request Construction & Dispatch

The browser constructs an **HTTP request message**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Request Line} &: \;\; \text{GET /index.html HTTP/1.1} \\
\text{Headers} &: \;\; \text{Host: www.ktu.edu.in} \\
               &: \;\; \text{User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Modern Web)} \\
               &: \;\; \text{Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml} \\
               &: \;\; \text{Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br} \\
               &: \;\; \text{Cookie: sessionid=abc123} \\
\text{Empty Line} &: \\
\text{Body} &: \;\; \text{(empty for GET)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If using **HTTP/2**, multiple requests are **multiplexed** over a single TCP connection using **HPACK header compression** and **binary framing**.

If using **HTTP/3**, the transport is **QUIC** (UDP-based) for reduced handshake latency.

> **Reasoning:** The request message is the browser's formal "ask" to the server. Headers carry metadata (what I can accept, who I am, what cookies I have).

---

### Stage 6 — HTTP Response Reception

The server responds with a status line, headers, and a body:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Status Line} &: \;\; \text{HTTP/1.1 200 OK} \\
\text{Headers} &: \;\; \text{Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8} \\
               &: \;\; \text{Content-Length: 12345} \\
               &: \;\; \text{Cache-Control: max-age=3600} \\
               &: \;\; \text{Set-Cookie: sessionid=xyz789; HttpOnly} \\
\text{Empty Line} &: \\
\text{Body} &: \;\; \text{<!DOCTYPE html><html>...</html>}
\end{aligned}
$$

The body is the **HTML document** that begins the rendering pipeline.

> **Reasoning:** The status code (200, 404, 500, 301) tells the browser whether the request succeeded. Headers dictate how the body should be interpreted and cached.

---

### Stage 7 — The Rendering Pipeline (Critical Rendering Path)

This is the **heart** of the browser's job. It consists of **six sequential steps**:

#### Step 7.1 — HTML Parsing → DOM Tree Construction

The browser's **HTML parser** tokenizes the HTML and constructs a **Document Object Model (DOM) tree**:

$$
\text{HTML Bytes} \xrightarrow{\text{Tokenizer}} \text{Tokens} \xrightarrow{\text{Tree Builder}} \text{DOM Tree}
$$

#### Step 7.2 — CSS Parsing → CSSOM Tree Construction

The **CSS parser** tokenizes all CSS (inline, embedded, external) into a **CSS Object Model (CSSOM) tree**:

$$
\text{CSS Bytes} \xrightarrow{\text{Parser}} \text{CSSOM Tree}
$$

#### Step 7.3 — Render Tree Construction

The DOM and CSSOM are combined into a **Render Tree**, which contains *only the visible nodes* with their computed styles:

$$
\text{Render Tree} = \text{visible nodes from DOM} \cup \text{computed styles from CSSOM}
$$

#### Step 7.4 — Layout (Reflow)

Each node in the render tree is given **exact pixel coordinates** based on the viewport size, CSS box model, and layout algorithms (block, inline, flex, grid).

$$
\text{Output: } \text{Box Tree with }(x, y, \text{width}, \text{height}) \text{ for each element}
$$

#### Step 7.5 — Paint

The browser issues **paint operations** — converting the layout into a list of **paint records** (e.g., "draw this rectangle, draw this text, draw this border"). These are organized into **layers** for efficiency.

#### Step 7.6 — Compositing

The painted layers are **composited** together on the GPU to produce the final image displayed on screen. Modern browsers use **GPU acceleration** for this final stage.

> **Reasoning:** Each step is a *transformation* of data. The browser progressively converts *bytes* → *tokens* → *tree* → *pixels*. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests your knowledge of these six steps in order.

---

### Stage 8 — JavaScript Execution & Event Loop

After the initial render, the browser's **JavaScript engine** (V8, SpiderMonkey, JSC) executes any scripts. This is a non-blocking, asynchronous process managed by the **event loop**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Event Loop} &: \;\; \text{Execute } \rightarrow \text{Wait for Task} \rightarrow \text{Execute} \rightarrow \cdots \\
\text{Microtask Queue} &: \;\; \text{Promises, MutationObserver (priority)} \\
\text{Task Queue} &: \;\; \text{setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, UI events}
\end{aligned}
$$

JavaScript can mutate the DOM, which **triggers a re-render** of affected subtrees (a *reflow* and *repaint*).

> **Reasoning:** JavaScript is what makes a web page *alive* — handling clicks, fetching data via `fetch()`, animating elements, etc.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix — The Request-Response Cycle

| Stage | Input                | Process                     | Output                      |
|-------|----------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1     | URL string           | Parse & validate             | URL components              |
| 2     | Hostname             | DNS resolution                | IP address                  |
| 3     | IP + Port            | TCP 3-way handshake           | Established TCP socket      |
| 4     | TLS handshake        | Encryption negotiation        | Secure channel + session key|
| 5     | HTTP request         | Construct headers & body      | HTTP request message        |
| 6     | HTTP response        | Receive & parse status, body  | DOM bytes                   |
| 7     | DOM + CSSOM          | Render pipeline               | Pixels on screen            |
| 8     | JavaScript           | Execute, mutate DOM           | Interactive page            |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Browser Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph userLayer["USER INTERFACE LAYER"]
        direction LR
        uiA["Address Bar"]
        uiB["Back / Forward Buttons"]
        uiC["Tab Manager"]
        uiD["Bookmarks Bar"]
    end

    subgraph engineLayer["BROWSER ENGINE"]
        beMain["Central Coordinator - IPC Dispatcher"]
    end

    subgraph coreLayer["CORE PROCESSING LAYER"]
        reMain["Rendering Engine - Blink or Gecko or WebKit"]
        jsMain["JavaScript Engine - V8 or SpiderMonkey or JSC"]
        netMain["Networking Module - HTTP, DNS, TLS, QUIC"]
    end

    subgraph backendLayer["UI BACKEND AND OS BRIDGE"]
        ubMain["OS GUI Abstraction - Skia, CoreGraphics, Win32"]
    end

    subgraph storageLayer["DATA STORAGE LAYER"]
        dsA["Cookies and LocalStorage"]
        dsB["IndexedDB and Cache API"]
        dsC["Service Workers"]
    end

    subgraph securityLayer["SECURITY SANDBOX"]
        ssMain["SOP, CSP, CORS, Process Isolation"]
    end

    userLayer --> beMain
    beMain --> reMain
    beMain --> jsMain
    beMain --> netMain
    reMain --> ubMain
    jsMain --> reMain
    netMain --> beMain
    reMain --> storageLayer
    jsMain --> storageLayer
    securityLayer -. protects .-> coreLayer
    securityLayer -. protects .-> storageLayer
```

---

## 4.2 The Request-Response Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["User Types URL in Address Bar"] --> s2["URL Parsing and Validation"]
    s2 --> s3{"DNS Cache Hit?"}
    s3 -- Yes --> s5["Use Cached IP"]
    s3 -- No --> s4["Recursive DNS Resolution"]
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6["TCP Three Way Handshake"]
    s6 --> s7{"HTTPS?"}
    s7 -- Yes --> s8["TLS 1.3 Handshake"]
    s7 -- No --> s9["Construct HTTP Request"]
    s8 --> s9
    s9 --> s10["Send Request to Server"]
    s10 --> s11["Receive HTTP Response"]
    s11 --> s12{"Status Code Check"}
    s12 -- "2xx Success" --> s13["Parse HTML Bytes"]
    s12 -- "3xx Redirect" --> s2
    s12 -- "4xx or 5xx Error" --> s14["Display Error Page"]
    s13 --> s15["Build DOM Tree"]
    s15 --> s16["Parse CSS into CSSOM"]
    s16 --> s17["Combine into Render Tree"]
    s17 --> s18["Layout and Reflow"]
    s18 --> s19["Paint and Composite"]
    s19 --> s20["Execute JavaScript via Event Loop"]
    s20 --> s21["Page is Fully Interactive"]
```

---

## 4.3 The Critical Rendering Path (CRP) Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    html["HTML Bytes"] --> htmlParser["HTML Tokenizer"]
    htmlParser --> dom["DOM Tree"]
    css["CSS Bytes"] --> cssParser["CSS Tokenizer"]
    cssParser --> cssom["CSSOM Tree"]
    dom --> renderTree["Render Tree Construction"]
    cssom --> renderTree
    renderTree --> layout["Layout - Reflow"]
    layout --> paint["Paint Operations"]
    paint --> layers["GPU Layers"]
    layers --> composite["Compositing on Screen"]
    js["JavaScript Source"] --> jsEngine["JS Engine - V8"]
    jsEngine -->|"Mutates DOM or CSSOM"| dom
    jsEngine -->|"Re-trigger"| renderTree
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a web browser. List any four popular web browsers currently in use.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **web browser** is a software application that retrieves, interprets, and renders web resources from a server using protocols like **HTTP/HTTPS**, displaying them in a human-readable graphical form. It acts as a *User Agent* on behalf of the end user.

Four popular web browsers are:
1. **Google Chrome** (Blink + V8)
2. **Mozilla Firefox** (Gecko + SpiderMonkey)
3. **Apple Safari** (WebKit + Nitro)
4. **Microsoft Edge** (Blink + V8)

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming four browsers, 1 mark for engine accuracy — examiners check for the engine name.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Explain the role of the rendering engine in a web browser.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **rendering engine** is the core component responsible for converting HTML and CSS into visible pixels on the screen. Its key roles are:
1. **Parsing** — tokenizing HTML into a DOM tree and CSS into a CSSOM tree.
2. **Render Tree Construction** — combining DOM and CSSOM into a tree of visible nodes.
3. **Layout** — computing the exact geometric position of each element.
4. **Painting** — converting the layout into actual pixels using the OS's UI backend.
5. **Compositing** — combining painted layers on the GPU for final display.

Example rendering engines: **Blink** (Chrome), **Gecko** (Firefox), **WebKit** (Safari).

*(1 mark for naming the role, 1.5 marks for listing steps, 0.5 marks for example.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice is provided between Q1 and Q2.

---

### Question 1 (Option A) — **[14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Explain the layered architecture of a web browser with a neat block diagram. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A web browser is structured as a **multi-layered software stack** where each layer has a well-defined responsibility. The eight layers, from top to bottom, are:

1. **User Interface (UI)** — The browser's *chrome*: address bar, back/forward buttons, tabs, bookmarks. It is the visible shell and does not interact with the page content.

2. **Browser Engine** — Acts as a *mediator* between the UI and the rendering engine. It queries and manipulates the rendering engine based on user actions (e.g., navigation, refresh).

3. **Rendering Engine** — The *painter* of the browser. It parses HTML and CSS, builds the DOM and CSSOM, performs layout, and produces the visual representation. Examples: **Blink**, **Gecko**, **WebKit**.

4. **Networking Layer** — Manages all network I/O: **HTTP/HTTPS** requests, **DNS** resolution, **TCP** sockets, **TLS** handshakes, and **HTTP/2 multiplexing**.

5. **JavaScript Engine** — Parses, compiles (via JIT), and executes JavaScript code. Modern engines use optimization tiers (e.g., V8's Ignition interpreter + TurboFan compiler).

6. **UI Backend** — A thin abstraction over the host OS's GUI toolkit (e.g., **Cocoa** on macOS, **Win32** on Windows, **GTK** on Linux). Used to draw basic widgets like dropdowns and scrollbars.

7. **Data Storage Layer** — Persists state across sessions via **Cookies**, **LocalStorage**, **SessionStorage**, **IndexedDB**, and the **Cache API**.

8. **Security Sandbox** — Enforces the **Same-Origin Policy**, **Content Security Policy**, and **CORS**, isolating web content from the host system via process isolation.

*(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the visual representation.)*

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark for correct diagram.
- 1 mark per layer × 6 layers = 6 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — Explain the Critical Rendering Path in detail with its six stages. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Critical Rendering Path (CRP)** is the sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into actual pixels on the screen. It consists of **six sequential stages**:

1. **HTML Parsing → DOM Tree** — The HTML tokenizer reads raw bytes, identifies start/end tags, and builds the **Document Object Model (DOM) tree** in memory.

2. **CSS Parsing → CSSOM Tree** — The CSS parser tokenizes all CSS (inline, embedded, external stylesheets) into the **CSS Object Model (CSSOM)** tree, capturing the cascade and inheritance rules.

3. **Render Tree Construction** — DOM and CSSOM are merged. Only *visible* elements (excluding `display: none`, `head`, scripts) are included. Each node carries its *computed style*.

4. **Layout (Reflow)** — For each node in the render tree, the browser calculates its exact geometric position $(x, y)$ and dimensions $(\text{width}, \text{height})$ based on the viewport, CSS box model, and layout mode (block, flex, grid).

5. **Paint** — The browser converts the layout into *paint records* — a sequence of drawing instructions. These are organized into **layers** (each `<div>` with `transform`, `<video>`, `<canvas>` typically becomes its own layer).

6. **Compositing** — Painted layers are sent to the **GPU**, where they are combined into the final on-screen image. Compositing is GPU-accelerated and extremely fast.

**JavaScript's Role:** JavaScript can mutate the DOM or CSSOM at any time, which **invalidates** the relevant portion of the render tree and triggers a *re-layout* and *re-paint* of the affected regions.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark per stage × 6 = 6 marks.
- 1 mark for explaining JavaScript's impact on the CRP.

---

### Question 2 (Option B) — **[14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Explain the end-to-end workflow that occurs when a user types a URL and presses Enter. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

When a user types a URL such as `https://www.ktu.edu.in/index.html` and presses Enter, the browser executes the following **eight stages**:

1. **URL Parsing** — The browser breaks the URL into scheme (`https`), host (`www.ktu.edu.in`), port (`443`), and path (`/index.html`).

2. **DNS Resolution** — The browser resolves the hostname to an IP address by querying the DNS cache hierarchy (browser → OS → router → ISP → authoritative nameserver). For example, `www.ktu.edu.in` may resolve to `103.251.43.50`.

3. **TCP Connection** — The browser opens a **TCP socket** to the resolved IP on port 443 via a **three-way handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK).

4. **TLS Handshake** — Since the scheme is `https`, the browser and server perform a **TLS 1.3 handshake** to negotiate cipher suites, exchange certificates, and derive a symmetric session key.

5. **HTTP Request** — The browser constructs and sends an **HTTP GET request** with headers: `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`, `Accept-Encoding`, and any `Cookie` data.

6. **HTTP Response** — The server replies with a status line (e.g., `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`), response headers, and the HTML body in the response.

7. **Rendering** — The browser initiates the **Critical Rendering Path**: parses HTML to DOM, CSS to CSSOM, builds the render tree, performs layout, paint, and composite operations.

8. **JavaScript Execution** — Any inline or external JavaScript is executed by the **JS engine**, which can mutate the DOM and trigger re-renders. Event listeners are registered for user interactivity.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark for the URL parsing and DNS stages (combined).
- 1 mark for TCP/TLS handshakes.
- 1 mark for HTTP request/response.
- 2 marks for the rendering pipeline.
- 1 mark for JavaScript execution.
- 1 mark for a clear concluding statement.

---

#### Part (b) — Compare different browser engines. Explain the role of JavaScript engines in modern web applications. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison of Browser Engines:**

| Feature              | **Blink**                  | **Gecko**                  | **WebKit**                 |
|----------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Used By              | Chrome, Edge, Opera        | Firefox                    | Safari, iOS browsers       |
| Origin               | Forked from WebKit (2013)  | Standalone (Netscape era)  | Forked from KHTML (2001)   |
| Language             | C++                        | C++                        | C++ / Objective-C          |
| Multiprocess         | Yes (per-site isolation)   | Yes (Electrolysis)         | Yes (WebKit2)              |
| Standards Support    | Aggressive (HTTP/3 early)  | Conservative / privacy-first| Balanced (Safari focus)    |
| Memory Footprint     | High                       | Moderate                   | Low to moderate            |

**Role of JavaScript Engines:**

A **JavaScript engine** parses, compiles, and executes JavaScript code. Modern engines use **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation**, combining an interpreter (fast startup) with an optimizing compiler (fast steady-state performance).

- **V8** (Chrome, Node.js) — Uses *Ignition* (interpreter) + *TurboFan* (optimizing compiler) + *Orinoco* (garbage collector).
- **SpiderMonkey** (Firefox) — Uses *Baseline* + *IonMonkey*.
- **JavaScriptCore / Nitro** (Safari) — Uses a four-tier JIT (LLInt, Baseline, DFG, FTL).

JavaScript engines are critical to modern web applications because they power **SPAs (Single Page Applications)**, **WebAssembly** (compiled languages running in the browser), and **server-side JavaScript** (Node.js, Deno).

**Mark Distribution:**
- 3 marks for the comparison table.
- 2 marks for explaining JIT compilation.
- 1 mark for naming real engines with their pipelines.
- 1 mark for connecting to modern web applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the rendering engine with the JavaScript engine.** Blink is a rendering engine, V8 is a JS engine — they are *separate components* in Chrome.
> 2. **Skipping the TLS handshake** when describing HTTPS. Examiners expect at least a one-line mention of TLS for the 7-mark sub-part.
> 3. **Omitting JavaScript's role** in the rendering pipeline. The CRP is *interactive* — failing to mention that JS can re-trigger layout/paint loses at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the security sandbox layer.** Many students describe only seven layers. The eighth — security — is a *bonus differentiator* that examiners notice.
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams clearly.** When drawing a browser architecture diagram, label every block. An unlabeled diagram gets 0–1 marks only.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize This Before the Exam**

- [ ] A **web browser** is a *User Agent* that fetches, parses, renders, and executes web content.
- [ ] Browsers are built on **8 layers**: UI, Browser Engine, Rendering Engine, Networking, JS Engine, UI Backend, Data Storage, Security Sandbox.
- [ ] The **three major rendering engines** are **Blink** (Chrome/Edge), **Gecko** (Firefox), and **WebKit** (Safari).
- [ ] The **three major JS engines** are **V8** (Chrome), **SpiderMonkey** (Firefox), and **JavaScriptCore** (Safari).
- [ ] The **Critical Rendering Path** has **6 stages**: DOM, CSSOM, Render Tree, Layout, Paint, Composite.
- [ ] The **request lifecycle** has **8 stages**: URL Parse → DNS → TCP → TLS → HTTP Request → HTTP Response → Render → JS Execute.
- [ ] **HTTP/3** uses **QUIC** (UDP-based) for reduced latency compared to **HTTP/2** (TCP-based with multiplexing).
- [ ] **TLS 1.3** is the current standard for HTTPS encryption (RFC 8446).
- [ ] **JavaScript engines** use **JIT compilation** combining an interpreter and an optimizing compiler.
- [ ] The **Security Sandbox** enforces **Same-Origin Policy**, **CSP**, and **CORS** to isolate web content.
- [ ] **Cookies**, **LocalStorage**, **SessionStorage**, **IndexedDB**, and the **Cache API** are the five main browser storage mechanisms.
- [ ] **Browser DevTools** (e.g., Chrome DevTools) expose these internal components — the *Network* tab shows stages 1–6, the *Performance* tab shows stage 7, the *Console* shows stage 8.
- [ ] **Process isolation** (one tab = one process) is a Chrome innovation that limits the blast radius of a malicious page.
- [ ] KTU 2024 frequently asks for *neat diagrams* — always draw a labeled architecture diagram for 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Servers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Web Servers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Server (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Web Server** is a computer system (hardware) or a software application (program) that stores, processes, and delivers web pages and web-based content to clients over the Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) or its secure variant (HTTPS). It accepts incoming network requests from web clients (browsers) and responds with the requested resources such as HTML documents, CSS files, JavaScript, images, and multimedia content.

A web server has two distinct but related meanings:
1. **Hardware Web Server**: The physical computer that hosts the website's files and runs the server software.
2. **Software Web Server**: The application program (e.g., Apache HTTP Server, Nginx, Microsoft IIS) that handles HTTP requests and serves web content.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, students are expected to differentiate between **Static Web Servers** (serve pre-built files as-is) and **Dynamic Web Servers** (execute server-side scripts like PHP, Python, Node.js before sending the response).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Restaurant Analogy — The "Waiter-Waitress Model":**

Imagine a busy restaurant:
- **You (the Client/Browser)** are sitting at a table with a menu.
- The **Waiter (Web Server)** takes your order (HTTP Request) and brings food back (HTTP Response).
- The **Kitchen (Database/Backend)** prepares the food.
- The **Menu (URL/Resource)** lists what is available.

The waiter doesn't cook the food (in static serving) — they simply fetch it from a pre-prepared tray. In **dynamic serving**, the waiter passes the order to the chef, who cooks it fresh and hands it back to be served.

### 1.3 Core Functions of a Web Server

1. **HTTP Request Handling** — Parsing incoming GET, POST, PUT, DELETE requests.
2. **Resource Mapping** — Mapping URL paths to physical file system locations.
3. **Content Delivery** — Transmitting HTML, CSS, JS, images, and other static assets.
4. **Security Enforcement** — Implementing HTTPS/SSL, authentication, and access control.
5. **Logging & Monitoring** — Recording access logs, error logs, and traffic analytics.
6. **Concurrency Management** — Handling multiple simultaneous client requests efficiently.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Web Server Performance

> [!NOTE]
> Key performance metrics that KTU expects students to know:
> - **Requests per Second (RPS)**: Number of HTTP requests handled per second.
> - **Throughput**: Data transferred per unit time, typically measured in **Mbps** or **GB/s**.
> - **Latency**: Time taken to process a single request-response cycle, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Concurrent Connections**: Maximum number of simultaneous client connections supported.

### 1.5 Classification of Web Servers

| Category | Examples | Use Case |
|----------|----------|----------|
| **Open-Source HTTP Servers** | Apache HTTP Server, Nginx, Lighttpd | Linux-based hosting, high traffic |
| **Commercial Web Servers** | Microsoft IIS, IBM HTTP Server | Enterprise Windows environments |
| **Application Servers** | Apache Tomcat, JBoss WildFly, WebLogic | Java-based dynamic web apps |
| **Embedded Web Servers** | Tornado, Jetty, Gunicorn | Microservices, IoT applications |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The HTTP Request-Response Cycle (Step-by-Step)

The fundamental operational model of every web server is the **Request-Response paradigm**. Let us break it down:

1. **DNS Resolution** — The browser converts the domain name (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into an IP address using the Domain Name System.
