contract as a vector: $$ Q = (a, t, r, s) $$ where the four components carry engineering meaning: $$ \begin{aligned} a &= \text{availability (e.g., 99.9\%)} \\ t &= \text{latency / response time} \\ r &= \text{reliability (MTBF)} \\ s &= \text{security level (TLS, OAuth, etc.)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.2 Service Composition (Orchestration) When multiple services are composed in sequence, the composite service $S_{comp}$ is the function composition: $$ S_{comp} = S_3 \circ S_2 \circ S_1 $$ That is, for an input $x$: $$ S_{comp}(x) = S_3(S_2(S_1(x))) $$ **Step 3 — Derive the Output Step-by-Step:** Let us walk through the explicit intermediate state transitions: $$ \begin{aligned} y_1 &= S_1(x) \quad &\text{(Step 1: Input processed by Service 1)} \\ y_2 &= S_2(y_1) \quad &\text{(Step 2: Output of S1 fed as input to S2)} \\ y_3 &= S_3(y_2) \quad &\text{(Step 3: Output of S2 fed as input to S3)} \\ \therefore S_{comp}(x) &= y_3 \quad &\text{(Final composite output)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Total Latency Calculation:** If $t_i$ is the latency of $S_i$, then the composite latency in **sequential composition** is: $$ T_{comp} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} t_{net,i} $$ where the additional term is defined as: $$ t_{net,i} = \text{network overhead between } S_i \text{ and } S_{i+1} $$ **Step 5 — Parallel Composition:** When two independent services $S_a$ and $S_b$ execute in parallel, the composite latency becomes the **maximum** (not sum): $$ T_{parallel} = \max(t_a, t_b, t_{net}) $$ This is a critical KTU reasoning point: examiners frequently test the difference between sequential and parallel composition latency. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Principles to Architecture | SOA Principle | Architectural Manifestation | Examiner's Verification Question | |---|---|---| | Loose Coupling | XML/SOAP, async messaging | "Why is WSDL essential for loose coupling?" | | Reusability | Shared enterprise service bus | "How does ESB promote reuse?" | | Composability | BPEL orchestration engine | "Differentiate Orchestration vs Choreography" | | Statelessness | Database-backed state store | "Why can't SOA services hold session state?" | | Discoverability | UDDI registry, API gateway | "Role of Service Registry in SOA" | | Standardized Contract | WSDL/XSD schema validation | "Explain WSDL structure" | ## 3.3 Service-Oriented Architecture vs. Microservices This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. Here is the precise comparison: | Parameter | SOA (Traditional) | Microservices | |---|---|---| | **Granularity** | Coarse-grained (entire business process) | Fine-grained (single responsibility) | | **Communication** | SOAP over HTTP, ESB, often synchronous | REST/gRPC over HTTP, often async messaging | | **Data Storage** | Shared enterprise database | Database-per-service (Bounded Context) | | **Deployment** | Monolithic application with service layers | Independently deployable containers | | **Governance** | Centralized (Enterprise-wide standards) | Decentralized (Team autonomy) | | **Coupling** | Loosely coupled | *Even more* loosely coupled | | **Use Case** | Enterprise integration, B2B | Cloud-native, large-scale web apps | | **Example** | SAP NetWeaver, Oracle SOA Suite | Netflix OSS, Amazon Prime Video | > [!NOTE] > Microservices is a **specialized evolution** of SOA. In 14-mark KTU answers, frame microservices as a *cloud-native refinement* of SOA principles, **not a replacement**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SOA Publish-Find-Bind Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Service Provider] -->|1 Publish WSDL| B[Service Registry UDDI] C[Service Consumer] -->|2 Find Service| B B -->|3 Return Contract| C C -->|4 Bind and Invoke SOAP over HTTP| A A -->|5 Response XML Payload| C ``` ## 4.2 SOA Layered Reference Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Layer 1: Business Process Layer
Orchestration and Choreography] L2[Layer 2: Service Composition Layer
Composite Service Design] L3[Layer 3: Service Inventory Layer
Individual Web Services] L4[Layer 4: Service Infrastructure Layer
ESB, Messaging, Security] L5[Layer 5: Operational Systems Layer
Legacy Apps, Databases, ERPs] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` ## 4.3 Service Roles Interaction Model ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Roles["SOA Triad of Roles"] R1[Provider] R2[Registry UDDI] R3[Consumer] end R1 -->|publishes| R2 R3 -->|queries| R2 R2 -->|returns service metadata| R3 R1 -->|executes service via contract| R3 ``` ## 4.4 Service Composition — Sequential and Parallel ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SEQ["Sequential Composition"] X1[Input] --> S1[Service 1] --> S2[Service 2] --> S3[Service 3] --> Y1[Output] end subgraph PAR["Parallel Composition"] X2[Input] --> SP1[Service A] X2 --> SP2[Service B] SP1 --> MERGE[Merge Result] SP2 --> MERGE MERGE --> Y2[Output] end ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define Service Oriented Architecture. List any four principles of SOA.** **Model Answer:** Service Oriented Architecture (SOA) is an architectural paradigm in which application functionalities are packaged as interoperable, loosely coupled, and reusable services that communicate over a network using standardized protocols and data formats. The four principles of SOA are: 1. **Standardized Service Contract** — Services follow a formal, machine-readable contract (e.g., WSDL). 2. **Loose Coupling** — Services have minimal inter-dependency on each other. 3. **Service Reusability** — Services are designed to be reused across multiple applications. 4. **Service Discoverability** — Services can be discovered through a registry (e.g., UDDI). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Differentiate between Service Orchestration and Service Choreography.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Service Orchestration | Service Choreography | |---|---|---| | **Control** | Centralized (one orchestrator controls flow) | Decentralized (no central controller) | | **Coordination** | A central BPEL engine coordinates services | Services interact peer-to-peer via shared events | | **Visibility** | Orchestrator has full visibility of process | Each service has only local visibility | | **Protocol** | WS-BPEL | WS-CDL (Choreography Description Language) | | **Use Case** | Complex business workflows with defined order | Cross-organization collaborations | | **Example** | SAP NetWeaver BPM | Multi-party supply chain events | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** > (a) Explain the fundamental architecture of Service Oriented Architecture with a neat diagram. Describe the Publish-Find-Bind pattern. *(7 marks)* > > (b) Discuss in detail the **8 guiding principles of SOA** with suitable real-world examples. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks: **Step 1: Introduction to SOA Architecture (2 marks)** Service Oriented Architecture is a distributed software design paradigm that organizes an enterprise's software functionality as a collection of **interoperable services**. These services communicate via standardized messages, regardless of the underlying platform, language, or operating system. **Step 2: Three Primary Roles in SOA (2 marks)** 1. **Service Provider** — Hosts and implements the service; publishes its description in the registry. 2. **Service Registry (Broker)** — Maintains a searchable directory of available services (e.g., UDDI). 3. **Service Consumer / Requester** — Locates the required service via the registry, binds to it, and invokes its operations. **Step 3: The Publish-Find-Bind Pattern (3 marks)** - **Publish**: The Service Provider uploads a **WSDL (Web Services Description Language)** document into the **UDDI Registry**. This document formally describes the service's interface, operations, input/output message types, transport protocol, and endpoint URL. - **Find**: The Service Consumer queries the registry with specific criteria (e.g., service name, category, or functional keyword). The registry returns a list of matching WSDL contracts. - **Bind**: The Consumer uses the WSDL to generate client-side proxy/stub code. At runtime, the consumer constructs a **SOAP envelope** (an XML message), sends it over **HTTP/HTTPS** to the service endpoint, and awaits the response. *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for visual representation.)* #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks: The **8 Guiding Principles of SOA** with real-world examples: 1. **Standardized Service Contract** *(1 mark)* — Example: A bank's `AccountBalanceService` is described in WSDL, ensuring all client applications consume it identically regardless of language (Java, .NET, Python). 2. **Loose Coupling** *(1 mark)* — Example: An e-commerce site's `PaymentService` (Stripe/PayPal) can be swapped with another provider without modifying the checkout code. 3. **Service Abstraction** *(1 mark)* — Example: Consumers of a `WeatherService` see only `getTemperature(city)`, not the internal meteorological models or data pipelines. 4. **Service Reusability** *(1 mark)* — Example: A single `LoginService` is reused by 50+ internal enterprise applications for SSO. 5. **Service Autonomy** *(1 mark)* — Example: A `FraudDetectionService` runs on a dedicated server cluster with its own database, fully controlling its execution environment. 6. **Service Composability** *(1 mark)* — Example: A `TravelBookingService` composes `FlightService + HotelService + PaymentService` into a single composite workflow. 7. **Service Statelessness** *(1 mark)* — Example: An `InventoryCheckService` does not store session data; each query is independent, enabling horizontal scaling. 8. **Service Discoverability** *(1 mark)* — Example: Amazon API Gateway registry allows developers to discover available APIs via metadata search. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > (a) With a neat architecture diagram, explain the **layered reference model of SOA**. Discuss the role of **ESB (Enterprise Service Bus)**. *(7 marks)* > > (b) Compare and contrast **SOA and Microservices Architecture**. Discuss how microservices are an evolution of SOA. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks: **Step 1: SOA Layered Reference Model (4 marks)** The layered model (as standardized by OASIS) decomposes SOA into five logical layers: 1. **Operational Systems Layer** (Bottom) — Contains existing legacy systems, ERPs (SAP, Oracle), databases, and mainframes that perform core business functions. 2. **Service Infrastructure Layer** — Provides the communication backbone: **Enterprise Service Bus (ESB)**, message brokers (RabbitMQ, Kafka), security gateways, and protocol converters. 3. **Service Inventory Layer** — The actual catalogue of deployed services (web services, REST APIs, SOAP endpoints) that expose business capabilities. 4. **Service Composition Layer** — Defines composite services that aggregate and coordinate multiple atomic services to fulfill complex business processes. 5. **Business Process Layer** (Top) — Implements end-to-end business workflows via orchestration engines (e.g., BPEL) and BPMN tools. **Step 2: Role of ESB — Enterprise Service Bus (3 marks)** ESB is the **central nervous system** of SOA infrastructure. It performs the following functions: - **Protocol Translation** — Converts between SOAP, REST, JMS, FTP, etc. - **Message Routing** — Directs messages to the correct endpoint based on content or rules. - **Data Transformation** — Converts XML↔JSON, schema mappings. - **Security Enforcement** — Authentication, authorization, encryption (WS-Security). - **Monitoring & Logging** — Tracks service SLAs, error rates, latency. Examples: Mule ESB, IBM WebSphere ESB, Oracle Service Bus. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks: **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Feature | SOA | Microservices | |---|---|---| | Service Scope | Coarse-grained (large business process) | Fine-grained (single capability) | | Coupling | Loose | *Even looser* — independent deployment | | Communication | SOAP/ESB, synchronous | REST/gRPC, async event-driven | | Data Storage | Shared DB across services | Database-per-service | | Governance | Centralized standards | Decentralized, team-level autonomy | | Deployment | Monolithic | Containers (Docker/Kubernetes) | | Failure Handling | Resilient at ESB level | Circuit breakers (Hystrix/Resilience4j) | | Tech Stack | Homogeneous (Java EE, .NET) | Polyglot (Java, Go, Python, Node) | | Example | SAP NetWeaver SOA | Netflix, Uber, Amazon | **Microservices as an Evolution of SOA (2 marks):** Microservices is a **specialized, cloud-native refinement** of SOA. It retains SOA's core principles (loose coupling, reusability, discoverability, abstraction) but discards its heavyweight, centralized artifacts (ESB, UDDI, WSDL) in favour of: - Lightweight REST/gRPC contracts (OpenAPI, Protobuf) - Container-based deployment (Docker, Kubernetes) - Decentralized service discovery (Consul, Eureka, etcd) - Asynchronous, event-driven communication (Kafka, RabbitMQ) Hence, microservices can be philosophically seen as **"SOA done right in the cloud era"** — a more pragmatic, scalable, and DevOps-friendly incarnation of the original SOA vision by Roy Schulte and W3C. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing SOA with Web Services** — SOA is an *architecture*; Web Services (SOAP/REST) are an *implementation* of SOA. Examiners deduct 2 marks if these are used interchangeably. > > 2. **Skipping Real-World Examples** — In 14-mark questions, **at least one real-world example per principle** is mandatory. Generic textbook paraphrasing without application fetches partial marks only. > > 3. **Forgetting the Diagram** — KTU mandates a *neat labelled diagram* in architecture questions. A missing diagram costs 1–2 marks. > > 4. **Ignoring KTU Terminology** — Use exact KTU/KTU-equivalent syllabus terms: *Publish-Find-Bind*, *Service Contract*, *WSDL*, *UDDI*, *BPEL*, *ESB*. Avoid casual synonyms. > > 5. **Not Differentiating Orchestration vs Choreography** — This is the **#1 favourite KTU trap question**. Master the comparison table. > > 6. **Treating Microservices as a Replacement of SOA** — Frame it as an *evolution*, not a replacement. Examiners will mark down a hostile comparison. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Service Oriented Architectures** - **Definition**: SOA is a distributed architecture where functionalities are exposed as interoperable, loosely coupled, reusable services. - **8 Principles**: Standardized Contract, Loose Coupling, Abstraction, Reusability, Autonomy, Composability, Statelessness, Discoverability. - **4 Roles**: Service Provider, Service Consumer, Service Registry, Service Contract. - **Pattern**: **Publish-Find-Bind** (PFB) — the canonical SOA interaction lifecycle. - **Standards Stack**: **WSDL** (description), **SOAP** (messaging), **UDDI** (registry), **XML** (data), **HTTP/HTTPS** (transport). - **Composability Math**: $S_{comp} = S_3 \circ S_2 \circ S_1$; Latency = sum (sequential) or max (parallel). - **ESB**: Centralized integration backbone for routing, transformation, security. - **Orchestration vs Choreography**: Centralized (BPEL) vs Decentralized (peer-to-peer, WS-CDL). - **SOA vs Microservices**: SOA is coarse-grained + ESB + SOAP; Microservices is fine-grained + containers + REST/gRPC. - **Layered Model**: Operational Systems → Service Infrastructure (ESB) → Service Inventory → Service Composition → Business Process. - **Service Tuple**: $S = \langle I, O, Q, C, P \rangle$ — Inputs, Outputs, QoS, Capability, Policy. - **Quality Vector**: $Q = (a, t, r, s)$ — availability, latency, reliability, security. - **Real-World Examples to Quote**: Banking (NEFT/SWIFT), Amazon, SAP, Netflix, Kerala e-Governance. ---

topics.title: REST Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** REST Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**REST (Representational State Transfer)** is a distributed architectural style for hypermedia systems, originally introduced by **Roy Thomas Fielding** in his seminal doctoral dissertation (University of California, Irvine, **2000**). REST is not a protocol, standard, or technology — it is a set of **architectural constraints** that, when applied as a whole, emphasize the **scalability, generality, simplicity, and performance** of distributed network-based applications.

In the context of **Modern Web Applications (PECBT633)**, a **REST Service** (commonly called a **RESTful Web Service**) is a lightweight, stateless web service that strictly adheres to the REST constraints and uses **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** as its underlying transport protocol. Communication is performed via standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) operating on **resources** identified by **URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** REST is fundamentally an **architectural style**, not a specification like SOAP/WSDL. REST does not mandate a particular message format — **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)**, XML, YAML, or even plain text can be used as the representation format, with JSON being the de-facto industry standard for modern web APIs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin Citation:** The term "Representational State Transfer" was coined by Roy Fielding in Chapter 5 of his 2000 PhD dissertation, "Architectural Styles and the Design of Network-Based Software Architectures." This is a *board-favorite* reference question in KTU examinations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **Library Management System** where you are the **Client** (a web browser or mobile app) and the library is the **Server** (a remote data center hosting books, members, and transactions).

| REST Analogy | Library Equivalent | Technical Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Library Catalog | Collection of resources | `/api/books` |
| Unique Book ID (ISBN) | Unique Resource Identifier (URI) | `/api/books/123` |
| Borrowing a Book | Reading resource state | `HTTP GET` request |
| Donating a New Book | Creating a new resource | `HTTP POST` request |
| Updating Book Cover | Modifying resource state | `HTTP PUT / PATCH` request |
| Discarding a Book | Removing a resource | `HTTP DELETE` request |
| Borrowing Card / Session | Stateless — no memory of past | Each request is independent |

> **Plain English Intuition:** Think of a **waiter in a restaurant**. You (client) give the waiter (HTTP) a structured order (request: GET / POST / PUT / DELETE) using a menu card (URI). The kitchen (server) processes the order and returns the dish (response with JSON/XML representation + status code). The waiter does not remember your previous orders — every interaction is **self-contained (stateless)**. This is REST in action!

---

### 1.3 Why REST Matters in Modern Engineering

- **Industry Standard:** Used by **Google APIs, Facebook Graph API, Twitter API, Amazon S3, GitHub REST API**, and **Stripe**.
- **Statelessness** allows horizontal scaling across millions of nodes (e.g., cloud-native microservices).
- **Cache-friendly** due to HTTP semantics, drastically reducing bandwidth consumption.
- **Language-agnostic** — a Java client can consume a Python or Node.js REST API seamlessly.
- **Lightweight payload** via JSON reduces payload size by approximately **40–60%** compared to equivalent SOAP/XML messages.
- The de-facto backbone of **Single Page Applications (SPAs)**, **IoT (Internet of Things)**, and **Mobile-Backend-as-a-Service (MBaaS)** architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Memorize:** A typical REST request/response round-trip over HTTPS averages **100–300 ms** under standard broadband conditions, with a JSON payload size ranging from **1 KB (simple lookup)** to **5 MB (bulk data export)** depending on resource complexity.

---

### 1.4 The Six Guiding Constraints of REST (KTU High-Yield)

Fielding defined **6 architectural constraints** — all must be satisfied for a system to be considered truly RESTful:

1. **Client–Server Separation**
2. **Statelessness**
3. **Cacheability**
4. **Uniform Interface**
5. **Layered System**
6. **Code-On-Demand (Optional)**

*(Detailed treatment provided in Section 2.)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six REST Architectural Constraints (Detailed Breakdown)

#### **C1. Client–Server Constraint**
The principle of **separation of concerns**. The client is concerned with the **User Interface (UI) and user experience**, while the server handles **data persistence, business logic, and security**. This separation enables independent evolution — the mobile app can be redesigned without affecting the database schema, and vice versa.

> **Why it matters:** Enables parallel development teams, platform independence (web/mobile/desktop all consume the same API), and improved portability across operating systems.

---

#### **C2. Statelessness (The Most-Cited Constraint)**
Each request from client to server **MUST contain all information necessary to understand and complete the request**. The server **MUST NOT** store any client context between requests. All session state is held **exclusively on the client side**.

- **Pro:** Improved scalability, simplified server design, enhanced reliability.
- **Con:** Slight increase in request payload size (repeated auth tokens, metadata).

> **Example:** A JWT (JSON Web Token) is sent in **every** request header — the server does not maintain a session table.

---

#### **C3. Cacheability**
Responses **MUST** define themselves as **cacheable** or **non-cacheable** to prevent clients from reusing stale or inappropriate data. Server responses include explicit `Cache-Control`, `Expires`, and `ETag` HTTP headers.

- **Cacheable responses** improve efficiency, scalability, and user-perceived performance.
- Reduces average server load by approximately **30–70%** in read-heavy APIs.

---

#### **C4. Uniform Interface (The Most-Restrictive Constraint)**
This is the defining constraint of REST and is decomposed into **four sub-constraints**:

| Sub-Constraint | Definition | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Identification of Resources** | Each resource has a unique, stable URI | `/api/students/S101` |
| **Manipulation through Representations** | Client interacts with *representations* (JSON/XML), not the resource itself | `{"name": "Anu", "cgpa": 9.1}` |
| **Self-Descriptive Messages** | Each message contains enough info to describe how to process it | `Content-Type: application/json` |
| **HATEOAS** | Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State | Links in response guide navigation |

---

#### **C5. Layered System**
A client cannot ordinarily tell whether it is connected directly to the end server or to an **intermediary** (load balancer, proxy, CDN, API gateway). This enables:

- **Load Balancing** across multiple servers.
- **Shared Caches** at proxy layers.
- **Security Enforcement** at firewall/gateway layers.
- **Legacy Encapsulation** — old services wrapped as new.

---

#### **C6. Code-On-Demand (OPTIONAL)**
Servers can temporarily extend or customize client functionality by transferring **executable code** (e.g., JavaScript applets, WASM modules). This is the **only optional** constraint and is rarely used in modern REST APIs (mostly replaced by SPAs and client-side frameworks).

---

### 2.2 The Core REST Building Blocks

#### **A. Resources**
A **resource** is any piece of identifiable information — a document, image, customer record, or a collection of records. Resources are **abstract concepts**, not the actual data stored on the server. The *representation* is the concrete snapshot (JSON/XML) sent over the wire.

#### **B. URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)**
A URI uniquely identifies a resource. URIs are designed to be **descriptive, hierarchical, and stable**:
```
https://api.ktuuniversity.edu/v2/students/S101/courses/CS301/grades
```

- **URN (Uniform Resource Name):** Names a resource (`urn:isbn:9780132350884`)
- **URL (Uniform Resource Locator):** Names + locates a resource (`https://ktu.edu.in/books/cs301`)

#### **C. Representations**
A representation is a **byte sequence** plus **representation metadata** (media type, last-modified date). Common formats:

| Format | Media Type | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| JSON | `application/json` | Modern web & mobile APIs (default) |
| XML | `application/xml` | Legacy enterprise (SOAP bridges) |
| YAML | `application/yaml` | Configuration & DevOps |
| Plain Text | `text/plain` | Logs, simple flags |
| HTML | `text/html` | Web pages |
| Binary | `application/octet-stream` | File downloads |

#### **D. Standard HTTP Methods (Verbs)**

| HTTP Method | CRUD Operation | Idempotent? | Safe? | Body Allowed? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `GET` | Read | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (should be empty) |
| `POST` | Create | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| `PUT` | Update / Replace | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| `PATCH` | Partial Update | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| `DELETE` | Delete | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⚠️ Optional |
| `HEAD` | Headers only | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `OPTIONS` | Discover methods | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Idempotency** is a *guaranteed-same-result* property. A `PUT /students/S101` request executed once or 100 times produces the **same end state** (student S101 exists with given data). A `POST /students` creates a *new* student each time — **non-idempotent**.

#### **E. HTTP Status Codes (The Response Vocabulary)**

| Class | Range | Meaning | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1xx | **100–199** | Informational | `100 Continue` |
| 2xx | **200–299** | Success | `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content` |
| 3xx | **300–399** | Redirection | `301 Moved Permanently`, `304 Not Modified` |
| 4xx | **400–499** | Client Error | `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found`, `409 Conflict` |
| 5xx | **500–599** | Server Error | `500 Internal Server Error`, `503 Service Unavailable` |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Idempotent Calls | $N_{attempts} \to 1$ resulting state | Independent of retry count |
| Richardson Maturity Score | $RMS = \sum_{i=1}^{4} L_i$ where $L_i \in \{0,1\}$ | $RMS \in [0, 4]$ (max 4 = REST nirvana) |
| Cache Hit Ratio | $CHR = \frac{N_{cache\_hits}}{N_{total\_requests}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as a percentage (%) |
| Effective Latency | $T_{eff} = (1 - CHR) \cdot T_{origin} + CHR \cdot T_{cache}$ | Seconds (s) |
| Payload Overhead | $O_{rest} = \frac{S_{headers} + S_{body}}{S_{body}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage overhead |
| Stateless Token Cost | $C_{token} = S_{JWT} \times N_{req}$ | Bytes per session |
| Throughput | $\lambda = \frac{N_{req}}{T_{window}}$ | Requests per second (RPS) |
| HTTP Code Success Boundary | $200 \le S \le 299$ for success | $400 \le S \le 499$ client error |
| Time Complexity (URI lookup) | $O(1)$ (hash-table based) | Constant time on average |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-Commerce:** Amazon's product catalog REST API serves **billions of requests per day** with sub-100ms latency.
- **Banking:** UPI (Unified Payments Interface) of India uses RESTful JSON APIs for inter-bank transactions processing **> 10 billion transactions/month**.
- **Cloud Native:** Kubernetes API server exposes **100% REST API** for cluster management.
- **Social Media:** Twitter API v2, LinkedIn API, Instagram Graph API — all REST.
- **IoT & Smart Devices:** REST over HTTPS is used for telemetry ingestion, firmware updates, and remote control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Richardson Maturity Model (RMM) — Derived Levels of REST Compliance

The **Richardson Maturity Model (RMM)** was proposed by **Leonard Richardson** to grade APIs on a scale of **0 to 3** based on their adherence to REST principles. **Martin Fowler** later popularized it.

Let us define the level function:

$$
L_{total} = L_0 + L_1 + L_2 + L_3
$$

where each $L_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and the levels are **cumulative** (achieving $L_2$ requires having $L_1$, and so on).

---

#### **Level 0 — The Swamp of POX (Plain Old XML)**

At this level, the API uses **HTTP purely as a transport mechanism** — a tunnel for sending arbitrary data. There is **one URI**, **one HTTP method (POST)**, and the actual operation is encoded inside the request body (typically XML or JSON).

$$
URL_{L0} = \text{single endpoint} \quad ; \quad Method_{L0} = \{POST\} \text{ only}
$$

**Example (RPC-style SOAP):**
```http
POST /api HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: text/xml

<soap:Envelope>
  <soap:Body>
    <getStudent>
      <id>S101</id>
    </getStudent>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>
```

> **Conversion logic:** Every operation — read, write, delete — uses the *same* URI and *same* method, with the verb buried in the payload. This violates the **Uniform Interface** constraint.

---

#### **Level 1 — Resources**

The system introduces **multiple URIs** — one per resource. Different resources are exposed at different addresses, but HTTP methods are still not properly utilized.

$$
URL_{L1} = \{\text{/students}, \text{/students/S101}, \text{/courses}\} \quad ; \quad Method_{L1} \approx \{POST\}
$$

**Example:**
```http
POST /students/getStudent HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json
```
or
```http
POST /students/S101 HTTP/1.1
```

> **Conversion logic:** We now have *resource addressing* but the verbs (`get`, `delete`, `update`) are still embedded in URIs. The system is now addressable, not yet RESTful.

---

#### **Level 2 — HTTP Verbs**

This level introduces **proper HTTP methods** (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and **HTTP status codes**. Now the system truly begins to leverage the HTTP protocol.

$$
URL_{L2} = \text{resource URIs} \quad ; \quad Method_{L2} = \{GET, POST, PUT, DELETE\}
$$

**Example CRUD matrix:**

| Operation | URI | HTTP Method | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| List all students | `/api/students` | `GET` | `200 OK` |
| Get one student | `/api/students/S101` | `GET` | `200 OK` / `404 Not Found` |
| Create student | `/api/students` | `POST` | `201 Created` |
| Replace student | `/api/students/S101` | `PUT` | `200 OK` |
| Delete student | `/api/students/S101` | `DELETE` | `204 No Content` |

> **Conversion logic:** At Level 2, **safety** (GET is safe) and **idempotency** (PUT, DELETE are idempotent) semantics of HTTP are respected. The system is now considered **RESTful by most practitioners**, even if HATEOAS is missing.

---

#### **Level 3 — Hypermedia Controls (HATEOAS)**

The highest level of REST maturity. The API response **includes hypermedia links** that guide the client through possible subsequent actions. The client needs **no out-of-band knowledge** of the URI structure.

$$
\text{Response}_{L3} = \text{Data} \cup \text{Hypermedia Links}
$$

**Example HATEOAS response:**
```json
{
  "studentId": "S101",
  "name": "Anu Krishnan",
  "cgpa": 9.10,
  "links": [
    {"rel": "self",      "href": "/api/students/S101"},
    {"rel": "courses",   "href": "/api/students/S101/courses"},
    {"rel": "grades",    "href": "/api/students/S101/grades"},
    {"rel": "update",    "href": "/api/students/S101", "method": "PUT"},
    {"rel": "delete",    "href": "/api/students/S101", "method": "DELETE"}
  ]
}
```

> **Conversion logic:** The server is now in **complete control** of application state transitions. The client discovers available actions dynamically — no hardcoded URIs. This is the *purest* form of REST and was what Fielding originally envisioned.

---

### 3.2 Idempotency Mathematical Proof (Board-Favorite)

**Claim:** For a stateless REST API, the net resource state converges regardless of the number of repeated identical `PUT` or `DELETE` requests.

**Proof sketch for PUT:**

Let the resource state be a function $S: \mathbb{R}^+ \to \Sigma$, where $\Sigma$ is the set of all valid states. An idempotent operation $f: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ satisfies:

$$
f(f(s)) = f(s) \quad \forall s \in \Sigma
$$

For $n$ consecutive identical PUT requests $f$:

$$
S_n = f(S_{n-1}) = f^{(n)}(S_0)
$$

By the idempotency property $f \circ f = f$:

$$
S_n = f(S_0) \quad \forall n \ge 1
$$

Therefore, the state stabilizes after **exactly one** PUT request — repeated requests are **redundant but safe**.

**Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.3 REST vs SOAP — Comparative Derivation

Let us define three vectors for evaluation: $\vec{F}$ (functionality), $\vec{P}$ (performance), $\vec{C}$ (complexity).

$$
\text{Score}_{REST} = w_1 F_{REST} + w_2 P_{REST} - w_3 C_{REST}
$$

$$
\text{Score}_{SOAP} = w_1 F_{SOAP} + w_2 P_{SOAP} - w_3 C_{SOAP}
$$

For a typical modern web/mobile workload $(w_1, w_2, w_3) = (0.3, 0.5, 0.2)$:

| Parameter | REST | SOAP |
|---|---|---|
| $F$ (Features) | 0.7 (rich, growing) | 0.9 (WS-Security, WS-ReliableMessaging) |
| $P$ (Performance) | 0.95 (lightweight) | 0.5 (heavy XML parsing) |
| $C$ (Complexity) | 0.2 (low) | 0.8 (high WSDL, XSD) |
| **Net Score** | **0.745** | **0.567** |

> REST wins for modern web applications. SOAP remains relevant in **enterprise scenarios** requiring formal contracts, ACID-grade transactions, and WS-* standards.

---

### 3.4 A Complete REST Request–Response Trace (Step-by-Step)

We will derive a complete `GET` request lifecycle through a layered system.

**Step 1 — Client constructs request:**
$$
\text{Request} = \text{Method} \cup \text{URI} \cup \text{Headers} \cup \text{Body}
$$
$$
= \{GET, /api/students/S101, \{Accept: application/json, Authorization: Bearer <JWT>\}, \emptyset\}
$$

**Step 2 — DNS resolution:**
$$
\text{api.ktuuniversity.edu} \xrightarrow{DNS} IP_{server} = 192.0.2.45
$$

**Step 3 — TCP 3-way handshake:**
$$
\text{SYN} \to \text{SYN+ACK} \to \text{ACK} \quad (RTT_{setup} \approx 1.2 RTT)
$$

**Step 4 — TLS 1.3 handshake (if HTTPS):**
$$
\text{ClientHello} \to \text{ServerHello + EncryptedExtensions} \to \text{Finished} \quad (RTT_{TLS} = 1 \text{ RTT})
$$

**Step 5 — HTTP request transmission:**
$$
T_{req} = \frac{S_{headers} + S_{body}}{B_{bw}} \quad \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 6 — Server-side processing:**
$$
T_{server} = T_{auth} + T_{db} + T_{serial}
$$

**Step 7 — HTTP response transmission:**
$$
\text{Status: 200 OK, Content-Type: application/json}
$$

**Step 8 — Client renders representation.**

**Total time-to-first-byte:**
$$
T_{TTFB} = RTT_{DNS} + RTT_{setup} + RTT_{TLS} + T_{req} + T_{server}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level REST Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CLIENT LAYER\nBrowser, Mobile App, IoT Device] -->|HTTP Request\nGET / POST / PUT / DELETE| B[EDGE LAYER\nCDN, Load Balancer, API Gateway]
    B --> C[AUTH LAYER\nOAuth 2.0, JWT Validation]
    C --> D[BUSINESS LOGIC LAYER\nControllers, Services, Repositories]
    D --> E[PERSISTENCE LAYER\nRDBMS, NoSQL, Cache]
    E -->|Data| D
    D -->|Representation| C
    C -->|Validated Response| B
    B -->|HTTP Response\n200 / 201 / 204 / 404 / 500| A

    style A fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Richardson Maturity Model Progression (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0["LEVEL 0\nSwamp of POX\nSingle URI, Single POST\nAction in Body"]
    L1["LEVEL 1\nResources\nMultiple URIs\nResource Addressing"]
    L2["LEVEL 2\nHTTP Verbs\nGET POST PUT DELETE\nStatus Codes"]
    L3["LEVEL 3\nHATEOAS\nHypermedia Links\nSelf-Describing"]

    L0 ==>|"Add URIs"| L1
    L1 ==>|"Add HTTP Methods"| L2
    L2 ==>|"Add Hypermedia"| L3

    style L0 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    style L3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 REST CRUD Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[("RESOURCE\n/api/students")]
    R -->|GET| OP1[List All Students\nReturns 200 + JSON Array]
    R -->|POST| OP2[Create Student\nReturns 201 + Location Header]
    R -->|PUT| OP3[Replace Student\nReturns 200 / 204]
    R -->|DELETE| OP4[Remove Student\nReturns 204]
    R -->|PATCH| OP5[Partial Update\nReturns 200 + JSON]

    style R fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style OP1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    style OP3 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

---

### 4.4 Stateless Request–Response Sequence (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLI as Client
    participant LB  as Load Balancer
    participant API as REST API Server
    participant DB  as Database
    participant CACHE as Redis Cache

    CLI->>LB: GET /api/students/S101 (with JWT)
    LB->>API: Forward Request
    API->>API: Validate JWT, Parse Headers
    API->>CACHE: Check Cache for S101
    alt Cache HIT
        CACHE-->>API: Return Cached JSON (304/200)
    else Cache MISS
        API->>DB: SELECT * FROM students WHERE id=S101
        DB-->>API: Result Row
        API->>CACHE: Store with TTL=300s
        API-->>LB: 200 OK + JSON
    end
    LB-->>CLI: HTTP 200 OK + ETag + Cache-Control
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a RESTful Service

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        REQ[HTTP Request]
    end

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING ZONE"]
        ROUTER[Router / Dispatcher]
        AUTH[Authentication Middleware]
        VALID[Input Validation]
        CTRL[Controller]
        SVC[Service Layer]
        REPO[Repository Layer]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTGOING RESPONSE ZONE"]
        SER[Serializer: Object to JSON]
        STATUS[Status Code Mapper]
        RESP[HTTP Response]
    end

    REQ --> ROUTER --> AUTH --> VALID --> CTRL --> SVC --> REPO
    REPO -->|Domain Model| SVC --> CTRL
    CTRL --> SER --> STATUS --> RESP

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define REST. List any four guiding constraints of REST architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an **architectural style** for distributed hypermedia systems proposed by **Roy Fielding in 2000** in his PhD dissertation. It treats data as **resources** identified by **URIs** and manipulated via **standard HTTP methods**.

Four guiding constraints:
1. **Client–Server** — Separation of UI and data concerns.
2. **Stateless** — Each request is self-contained; server stores no client context.
3. **Cacheable** — Responses must indicate cacheability.
4. **Uniform Interface** — Standardized resource addressing and manipulation.

*(+1 mark for naming Fielding; +1 for any 4 constraints with one-line explanation; +1 for brief definition.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between PUT and POST HTTP methods in REST.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `PUT` | `POST` |
|---|---|---|
| **Idempotent** | Yes | No |
| **URI** | Client specifies resource URI | Server generates URI (Location header) |
| **Purpose** | Create or **replace** at known URI | **Create** a new subordinate resource |
| **Response Code** | `200 OK` / `204 No Content` | `201 Created` + `Location` header |
| **Safety** | Unsafe (modifies state) | Unsafe (modifies state) |

*(1 mark for each distinguishing row, capped at 3.)*

---

### 📘 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **six architectural constraints** of REST with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Richardson Maturity Model (RMM)**. Illustrate with a small example how a Student API evolves from Level 0 to Level 3. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Client–Server (1 mark):**
Separates concerns — client handles UI, server handles data and logic. Example: React frontend calling Spring Boot REST backend.

**2. Statelessness (1.5 marks):**
Server stores no client session data. Every request carries full context (e.g., JWT in `Authorization` header). Example: `Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1...`.

**3. Cacheability (1 mark):**
Responses use `Cache-Control: max-age=3600` and `ETag` headers. Example: A `GET /api/news/headlines` response cached at CDN edge for 1 hour.

**4. Uniform Interface (2 marks):** Has four sub-constraints:
- Resource identification via URI → `GET /api/students/S101`
- Manipulation via representations → client sees JSON, not DB row
- Self-descriptive messages → `Content-Type: application/json`
- HATEOAS → response contains navigation links

**5. Layered System (1 mark):**
Client sees only the gateway; intermediate proxies, load balancers are invisible. Example: Cloudflare CDN + AWS ALB + EC2 API server.

**6. Code-On-Demand (0.5 mark):**
Optional. Server sends executable code (JS/WASM). Example: legacy Web 1.0 applets, modern feature flags.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Richardson Maturity Model (RMM)** — 3 levels of increasing REST compliance (Level 0 is the baseline).

| Level | Concept | URI Style | Methods | Example Endpoint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | POX Tunnel | Single URI | POST only | `POST /api` with `<getStudent><id>S101</id></getStudent>` |
| **1** | Resources | Multiple URIs | Mostly POST | `POST /students/S101` |
| **2** | HTTP Verbs | Resource URIs | GET/POST/PUT/DELETE | `GET /students/S101` |
| **3** | HATEOAS | Resource URIs | Full HTTP verbs | Response includes `{"links": [...]}` |

**Example progression (1 mark each level):**

**Level 0 (Swamp of POX):**
```http
POST /apiServer
Content-Type: text/xml

<request>
  <operation>getStudent</operation>
  <id>S101</id>
</request>
```

**Level 1 (Resources):**
```http
POST /students/S101
Content-Type: application/json
{"action": "get"}
```

**Level 2 (HTTP Verbs + Status Codes):**
```http
GET /students/S101 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.university.edu
Accept: application/json
Authorization: Bearer eyJ...

→ 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
{"id":"S101","name":"Anu","cgpa":9.1}
```

**Level 3 (HATEOAS):**
```http
GET /students/S101

→ 200 OK
{
  "id":"S101",
  "name":"Anu",
  "cgpa":9.1,
  "links":[
    {"rel":"courses","href":"/students/S101/courses"},
    {"rel":"grades","href":"/students/S101/grades"},
    {"rel":"self","href":"/students/S101"}
  ]
}
```

**Most APIs in the industry operate at Level 2** (Google, GitHub, Stripe). Level 3 (full HATEOAS) is rare in production due to caching complexity and tooling limitations.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **REST and SOAP** web services across **eight parameters**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a RESTful API for a **"Library Management System"** with at least **5 resources**, specifying URIs, HTTP methods, status codes, and a sample JSON representation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

| Parameter | REST | SOAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Representational State Transfer | Simple Object Access Protocol |
| **Type** | Architectural style | Protocol |
| **Data Format** | JSON, XML, YAML, plain text | XML only |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS |
| **State** | Stateless | Can be stateful or stateless |
| **Contract** | No formal contract | WSDL (Web Services Description Language) |
| **Security** | HTTPS, OAuth2, JWT | WS-Security, SAML |
| **Performance** | Lightweight, faster | Heavier due to XML envelope |
| **Error Handling** | HTTP Status Codes (4xx, 5xx) | SOAP Faults in XML body |
| **Tooling** | Postman, Swagger/OpenAPI, curl | SoapUI, Apache CXF |
| **Best For** | Web, mobile, public APIs | Enterprise, banking, formal contracts |
| **Example** | GitHub REST API | Banking SWIFT integrations |

*(1 mark for the comparison table covering 7–8 distinct parameters; remaining marks for examples.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Library Management System REST API Design**

**1. Resources Identified (1 mark):**
- `books`, `members`, `loans`, `authors`, `categories`

**2. URI Design & Operations (3 marks):**

| Resource | URI | GET (Read) | POST (Create) | PUT (Update) | DELETE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Books | `/api/books` | List all books | Add new book | — | — |
| Book | `/api/books/{id}` | Get one book | — | Replace book | Remove book |
| Members | `/api/members` | List all members | Register member | — | — |
| Member | `/api/members/{id}` | Get one member | — | Update member | Deregister |
| Loans | `/api/loans` | List all loans | Issue new loan | — | — |
| Loan | `/api/loans/{id}` | Get one loan | — | Update (e.g., return) | Cancel loan |
| Search | `/api/books?author=Rowling` | Filter books | — | — | — |

**3. Sample JSON Representation (2 marks):**

```json
{
  "bookId": "B-2024-001",
  "title": "Clean Code",
  "authors": ["Robert C. Martin"],
  "isbn": "9780132350884",
  "category": "Software Engineering",
  "totalCopies": 5,
  "availableCopies": 3,
  "publishedYear": 2008,
  "links": [
    {"rel": "self",     "href": "/api/books/B-2024-001"},
    {"rel": "loans",    "href": "/api/loans?bookId=B-2024-001"},
    {"rel": "category", "href": "/api/categories/SE"},
    {"rel": "authors",  "href": "/api/authors/RCMartin"}
  ]
}
```

**4. Status Code Conventions (1 mark):**
- `200 OK` — successful read
- `201 Created` — resource created
- `204 No Content` — successful delete/update
- `404 Not Found` — resource missing
- `409 Conflict` — duplicate ISBN registration
- `400 Bad Request` — malformed JSON
- `401 Unauthorized` — missing/invalid JWT
- `500 Internal Server Error` — server-side failure

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SOAP and REST in definition questions.** REST is an *architectural style*; SOAP is a *protocol*. (Penalty: -2 marks.)
> 2. **Idempotency is NOT the same as safety.** `GET` is *safe* and *idempotent*. `PUT`/`DELETE` are *idempotent but unsafe*. `POST` is neither.
> 3. **Avoid the term "REST API" loosely** — strictly distinguish between *REST-compliant*, *RESTful*, and *REST-like* APIs.
> 4. **Do NOT claim REST uses only JSON.** Fielding never mandated a format — XML, JSON, YAML, and even HTML are valid.
> 5. **Avoid drawing SOAP arrows in REST diagrams.** REST is resource-oriented, not operation-oriented.
> 6. **Always include status codes** in your answer to HTTP method questions — they form a 1-mark component separately evaluated.
> 7. **HATEOAS ≠ Hyperlinks everywhere.** HATEOAS is about *application state transitions*, not just pretty links.

---

### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **REST** = Representational State Transfer, an **architectural style** by **Roy Fielding (2000)**.
- REST is **NOT a protocol or standard** — SOAP is a protocol; REST is a style.
- **Resource** = any named, addressable information entity.
- **URI** = Uniform Resource Identifier; **URL** is a locatable subset.
- **Representation** = concrete form of a resource (JSON most common today).
- The **6 constraints** (Client–Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-On-Demand) — *memorize all 6; 5 mandatory + 1 optional.*
- **HTTP Methods:** `GET` (safe, idempotent, no body), `POST` (create, non-idempotent), `PUT` (replace, idempotent), `PATCH` (partial update, non-idempotent), `DELETE` (idempotent).
- **Idempotent methods:** `GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS`.
- **Status Codes:** 1xx info, 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
- **Richardson Maturity Model:** 4 levels (0=POX swamp, 1=resources, 2=HTTP verbs + status codes, 3=HATEOAS).
- Most production APIs operate at **RMM Level 2**.
- **HATEOAS** = Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State — server-driven navigation via embedded links.
- **Statelessness** means **no server-side session**; client carries all context (typically in JWT).
- **Cacheability** is enabled by `Cache-Control`, `ETag`, `Expires` headers.
- **REST vs SOAP:** REST = JSON/HTTP/lightweight; SOAP = XML/any-transport/formal WSDL contract.
- **Uniform Interface** has 4 sub-constraints: identification, representations, self-descriptive, HATEOAS.
- **JSON** is the de-facto modern representation; use `Content-Type: application/json` header.
- **Layered System** enables load balancers, proxies, CDNs to act transparently.
- **Code-On-Demand** is the *only optional* REST constraint.
- KTU exam **hot keywords**: *architectural style, constraints, statelessness, hypermedia, idempotency, uniform interface.*
- A RESTful design **must be**: addressable, stateless, cache-friendly, uniformly interfaced, layered, and optionally executable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyper Text Mark Up Language: Languages Used for Website Development, HTML5: Basic Tags, Formatting Tags, Adding Images, Lists, Embedding Multimedia in Web Pages, Inserting Tables, Internal and External Linking, Frames, Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hyper Text Mark Up Language: Languages Used for Website Development, HTML5: Basic Tags, Formatting Tags, Adding Images, Lists, Embedding Multimedia in Web Pages, Inserting Tables, Internal and External Linking, Frames, Forms # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Hyper Text Markup Language (HTML) — Formal Definition **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** is the standard, declarative, structural markup language used to create and design the skeleton of web pages. It is governed by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** and the **WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group)**. HTML uses a system of **tags** and **attributes** to annotate text, images, and other content for display in a web browser. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Official Definition:** HTML is *NOT* a programming language; it is a **markup language** because it uses tags to "mark up" or structure content. It is **declarative** — you describe *what* should appear, not *how* the browser should compute it. ## 1.2 Languages Used for Website Development A modern website is typically built using a **tiered architecture** of languages: | Layer | Purpose | Languages / Technologies | |---|---|---| | **Front-End (Client-Side)** | Structure, Style, Behavior | **HTML5** (Structure), **CSS3** (Style), **JavaScript** (Behavior) | | **Back-End (Server-Side)** | Logic, Database, Server Operations | **PHP, Python (Django/Flask), Java (Spring), Node.js, Ruby** | | **Database** | Persistent Data Storage | **MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Oracle** | | **Markup / Data Exchange** | Data interchange | **XML, JSON** | | **Stylistic / Scripting** | Dynamic & presentation logic | **jQuery, AJAX, Bootstrap, Sass** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint of a House" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of a webpage as a house:** > - **HTML** = The **structural blueprint** — walls, doors, windows, rooms. It defines *what exists*. > - **CSS** = The **interior decoration & exterior paint** — colors, fonts, layouts. It defines *how it looks*. > - **JavaScript** = The **electrical wiring & plumbing** — interactivity, lights, water flow. It defines *how it behaves*. > - **Server-Side Language (PHP/Python)** = The **municipal water & power supply** — fetched from outside, dynamic. > - **Database** = The **storage warehouse** — where belongings (data) are kept long-term. Without HTML, a browser has nothing to render. HTML is the **fundamental layer of the web**. ## 1.4 Why HTML5? (Evolution Timeline) | Version | Year | Key Innovation | |---|---|---| | HTML 1.0 | 1993 | Basic tags, links | | HTML 2.0 | 1995 | Forms, tables | | HTML 3.2 | 1997 | Scripts, applets | | HTML 4.01 | 1999 | CSS separation, strict mode | | **XHTML 1.0** | 2000 | XML-based strict syntax | | **HTML5** | **2014 (W3C Recommendation)** | **Semantics, audio/video, canvas, geolocation, offline storage, mobile-first** | > [!NOTE] > **HTML5 is the current W3C standard** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. It is designed to deliver rich content *without* requiring plugins like Adobe Flash. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an HTML5 Document Every HTML5 document follows a strict skeleton: ```html Page Title ``` - `` — Declares the document as **HTML5**. Not a tag, but a directive. - `` — The **root element**; wraps everything. - `` — Contains **metadata** (not displayed). Links CSS, sets title, charset. - `` — Contains all **visible** content. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield HTML5 Tag Reference Sheet | Category | Tag | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Structure** | ``, ``, `` | Document skeleton | `` | | **Metadata** | ``, ``, `<link>` | Page info (SEO) | `<meta charset="UTF-8">` | | **Headings** | `<h1>` … `<h6>` | Section titles | `<h1>Welcome</h1>` | | **Paragraph** | `<p>` | Block of text | `<p>Hello World</p>` | | **Line Break** | `<br>` | Single line break (self-closing) | `Line 1<br>Line 2` | | **Horizontal Rule** | `<hr>` | Thematic break (self-closing) | `<hr>` | | **Formatting** | `<b>`, `<strong>`, `<i>`, `<em>`, `<u>`, `<mark>`, `<small>`, `<del>`, `<ins>`, `<sub>`, `<sup>` | Inline text styling | `<strong>Bold</strong>` | | **Image** | `<img>` | Embed images | `<img src="pic.jpg" alt="...">` | | **Link** | `<a>` | Hyperlink (anchor) | `<a href="url">Click</a>` | | **List** | `<ul>`, `<ol>`, `<li>`, `<dl>`, `<dt>`, `<dd>` | Ordered/Unordered lists | `<ul><li>Item</li></ul>` | | **Table** | `<table>`, `<tr>`, `<th>`, `<td>`, `<caption>`, `<thead>`, `<tbody>`, `<tfoot>` | Tabular data | `<table><tr><td>...</td></tr></table>` | | **Multimedia** | `<audio>`, `<video>`, `<source>`, `<embed>`, `<object>` | Audio/Video embedding | `<video controls><source src="v.mp4"></video>` | | **Frame** | `<iframe>` | Inline frame (embed another page) | `<iframe src="page.html"></iframe>` | | **Form** | `<form>`, `<input>`, `<textarea>`, `<select>`, `<button>`, `<label>`, `<fieldset>`, `<legend>` | User input collection | `<form action="/submit">...</form>` | | **Semantic** | `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<article>`, `<section>`, `<aside>`, `<footer>` | Meaningful structure | `<nav>...</nav>` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Self-closing/void elements (`<br>`, `<hr>`, `<img>`, `<meta>`, `<input>`, `<source>`) **do not require** a closing tag. Block elements (`<p>`, `<div>`, `<h1>`) **must** be closed. ## 2.3 Attributes — The "Adjectives" of HTML Tags Attributes provide **additional information** about an element. They are written as `name="value"` inside the opening tag. **Global Attributes** (work on any tag): | Attribute | Role | Example | |---|---|---| | `id` | Unique identifier | `<div id="header">` | | `class` | CSS/JS hook (multiple allowed) | `<p class="intro note">` | | `style` | Inline CSS | `<p style="color:red">` | | `title` | Tooltip text on hover | `<abbr title="...">` | | `lang` | Language declaration | `<html lang="en">` | | `data-*` | Custom data storage | `<div data-user-id="42">` | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World - **SEO (Search Engine Optimization):** Proper semantic HTML5 improves search ranking. - **Accessibility (WCAG Compliance):** Screen readers use semantic tags to navigate. - **Mobile Responsiveness:** HTML5 + CSS3 = single codebase for all devices. - **Web APIs:** HTML5 unlocks APIs like **Geolocation, Drag & Drop, Local Storage, Canvas, WebSockets**. - **Enterprise Applications:** Used in dashboards (banking, healthcare, e-commerce) to render UI from server data. <!-- SECTION_2_END --> <!-- SECTION_3_START --> # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The KTU Standard: "Deriving" a Complete HTML5 Page Step-by-Step Below is the **logical construction flow** of an HTML5 page — the same way you'd answer a "design a webpage" question in the exam. ### Step 1 — Declaration ```html <!DOCTYPE html> ``` **Why:** Tells the browser to render in **standards mode** (HTML5). Without it, browsers fall back to "quirks mode". ### Step 2 — Root & Language ```html <html lang="en"> ``` **Why:** Root container; `lang="en"` helps screen readers and search engines. ### Step 3 — Metadata Block ```html <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> <title>Modern Web Apps - KTU ``` **Why:** `charset="UTF-8"` supports all international characters. `viewport` is **mandatory for mobile responsiveness**. ### Step 4 — Formatting Tags (Inline Styling for Meaning) ```html

Module 2: HTML5

Use <strong> for important text and highlighted text.

``` **Why:** `` is purely visual; `` carries **semantic importance**. `` is semantic emphasis. `` highlights (like a highlighter pen). ### Step 5 — Adding Images ```html ``` **Why:** `src` = image path; `alt` = **mandatory alternative text for accessibility & SEO**. Width/height in pixels (or `%`). ### Step 6 — Lists **Unordered (bulleted):** ```html
  • HTML5
  • CSS3
  • JavaScript
``` **Ordered (numbered) with start:** ```html
  1. First
  2. Second
``` **Why:** `type` can be `1, A, a, I, i`; `start` = beginning value. **Description List (definition):** ```html
HTML
HyperText Markup Language
``` ### Step 7 — Embedding Multimedia **Audio:** ```html ``` **Video:** ```html ``` **Why:** Multiple `` tags provide **fallback formats**. `poster` is the image shown before play. **Embedding YouTube (iframe technique):** ```html ``` ### Step 8 — Inserting Tables ```html
Student Marks
Roll No Name Marks
1 Arjun 95
2 Meera 88
Total 183
``` **Analytical Matrix — Table Attributes:** | Attribute | Scope | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `border` | `` | Border thickness (in pixels) | | `cellpadding` | `
` | Inner cell padding | | `cellspacing` | `
` | Space between cells | | `colspan` | `
`, `` | Span across columns | | `rowspan` | ``, `` | Span across rows | | `headers` | `` | Links cell to `` for accessibility | | `scope` | `` | `row` / `col` / `rowgroup` / `colgroup` | ### Step 9 — Internal & External Linking **External Link (absolute URL):** ```html KTU Official Website ``` - `target="_blank"` → opens in new tab - `rel="noopener noreferrer"` → **security best practice** in HTML5 **Internal Link (same site):** ```html About Us Jump to Section 2 Go to Laptops ``` **Email & Phone:** ```html Email Us Call Us ``` **Image as a Link:** ```html ``` ### Step 10 — Frames (` ``` > [!WARNING] > The **HTML5 standard deprecates ``, ``, and ``** (used in HTML 4). In modern HTML5, use `<iframe>` for embedding external/inner pages. KTU examiners may still ask about old frames — mention both. ### Step 11 — Forms (User Input Collection) ```html <form action="/login" method="POST" enctype="multipart/form-data"> <fieldset> <legend>User Registration</legend> <label for="fname">First Name:</label> <input type="text" id="fname" name="fname" required placeholder="Enter name" maxlength="50"> <label for="email">Email:</label> <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required> <label for="pwd">Password:</label> <input type="password" id="pwd" name="pwd" minlength="8"> <label for="age">Age:</label> <input type="number" id="age" name="age" min="18" max="100"> <label for="dob">Date of Birth:</label> <input type="date" id="dob" name="dob"> <label for="file">Upload Resume:</label> <input type="file" id="file" name="resume" accept=".pdf,.docx"> <fieldset> <legend>Gender</legend> <input type="radio" id="m" name="gender" value="male"> <label for="m">Male</label> <input type="radio" id="f" name="gender" value="female"> <label for="f">Female</label> </fieldset> <label for="course">Course:</label> <select id="course" name="course" multiple> <option value="btech">B.Tech</option> <option value="mtech" selected>M.Tech</option> <option value="phd">Ph.D</option> </select> <label for="msg">Comments:</label> <textarea id="msg" name="msg" rows="4" cols="40"></textarea> <label> <input type="checkbox" name="agree" required> I agree to the terms </label> <button type="submit">Register</button> <button type="reset">Clear</button> </fieldset> </form> ``` **Analytical Matrix — HTML5 New Input Types (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):** | Input Type | Purpose | |---|---| | `color` | Color picker | | `date` | Date (yyyy-mm-dd) | | `datetime-local` | Date + time (no timezone) | | `email` | Validates email format | | `month` | Month & year picker | | `number` | Numeric spinner | | `range` | Slider control | | `search` | Search field with clear button | | `tel` | Telephone number | | `time` | Time picker | | `url` | Validates URL | | `week` | Week & year picker | **Form Attributes:** | Attribute | Purpose | |---|---| | `action` | URL to submit data | | `method` | `GET` (URL visible) / `POST` (in body) | | `enctype` | Encoding type, e.g. `multipart/form-data` for file upload | | `target` | `_self`, `_blank`, `_parent`, `_top` | | `autocomplete` | `on` / `off` | | `novalidate` | Disable HTML5 form validation | | `required` | Field cannot be empty | | `placeholder` | Hint text | | `pattern` | Regex validation | | `min`, `max`, `step` | Numeric/range constraints | <!-- SECTION_3_END --> <!-- SECTION_4_START --> # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 HTML5 Document Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DOCTYPE html] --> B[html lang=en] B --> C[head] B --> D[body] C --> C1[meta charset] C --> C2[meta viewport] C --> C3[title] C --> C4[link to CSS] C --> C5[script async] D --> D1[header - semantic] D --> D2[nav - menu] D --> D3[main - content] D2 --> D2a[ul - links] D3 --> D3a[article] D3 --> D3b[section] D3 --> D3c[aside] D --> D4[footer] D3a --> E1[h1 to h6] D3a --> E2[p - paragraph] D3a --> E3[img - image] D3a --> E4[video / audio] D3a --> E5[table] D3a --> E6[form] D3a --> E7[iframe] ``` ## 4.2 Web Development Language Stack ```mermaid flowchart LR USER[User Browser] -->|HTTP Request| SERVER[Web Server] SERVER -->|HTML+CSS+JS| USER SERVER <-->|Query| DB[(Database)] subgraph CLIENT_SIDE[Client-Side - Front End] HTML[HTML5 Structure] CSS[CSS3 Style] JS[JavaScript Behavior] end subgraph SERVER_SIDE[Server-Side - Back End] PHP[PHP] PY[Python] JAVA[Java / Spring] NODE[Node.js] end USER -.-> CLIENT_SIDE SERVER -.-> SERVER_SIDE SERVER_SIDE <--> DB ``` ## 4.3 HTML Form Data Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant F as HTML Form participant V as Browser Validation participant S as Server (action URL) participant D as Database U->>F: Fill inputs U->>F: Click Submit F->>V: Validate (required, type, pattern) alt Valid V->>S: Send Form Data (GET/POST) S->>D: Store / Process D-->>S: Confirmation S-->>U: Success Response Page else Invalid V-->>U: Show Error Messages end ``` ## 4.4 Multimedia Embedding Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Multimedia Content] --> M1[audio tag] M --> M2[video tag] M --> M3[iframe - YouTube/Vimeo] M --> M4[embed - legacy fallback] M --> M5[object - PDF/Flash fallback] M1 --> S1[source: mp3] M1 --> S2[source: ogg] M2 --> S3[source: mp4] M2 --> S4[source: webm] S1 --> F[Fallback Text: Browser unsupported] S3 --> F ``` <!-- SECTION_4_END --> <!-- SECTION_5_START --> # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Differentiate between HTML and XHTML. List any four features of HTML5.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | HTML | XHTML | |---|---|---| | Type | Markup language (forgiving) | Stricter XML-based markup | | Case-sensitivity | Tags can be upper/lower | Must be **lowercase** | | Closing tags | Optional for some | **Mandatory** for all | | Attribute quotes | Optional | **Mandatory** | | File extension | `.html` or `.htm` | `.xhtml` or `.xml` | | Standards body | W3C + WHATWG | W3C | **Four features of HTML5:** 1. **Native Audio/Video support** — no plugins (Flash) needed. 2. **Canvas & SVG** for 2D/vector graphics. 3. **Semantic elements** — `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<article>`, `<footer>`. 4. **Web Storage** — `localStorage` & `sessionStorage` (replaces cookies for client-side). 5. **New form input types** — `email`, `date`, `range`, `number`, `color`. 6. **Geolocation API** and **Drag & Drop API**. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **Explain the difference between `<strong>` and `<b>` tags with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** - `<b>` (**Bold**) is a *presentational* tag — it only makes text visually bold. It carries **no semantic meaning**. - `<strong>` is a *semantic* tag — it indicates that the text is of **strong importance or seriousness**. Browsers usually render it bold, but screen readers may **emphasize it vocally**. **Example:** ```html <p>The <b>exam</b> starts at 9 AM.</p> <!-- Just visually bold --> <p><strong>Warning:</strong> Do not cheat.</p> <!-- Semantically important --> ``` *Always prefer `<strong>` for accessibility and SEO.* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** > **(a)** Design a complete HTML5 page for a **"Student Profile Dashboard"** showing the student's name, photo, and a table of subjects with marks. Use semantic tags, image, and a properly structured table with `<thead>`, `<tbody>`, and `<tfoot>`. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Write HTML code to create a **registration form** for a B.Tech admission portal with fields: Name, Email, Password, Date of Birth, Gender (radio), Branch (dropdown), Skills (checkboxes), and Submit/Reset buttons. Apply HTML5 validation. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution A(a) — Student Profile Dashboard ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <title>Student Profile Dashboard</title> </head> <body> <header> <h1>Student Profile Dashboard</h1> </header> <main> <article> <figure> <img src="student.jpg" alt="Student Photo" width="150" height="150"> <figcaption>Arjun R. — S6 CSE</figcaption> </figure> <p><strong>Name:</strong> Arjun Raj</p> <p><strong>Roll No:</strong> KTU2021CS042</p> <p><strong>Branch:</strong> Computer Science & Engineering</p> </article> <section> <h2>Semester Marks</h2> <table border="1" cellpadding="8" cellspacing="0"> <caption>Academic Performance - S5</caption> <thead> <tr> <th scope="col">Subject Code</th> <th scope="col">Subject Name</th> <th scope="col">Marks (/100)</th> <th scope="col">Grade</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>CST301</td> <td>Data Structures</td> <td>92</td> <td>A+</td> </tr> <tr> <td>CST303</td> <td>Operating Systems</td> <td>85</td> <td>A</td> </tr> <tr> <td>CST305</td> <td>Database Systems</td> <td>78</td> <td>B+</td> </tr> </tbody> <tfoot> <tr> <td colspan="2"><strong>Total</strong></td> <td><strong>255</strong></td> <td><strong>A</strong></td> </tr> </tfoot> </table> </section> </main> <footer> <p>© 2024 KTU - Modern Web Applications</p> </footer> </body> </html> ``` **Explanation:** - **Semantic tags** used: `<header>`, `<main>`, `<article>`, `<section>`, `<footer>`, `<figure>`, `<figcaption>`. - **Image** with `alt` text and `figure/figcaption` for accessibility. - **Table** uses `<thead>`, `<tbody>`, `<tfoot>`, `colspan`, and `scope` for proper accessibility. - `<caption>` provides the table title for screen readers. ### Model Solution A(b) — B.Tech Registration Form ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <title>B.Tech Admission Registration</title> </head> <body> <h1>B.Tech Admission Portal - 2024</h1> <form action="/register" method="POST" novalidate> <fieldset> <legend>Personal Information</legend> <label for="name">Full Name *</label><br> <input type="text" id="name" name="name" required minlength="3" maxlength="50" placeholder="Enter full name"><br><br> <label for="email">Email *</label><br> <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required placeholder="user@ktu.ac.in"><br><br> <label for="pwd">Password *</label><br> <input type="password" id="pwd" name="pwd" required minlength="8" pattern="(?=.*\d)(?=.*[a-z])(?=.*[A-Z]).{8,}" title="Must contain 8+ chars, 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 digit"><br><br> <label for="dob">Date of Birth *</label><br> <input type="date" id="dob" name="dob" required min="2000-01-01" max="2007-12-31"><br><br> </fieldset> <fieldset> <legend>Gender *</legend> <input type="radio" id="male" name="gender" value="male" required> <label for="male">Male</label> <input type="radio" id="female" name="gender" value="female"> <label for="female">Female</label> <input type="radio" id="other" name="gender" value="other"> <label for="other">Other</label><br><br> </fieldset> <label for="branch">Branch *</label><br> <select id="branch" name="branch" required> <option value="">--Select--</option> <option value="cse">Computer Science</option> <option value="ece">Electronics</option> <option value="mech">Mechanical</option> <option value="civil">Civil</option> <option value="eee">Electrical</option> </select><br><br> <fieldset> <legend>Skills (Select all that apply)</legend> <input type="checkbox" id="web" name="skills" value="web"> <label for="web">Web Development</label> <input type="checkbox" id="py" name="skills" value="python"> <label for="py">Python</label> <input type="checkbox" id="java" name="skills" value="java"> <label for="java">Java</label> <input type="checkbox" id="ml" name="skills" value="ml"> <label for="ml">Machine Learning</label> </fieldset><br> <button type="submit">Register</button> <button type="reset">Reset</button> </form> </body> </html> ``` **HTML5 Validation Used:** `required`, `type="email"`, `minlength`, `maxlength`, `min`, `max`, `pattern` (regex), and semantic input types. --- > ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** > **(a)** Explain the different **types of lists** supported by HTML5 with syntax and examples. Compare the use of `<ul>`, `<ol>`, and `<dl>`. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Demonstrate how to **embed audio, video, and an external YouTube video** in an HTML5 page with proper fallback handling. Explain the role of the `<source>` tag. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution B(a) — Types of Lists HTML5 supports three primary list types: **1. Unordered List `<ul>`** — Bulleted, order doesn't matter. ```html <ul type="square"> <li>HTML5</li> <li>CSS3</li> <li>JavaScript</li> </ul> ``` **Attributes:** `type="disc" | "circle" | "square"` (CSS `list-style-type` preferred in HTML5). **2. Ordered List `<ol>`** — Numbered, sequential. ```html <ol type="I" start="3" reversed> <li>Wake up</li> <li>Code</li> <li>Sleep</li> </ol> ``` **Attributes:** `type` (`1`, `A`, `a`, `I`, `i`), `start`, `reversed`. **3. Description List `<dl>`** — Term-definition pairs. ```html <dl> <dt>HTML</dt> <dd>HyperText Markup Language</dd> <dt>CSS</dt> <dd>Cascading Style Sheets</dd> </dl> ``` **Comparison Table:** | Feature | `<ul>` | `<ol>` | `<dl>` | |---|---|---|---| | Order | Unordered | Ordered/Sequential | Term-Definition | | Default marker | Bullet (•) | Number (1,2,3) | None | | Typical use | Menus, feature lists | Rankings, steps | Glossaries, FAQs | | Child elements | `<li>` | `<li>` | `<dt>`, `<dd>` | **Nested List Example:** ```html <ul> <li>Front-End <ul> <li>HTML5</li> <li>CSS3</li> </ul> </li> <li>Back-End <ol> <li>Node.js</li> <li>Python</li> </ol> </li> </ul> ``` ### Model Solution B(b) — Embedding Multimedia **Audio Embedding:** ```html <audio controls autoplay loop muted> <source src="music.mp3" type="audio/mpeg"> <source src="music.ogg" type="audio/ogg"> Your browser does not support the audio element. </audio> ``` **Video Embedding:** ```html <video width="640" height="360" controls poster="thumbnail.jpg" preload="metadata"> <source src="intro.mp4" type="video/mp4"> <source src="intro.webm" type="video/webm"> <track src="captions_en.vtt" kind="subtitles" srclang="en" label="English"> Your browser does not support the video tag. </video> ``` **YouTube via `<iframe>`:** ```html <iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/dQw4w9WgXcQ" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen> </iframe> ``` **Role of `<source>` tag:** - The `<source>` element allows specifying **multiple media files in different formats**. - The browser picks the **first one it supports** from the list — this is the **fallback mechanism**. - Attributes: `src` (file URL), `type` (MIME type), `media` (query for responsive sources), `srcset` (for images). **`preload` attribute values:** `none` | `metadata` | `auto`. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Missing `alt` attribute** in `<img>` → loses accessibility marks (-1 to -2). > 2. **Not using semantic tags** (`<header>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<footer>`) when asked for a complete page → -2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting to close block tags** (`<p>`, `<div>`, `<li>`) → strict XHTML penalty if mentioned. > 4. **Wrong form `method`:** Default is GET; state explicitly when uploading files use `POST` + `enctype="multipart/form-data"`. > 5. **Using deprecated `<frameset>`** instead of `<iframe>` for modern HTML5 questions → -2 marks. > 6. **Missing `lang`, `charset`, `viewport`** in `<head>` → -1 each. > 7. **No fallback text** in `<audio>`/`<video>` → -1 mark. > 8. **Confusing `<b>` vs `<strong>` and `<i>` vs `<em>`** in theory questions → -1 to -2. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Remember High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 Exam:** - ✅ **HTML = Markup language**, not a programming language. Governed by **W3C & WHATWG**. - ✅ **HTML5 DOCTYPE** is simply `<!DOCTYPE html>` (case-insensitive, but lowercase preferred). - ✅ **Root element:** `<html lang="en">`. **Metadata** in `<head>`, **content** in `<body>`. - ✅ **Semantic tags** (HTML5): `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<article>`, `<section>`, `<aside>`, `<footer>`, `<figure>`, `<figcaption>`. - ✅ **Self-closing/void tags:** `<br>`, `<hr>`, `<img>`, `<meta>`, `<input>`, `<link>`, `<source>`, `<area>`, `<base>`, `<col>`, `<embed>`, `<param>`, `<track>`, `<wbr>`. - ✅ **`<b>` vs `<strong>`:** Visual vs Semantic. **`<i>` vs `<em>`:** Visual vs Semantic. **Always prefer semantic tags.** - ✅ **Image must have `alt`** for accessibility; use `width` and `height` to prevent layout shift. - ✅ **Lists:** `<ul>` (bullets), `<ol>` (numbers, supports `type`, `start`, `reversed`), `<dl>` (term-definition with `<dt>`, `<dd>`). - ✅ **Multimedia:** `<audio>` and `<video>` are HTML5 native; use multiple `<source>` for format fallbacks. `<track>` for subtitles (`.vtt`). - ✅ **Tables** must have `<thead>`, `<tbody>`, `<tfoot>` for semantic clarity; use `colspan`, `rowspan`, `scope` for accessibility. - ✅ **Links:** `<a href="...">` — absolute vs relative. `target="_blank"` + `rel="noopener noreferrer"` for security. `#anchor` for in-page jumps. - ✅ **Frames:** HTML5 uses **`<iframe>`** only. Old `<frameset>`, `<frame>`, `<noframes>` are **deprecated**. - ✅ **Forms:** `<form action="" method="GET|POST">`. New HTML5 input types: `email`, `url`, `date`, `time`, `number`, `range`, `color`, `tel`, `search`, `month`, `week`, `datetime-local`. - ✅ **Validation attributes:** `required`, `min`, `max`, `minlength`, `maxlength`, `pattern` (regex), `step`. - ✅ **File upload requires:** `method="POST"` + `enctype="multipart/form-data"` + `<input type="file">`. - ✅ **`<button type="">`** can be `submit` (default), `reset`, or `button`. - ✅ **`<select>`** supports `multiple` and `size`; first `<option>` can be `selected` or have empty value for placeholder. - ✅ **Web stack:** HTML (structure) + CSS (style) + JavaScript (behavior) = Front-end. PHP/Python/Node = Back-end. MySQL/MongoDB = Database. - ✅ **Character encoding:** Always use `<meta charset="UTF-8">` for international support. - ✅ **Mobile responsive:** `<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">` is **mandatory**. - ✅ **Comments:** `<!-- comment -->` (browser does not render). - ✅ **Entities:** `<` (<), `>` (>), `&` (&), ` ` (non-breaking space), `©` (©). <!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascading Style Sheets (CSS3): Basics of Cascading Style Sheets, Advantages of CSS, External Style Sheet, Internal Style Sheet, Inline Style Sheet, CSS Syntax, Color, Background, Font, Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cascading Style Sheets (CSS3): Basics of Cascading Style Sheets, Advantages of CSS, External Style Sheet, Internal Style Sheet, Inline Style Sheet, CSS Syntax, Color, Background, Font, Images ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cascading Style Sheets (CSS)** is a **declarative, rule-based style sheet language** used to describe the **presentation semantics** (layout, colors, fonts, spacing, and visual effects) of a document written in a markup language such as **HTML5** or **XML**. The term **"Cascading"** refers to the prioritized, hierarchical scheme by which the browser resolves conflicts when **multiple style rules** attempt to control the same HTML element. The cascade combines: 1. **Origin** (User-Agent, Author, User) 2. **Importance** (`!important` declarations) 3. **Specificity** (selector weight) 4. **Source Order** (later rule wins) CSS3 is the modularized third level of CSS, organized into independent specifications (Selectors, Box Model, Backgrounds, Borders, Fonts, Animations, Transforms, Flexbox, Grid, etc.). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The House Painter Model"** 🏠 > > Imagine an HTML document is the **bare skeleton of a house** — walls, doors, windows, electrical wiring. CSS is the **interior designer and painter** who walks through the house and decides: > - What **color** are the walls? > - What **font** is on the nameplate? > - What **background** image is behind the sofa? > - How **large** should the kitchen window appear? > > **Three ways the designer can give instructions:** > 1. **A handwritten sticky note** on a single door → **Inline Style** (affects only that element). > 2. **A printed instruction sheet pinned to the kitchen wall** → **Internal Style Sheet** (affects that one HTML page). > 3. **A master design rulebook placed in the office** used across the entire building → **External Style Sheet** (affects the whole website). > > The **"Cascade"** is the rule the designer follows: *If the sticky note says "Blue" but the rulebook says "White", the more specific instruction (sticky note) wins, unless the rulebook says `!important`.* ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (CSS-3 Specifications) - **Latest stable recommendation:** CSS Level 3 (modularized; updated continuously) - **Default specificity weights:** inline (1000) > ID (100) > Class/Attribute/Pseudo-class (10) > Element/Pseudo-element (1) - **Standard color models:** RGB, HSL, HEX, named keywords - **Standard image formats supported:** JPEG, PNG, GIF, SVG, WebP, AVIF --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Syntax Sheet ### 2.1 Three Methods of Inserting CSS > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners frequently test the **differences, syntax, and use-cases** of all three insertion methods.
