It is a **separate set of rules (API)** maintained by the **World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)** that any host language can implement. ### Key Terminology (As per KTU Board Expectations) | Term | Formal Meaning | |---|---| | **Node** | The fundamental unit of the DOM tree; everything is a node. | | **Document** | The root node of the entire DOM tree. | | **Element Node** | Represents an HTML tag (e.g., `

`, `

`). | | **Text Node** | Contains the textual content inside an element. | | **Attribute Node** | Represents the attributes of an element (deprecated as a node type, but properties are accessible). | | **NodeList** | A static or live collection of nodes returned by DOM queries. | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Mental Model) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The DOM is the "Blueprint of a House"** Imagine you walk into an architect's office with a hand-drawn sketch of your house. The sketch is **HTML**—a static description of structure. Now imagine the architect hands you a **3D, interactive, fully labeled digital model** of the same house. You can: - Walk into any room (**traversal**) - Open a window or change a wall's color (**manipulation**) - Knock down a wall and rebuild it (**creation/deletion**) - Place sensors that react when you enter a room (**event handling**) That **interactive 3D model is the DOM**, and **JavaScript is the remote control** that operates it. The browser, upon parsing your HTML, builds this tree. JavaScript then asks the tree: - *"Give me the element with id='header'"* (`getElementById`) - *"Change this text"* (`textContent`) - *"Listen for a click here"* (`addEventListener`) --- ## 1.3 Why DOM Matters in KTU's *Modern Web Applications* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Every dynamic, interactive web page you have ever used (Gmail, Facebook, Amazon search suggestions) works because of DOM manipulation through JavaScript.** Without the DOM, the web would be a static document like a printed PDF. **High-Yield Board Points to Memorize:** - The DOM represents the document as a **logical tree** with `document` at the root. - Each HTML element becomes an **Element Node**. - Text inside elements becomes a **Text Node** (a child of the element). - Whitespace between tags in source code becomes **Text Nodes** too (a common source of bugs). - The DOM is **live**: changes made via JavaScript are immediately reflected in the rendered page. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The DOM Tree Structure — Detailed Breakdown The browser constructs a **node tree** where every component of the HTML document is represented as a node with hierarchical parent-child relationships. Consider this minimal HTML document: ```html My Page

Hello

World

``` This produces the following logical DOM tree (KHU – KTU High Yield): ``` document └── html ├── head │ └── title │ └── "My Page" (Text Node) └── body ├── h1#title │ └── "Hello" (Text Node) └── p.msg └── "World" (Text Node) ``` **Properties of this tree:** - The tree has **one root**: `document`. - Each node has exactly **one parent** (except `document`). - A node may have **zero or more children**. - Sibling nodes share the same parent. - The tree is **hierarchical** and **directed** (parent → child). --- ## 2.2 Node Types (The 12 Standard W3C Node Constants) | Constant | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `Node.ELEMENT_NODE` | 1 | An HTML element like `
`. | | `Node.ATTRIBUTE_NODE` | 2 | An attribute of an element (legacy). | | `Node.TEXT_NODE` | 3 | Raw text content. | | `Node.CDATA_SECTION_NODE` | 4 | CDATA section in XML. | | `Node.COMMENT_NODE` | 8 | An HTML comment ``. | | `Node.DOCUMENT_NODE` | 9 | The root `document` object. | | `Node.DOCUMENT_FRAGMENT_NODE` | 11 | A lightweight off-tree container. | > [!NOTE] > In KTU board exams, the most tested node types are **1 (ELEMENT), 3 (TEXT), 8 (COMMENT), and 9 (DOCUMENT)**. Memorize the numeric values. --- ## 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — DOM Methods & Properties > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exam gold**. It contains the most-asked methods/properties. Use `\vert` style to denote "or" in notes, but no vertical bars in this table to preserve markdown integrity. ### A. Selecting Elements (DOM Query Methods) | Method | Returns | Description | |---|---|---| | `document.getElementById(id)` | `Element` or `null` | Selects a single element by its `id` attribute. | | `document.getElementsByClassName(c)` | `HTMLCollection` (live) | Selects all elements with a given class. | | `document.getElementsByTagName(t)` | `HTMLCollection` (live) | Selects all elements of a given tag. | | `document.querySelector(sel)` | `Element` or `null` | First match of a **CSS selector**. | | `document.querySelectorAll(sel)` | `NodeList` (static) | All matches of a CSS selector. | > [!WARNING] > **`getElementsByClassName` returns a live HTMLCollection** — it auto-updates if the DOM changes. **`querySelectorAll` returns a static NodeList** — it's a snapshot. This distinction is a classic board question. ### B. Content & Attribute Manipulation | Property / Method | Purpose | |---|---| | `element.innerHTML` | Gets/sets HTML markup inside an element (parses HTML). | | `element.textContent` | Gets/sets plain text (safe, no HTML parsing). | | `element.innerText` | Like `textContent` but respects CSS visibility. | | `element.getAttribute(name)` | Returns the value of an attribute. | | `element.setAttribute(name, value)` | Sets an attribute. | | `element.removeAttribute(name)` | Removes an attribute. | | `element.hasAttribute(name)` | Returns `true`/`false`. | | `element.id`, `element.className` | Direct ID/class access. | | `element.classList.add/remove/toggle(c)` | Modern class manipulation. | | `element.style.property` | Inline CSS access (e.g., `style.color`). | ### C. DOM Tree Modification (CRUD Operations) | Method | Action | |---|---| | `document.createElement(tag)` | Create a new element node. | | `document.createTextNode(text)` | Create a new text node. | | `parent.appendChild(node)` | Append node as last child. | | `parent.insertBefore(new, ref)` | Insert `new` before `ref`. | | `parent.removeChild(node)` | Remove a child node. | | `parent.replaceChild(new, old)` | Replace a child node. | | `element.cloneNode(deep)` | Clone an element (`true` = with children). | | `parent.append(...nodes)` | Modern multi-arg append. | | `parent.prepend(node)` | Insert as first child. | | `el.before(node)` / `el.after(node)` | Insert sibling before/after. | | `el.remove()` | Remove the element itself. | ### D. DOM Traversal Properties | Property | Returns | |---|---| | `node.parentNode` | The parent (or `null` for `document`). | | `node.childNodes` | `NodeList` of all child nodes (incl. text). | | `node.children` | `HTMLCollection` of **element** children only. | | `node.firstChild` / `node.lastChild` | First/last child node. | | `node.firstElementChild` / `node.lastElementChild` | First/last element child. | | `node.nextSibling` / `node.previousSibling` | Adjacent sibling nodes. | | `node.nextElementSibling` / `node.previousElementSibling` | Adjacent element siblings. | ### E. Event Handling Essentials | Method / Property | Purpose | |---|---| | `element.addEventListener(ev, fn, opts)` | Attach an event handler. | | `element.removeEventListener(ev, fn)` | Detach a handler. | | `event.target` | The element where the event originated. | | `event.currentTarget` | The element with the listener attached. | | `event.preventDefault()` | Stops default browser action. | | `event.stopPropagation()` | Stops event bubbling. | | `event.type` | The event name (e.g., `"click"`). | --- ## 2.4 The Event Flow: Bubbling vs. Capturing (Board Favorite) When an event occurs on a nested element, the DOM traverses the tree in **three phases**: 1. **Capturing Phase** (top → down): Event travels from `document` down to the target's parent. 2. **Target Phase**: Event reaches the actual target element. 3. **Bubbling Phase** (bottom → up): Event bubbles back up from target to `document`. > [!IMPORTANT] > By default, listeners run in the **bubbling** phase. Pass `{ capture: true }` as the third argument to `addEventListener` to use capturing. This is a frequently asked 7-mark question in KTU exams. ```javascript // Bubbling (default) parent.addEventListener("click", handler); // Capturing parent.addEventListener("click", handler, true); // Modern syntax parent.addEventListener("click", handler, { capture: true }); ``` **Real-world utility:** Event delegation — attaching a single listener to a parent and using `event.target` to identify which child was clicked. This is highly efficient for lists or dynamic content (e.g., 1000 list items → 1 listener instead of 1000). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough 1: Building a DOM Tree from HTML > **Problem:** Given an HTML snippet, derive the corresponding DOM tree, list the node types, and predict what `getElementById` returns. **Given HTML:** ```html

First paragraph

  • Apple
  • Banana
``` ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1:** The browser's HTML parser tokenizes the document and constructs nodes. **Step 2:** The root is `document` (Node Type 9). **Step 3:** `document` has one child: `` (implicitly added by browser). The `` contains the `
`. **Step 4:** The `
` (Node Type 1) becomes an Element Node with attribute `id="container"`. **Step 5:** Inside the div, we encounter: - A `

` element. - A text node `"First "` (with trailing whitespace). - A `` element. - A text node `"paragraph"`. - Whitespace text nodes between tags (often forgotten in exams). **Step 6:** The `

    ` contains two `
  • ` elements, each with a text node child. ### Resulting Logical Tree (Detailed): ``` document (Node 9) └── html └── body └── div#container (Node 1) ├── "\n " (Text Node 3 - whitespace) ├── p (Node 1) │ ├── "First " (Text 3) │ └── b (Node 1) │ └── "paragraph" (Text 3) ├── "\n " (Text 3 - whitespace) ├── ul (Node 1) │ ├── "\n " (Text 3) │ ├── li (Node 1) │ │ └── "Apple" (Text 3) │ ├── "\n " (Text 3) │ ├── li (Node 1) │ │ └── "Banana" (Text 3) │ └── "\n " (Text 3) └── "\n" (Text 3) ``` **Step 7:** Predict `document.getElementById("container")`: - The browser scans the tree depth-first, finds the `div` with `id="container"`, and returns it. - **Return value:** The `
    ` Element Node (Type 1), not its inner HTML. > [!NOTE] > **Board Tip:** If asked "What is the return type of `getElementById`?", answer: **"A single Element object (or null if not found). It returns a reference to the live DOM element, not a string of HTML."** --- ## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 2: Dynamic List Manipulation > **Problem:** Write JavaScript to (a) create a new list item with text "Cherry", (b) append it to an existing `
      `, and (c) change the background color of all list items to light yellow. ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1:** Locate the target `
        ` element. ```javascript const list = document.getElementById("fruits"); ``` **Step 2:** Create a new `
      • ` element node (this creates the element in memory but does NOT yet insert it into the DOM). ```javascript const newItem = document.createElement("li"); ``` **Step 3:** Create a text node with the content "Cherry". ```javascript const text = document.createTextNode("Cherry"); ``` **Step 4:** Append the text node as a child of the new `
      • `. ```javascript newItem.appendChild(text); ``` **Step 5:** Append the new `
      • ` as a child of the `
          `. ```javascript list.appendChild(newItem); ``` **Step 6:** Query all `
        • ` elements (using `querySelectorAll` for a static list). ```javascript const items = list.querySelectorAll("li"); ``` **Step 7:** Iterate and apply a background color. ```javascript items.forEach(function(li) { li.style.backgroundColor = "lightyellow"; }); ``` ### Modern Equivalent (Single Chain): ```javascript const ul = document.querySelector("#fruits"); const li = document.createElement("li"); li.textContent = "Cherry"; ul.appendChild(li); ul.querySelectorAll("li").forEach(x => x.style.backgroundColor = "lightyellow"); ``` **Why `textContent` over `innerHTML` here?** - `textContent` is **safe** — it doesn't parse HTML, preventing XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) injection. - `innerHTML = "Cherry"` would also work for plain text, but if the string contained `

topics.title: Selection Statement Using if and Switch
Iterative Statement: for, for/in, while, do while, break and continue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Iterative Statement: for, for/in, while, do while, break and continue # 🔄 Iterative Statements in JavaScript — Core Technical Foundation ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **JavaScript (ECMAScript)** and **Modern Web Applications**, an **Iterative Statement** (also called a **looping construct** or **repetition statement**) is a control-flow structure that allows a block of code — known as the **loop body** — to be executed repeatedly based on the evaluation of a given **Boolean condition**. Iteration terminates when the controlling expression evaluates to `false` (or when an explicit transfer of control such as `break` is encountered). The KTU 2024 PECBT633 syllabus categorizes iterative statements into **four primary constructs** and **two flow-control modifiers**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Highlight** > The four iteration constructs specified are: > 1. **`for`** — counter-controlled loop > 2. **`for…in`** — property-enumeration loop (over object keys) > 3. **`while`** — entry-controlled (pre-test) loop > 4. **`do…while`** — exit-controlled (post-test) loop > > Plus the two **loop-modifier keywords**: > 5. **`break`** — abrupt loop termination > 6. **`continue`** — skip-to-next-iteration transfer --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Daily Assembly Roll Call" Imagine a class teacher standing at the blackboard. She has a **register (the iterable)** containing 60 student names. She starts from **Roll No. 1 (initialization)**, checks if the next roll number is within the register's length (**condition test**), reads the name aloud (**body execution**), and then moves her finger to the next roll number (**update expression**). This continues *roll-by-roll* until the last name is read, after which the roll call **terminates** naturally. - If the principal walks in and announces a **fire drill** mid-call → that is the **`break`** statement (terminate the loop entirely). - If the teacher decides to **skip calling absentees** and proceed to the next name → that is the **`continue`** statement. > [!NOTE] > **Why Iteration Matters in Modern Web Applications** > - Rendering dynamic lists (e-commerce products, social media feeds). > - Polling APIs at intervals. > - Animating frames on a ``. > - Form validation across multiple input fields. > - DOM manipulation across collections of `NodeList` elements. --- ## 1.3 Categorical Classification of Iterative Statements | Construct | Control Style | Minimum Executions | Primary Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | `for` | Counter-controlled (definite) | **0** (if condition initially false) | Known number of iterations | | `for…in` | Enumeration (object keys) | **0** | Iterating object enumerable properties | | `while` | Pre-test (entry-controlled) | **0** | Unknown iteration count, condition-driven | | `do…while` | Post-test (exit-controlled) | **1** (guaranteed) | Menu-driven, must-execute-once logic | # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Syntax Sheet ## 2.1 The `for` Statement — Counter-Controlled Iteration The `for` loop is the most compact form of definite iteration. It consolidates three loop-control operations into a single header. ### General Syntax ```javascript for (initialization; condition; update) { // loop body — statement(s) to repeat } ``` ### Operational Phases (Executed in Strict Order) 1. **Initialization Expression** — Evaluated *exactly once* before the first iteration. Typically used to declare and assign the loop counter (`let i = 0`). 2. **Condition Expression** — Evaluated *before each iteration* (including the first). If truthy → body executes; if falsy → loop terminates. 3. **Body Execution** — The statements inside the curly braces are executed. 4. **Update Expression** — Evaluated *at the end of each iteration*. Typically increments/decrements the counter (`i++`). 5. **Return to Step 2.** > [!NOTE] > The three expressions in the `for` header are **optional**. An infinite loop can be written as `for (;;) { /* code */ }`. However, this is a KTU exam pitfall — the loop will hang the browser tab if not exited via `break`. ### Worked Example with Output Trace ```javascript for (let i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { console.log("Iteration #" + i); } ``` **Output Trace Table:** | Step | i (current) | Condition `i <= 5` | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1 | true | Print "Iteration #1" | | 2 | 2 | true | Print "Iteration #2" | | 3 | 3 | true | Print "Iteration #3" | | 4 | 4 | true | Print "Iteration #4" | | 5 | 5 | true | Print "Iteration #5" | | 6 | 6 | false | **Loop terminates** | --- ## 2.2 The `for…in` Statement — Property Enumeration The `for…in` loop iterates over the **enumerable property names (keys)** of an object. Each iteration assigns the next property key to the loop variable. ### General Syntax ```javascript for (let key in object) { // body — access value via object[key] } ``` ### Operational Rules - Iterates over **own** and **inherited enumerable string-keyed properties**. - **Order is not guaranteed** by specification (though modern engines follow insertion order for string keys). - Should **not** be used to iterate over `Array` indices in production code — use `for…of` or `Array.prototype.forEach()` instead. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — `for…in` vs `for…of`** > - `for…in` → iterates **keys/properties** (objects) > - `for…of` → iterates **values** of iterable objects (Arrays, Strings, Maps, Sets) — *not in current syllabus but commonly confused in exams.* ### Worked Example ```javascript const student = { name: "Anu", age: 21, branch: "CSE" }; for (let key in student) { console.log(key + " → " + student[key]); } ``` **Output:** ``` name → Anu age → 21 branch → CSE ``` --- ## 2.3 The `while` Statement — Pre-Test (Entry-Controlled) Loop The `while` loop executes its body **as long as** a specified condition remains truthy. The condition is tested *before* every iteration. ### General Syntax ```javascript while (condition) { // body // update statement (must modify condition variable) } ``` ### Operational Rules - If the condition is **falsy on initial evaluation**, the body is **never executed**. - The programmer is **responsible** for updating the condition-controlling variable inside the body; otherwise, an **infinite loop** occurs. - Suitable for **indefinite iteration** — when the number of repetitions is not known in advance (e.g., reading user input until sentinel value). ### Worked Example ```javascript let n = 1; while (n <= 4) { console.log("n = " + n); n = n * 2; } ``` **Trace:** 1 → 2 → 4 → 8 (terminates as 8 > 4). --- ## 2.4 The `do…while` Statement — Post-Test (Exit-Controlled) Loop The `do…while` loop is structurally identical to `while`, **except the condition is evaluated *after* the body executes**. ### General Syntax ```javascript do { // body // update statement } while (condition); ``` ### Operational Rules - The body is executed **at least once**, regardless of the condition's initial value. - The **semicolon** after `while (condition)` is **mandatory** — this is a frequent KTU exam question. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting the trailing semicolon in `do…while` does *not* cause a syntax error in some linters, but it breaks ASI (Automatic Semicolon Insertion) consistency and may lead to subtle bugs. Always include it. ### Worked Example ```javascript let password; let attempt = 0; do { password = "test" + attempt; console.log("Trying: " + password); attempt++; } while (attempt < 3); ``` **Output:** ``` Trying: test0 Trying: test1 Trying: test2 ``` --- ## 2.5 The `break` Statement — Loop Termination `break` immediately **terminates the innermost enclosing loop** (or `switch`) and transfers control to the statement following the loop. ```javascript for (let i = 1; i <= 10; i++) { if (i === 6) break; // exits when i reaches 6 console.log(i); } // Output: 1 2 3 4 5 ``` ### Behavior in Nested Loops `break` only exits **one level** of nesting. To exit multiple levels, use a **labeled `break`** (KTU 2024 advanced topic): ```javascript outer: for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) { for (let j = 0; j < 3; j++) { if (i === 1 && j === 1) break outer; console.log(i + "," + j); } } ``` --- ## 2.6 The `continue` Statement — Skip Iteration `continue` **skips the remainder of the current iteration** and proceeds to the next iteration's condition evaluation (and update expression in `for` loops). ```javascript for (let i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { if (i === 3) continue; // skips printing 3 console.log(i); } // Output: 1 2 4 5 ``` --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Syntax Comparison Table | Feature | `for` | `for…in` | `while` | `do…while` | |---|---|---|---|---| | Header expressions | 3 | 1 | 0 (in header) | 0 (in header) | | Condition test point | Pre-iteration | Pre-iteration | Pre-iteration | **Post-iteration** | | Minimum executions | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** | | Ideal for | Counted loops | Object keys | Indefinite loops | Must-run-once logic | | Update responsibility | In header | Automatic | Inside body | Inside body | | Trailing semicolon | Not required | Not required | Not required | **Required** | | Companion `break` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Companion `continue` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | # 🧮 Step-by-Step Execution Analysis & Analytical Trace ## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace of a Nested Loop with `break` and `continue` ### Problem Statement Predict the output of the following JavaScript snippet (a classic KTU exam question): ```javascript for (let i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { for (let j = 1; j <= 3; j++) { if (i === j) continue; if (i + j === 4) break; console.log("i=" + i + " j=" + j); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Execution Trace **Iteration Block (i=1):** - j=1: `i===j` → `1===1` TRUE → `continue` (skip rest, go to j++) - j=2: `i===j` → FALSE; `i+j===4` → `1+2=3` FALSE → print `i=1 j=2` - j=3: `i===j` → FALSE; `i+j===4` → `1+3=4` TRUE → `break` (exit inner loop) **Iteration Block (i=2):** - j=1: `i===j` → FALSE; `i+j===4` → `2+1=3` FALSE → print `i=2 j=1` - j=2: `i===j` → `2===2` TRUE → `continue` (skip rest, go to j++) - j=3: `i===j` → FALSE; `i+j===4` → `2+3=5` FALSE → print `i=2 j=3` - (j increments to 4, condition false, inner loop ends) **Iteration Block (i=3):** - j=1: `i===j` → FALSE; `i+j===4` → `3+1=4` TRUE → `break` - (inner loop ends, no output for i=3) ### Final Output $$ \text{Output} = \{ \ (i=1,\ j=2),\ (i=2,\ j=1),\ (i=2,\ j=3) \} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Learning:** The `continue` skips ONLY the *current* inner-loop iteration. The `break` exits the *inner* loop only, not the outer `for`. This is a KTU favorite question. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace — `do…while` Guarantee ### Problem Statement Determine the output: ```javascript let x = 10; do { console.log("x = " + x); x--; } while (x < 5); ``` ### Step-by-Step Analysis - **Entry into `do` block:** No pre-condition check. - **Iteration 1:** `x=10` → print `x = 10` → `x--` makes `x=9` → check `9 < 5` → FALSE → **terminate**. **Final Output:** ``` x = 10 ``` > [!NOTE] > Even though the condition `x < 5` is never true, the `do…while` loop **executes its body exactly once** — this is the defining characteristic that distinguishes it from `while`. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Trace — `for…in` with Inherited Properties ```javascript const proto = { inheritedProp: "fromProto" }; const obj = Object.create(proto); obj.ownProp = "mine"; for (let key in obj) { console.log(key); } ``` ### Step-by-Step Analysis - The `for…in` loop iterates over **own + inherited enumerable string-keyed properties**. - `obj` has `ownProp` (own) and inherits `inheritedProp`. - Output: ``` ownProp inheritedProp ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Use `obj.hasOwnProperty(key)` inside `for…in` to filter only own properties. Otherwise, prototype pollution can cause bugs. --- ## 3.4 Mathematical Expression of Loop Iteration Count For a standard `for (let i = a; i < b; i++)` loop with step `k`, the number of iterations is: $$ N_{iter} = \max\left(0,\ \left\lceil \frac{b - a}{k} \right\rceil\right) $$ **Example derivation:** `for (let i = 0; i < 10; i += 2)` produces i = 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 → 5 iterations. $$ N_{iter} = \left\lceil \frac{10 - 0}{2} \right\rceil = 5 $$ **Example with negative range:** `for (let i = 5; i >= 1; i--)` produces 5 iterations. $$ N_{iter} = 5 - 1 + 1 = 5 $$ # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Execution Schematics ## 4.1 Master Comparison Flowchart — Selecting the Right Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Need to repeat code] --> B{Is iteration count known?} B -- Yes --> C{Need to iterate over object keys?} B -- No --> D{Must the body execute at least once?} C -- Yes --> E["Use: for...in"] C -- No --> F["Use: for"] D -- Yes --> G["Use: do...while"] D -- No --> H["Use: while"] E --> I[End] F --> I G --> I H --> I ``` > **Note on Mermaid safety:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`, etc.) and prefixed where needed (`nodeA`). No reserved keywords like `end` are used as IDs. Labels contain only clean text without markdown formatting. --- ## 4.2 Control Flow Diagram — `for` Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD startLoop[Start for Loop] --> initExp[Initialization Expression
Executed ONCE] initExp --> condTest{Condition
True or False?} condTest -- True --> bodyExec[Execute Loop Body] bodyExec --> updateExp[Update Expression
i++, i--, etc.] updateExp --> condTest condTest -- False --> exitLoop[Exit Loop
Continue after closing brace] ``` --- ## 4.3 Control Flow Diagram — `while` Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD startWhile[Start while Loop] --> condCheck{Condition
True or False?} condCheck -- True --> bodyRun[Execute Loop Body
Must include update logic] bodyRun --> condCheck condCheck -- False --> exitWhile[Exit Loop] ``` --- ## 4.4 Control Flow Diagram — `do…while` Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD startDo[Start do block] --> bodyExec[Execute Loop Body
Runs at least ONCE] bodyExec --> condCheck{Condition
True or False?} condCheck -- True --> bodyExec condCheck -- False --> exitDo[Exit Loop] ``` --- ## 4.5 Architecture Diagram — `break` vs `continue` Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TD loopStart[Loop Iteration Start] --> execBody[Execute Body Statements] execBody --> checkBreak{break
encountered?} checkBreak -- Yes --> exitOuter[Exit Loop Immediately] checkBreak -- No --> checkCont{continue
encountered?} checkCont -- Yes --> nextIter[Skip to Next Iteration
Update expression runs first in for] checkCont -- No --> nextIter2[Proceed Normally] nextIter --> loopStart nextIter2 --> loopStart exitOuter --> afterLoop[Code After Loop] ``` --- ## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Iterative Statement in a Web App Context ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Client["Browser / Frontend Layer"] UI[User Interface
HTML Form or DOM Event] end subgraph Logic["JavaScript Engine Layer"] EventHandler[Event Handler Triggered] LoopControl{Iterative Construct} LoopBody[Loop Body
DOM Update / Fetch / Validation] ModFlow[break or continue Modifiers] end subgraph Output["Output Layer"] Render[Rendered DOM] Console[Console Output] end UI --> EventHandler EventHandler --> LoopControl LoopControl --> LoopBody LoopBody --> ModFlow ModFlow -- continue --> LoopControl ModFlow -- break --> Render LoopBody --> Render LoopBody --> Console ``` # 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Differentiate between `while` and `do…while` loops in JavaScript. Provide a suitable code example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `while` | `do…while` | |---|---|---| | Type | Pre-test (entry-controlled) | Post-test (exit-controlled) | | Condition test | **Before** body execution | **After** body execution | | Minimum executions | 0 (body may not run even once) | 1 (body runs at least once) | | Trailing semicolon | Not required | **Required** | **Code Examples:** ```javascript // while loop — may not execute let x = 10; while (x < 5) { console.log(x); x++; } // Output: (nothing — condition false on entry) // do-while loop — executes once let y = 10; do { console.log(y); y++; } while (y < 5); // Output: 10 ``` --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Explain the purpose of the `for…in` loop in JavaScript. Why is it generally not recommended for iterating over arrays?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `for…in` loop iterates over the **enumerable property names (keys)** of an object, including inherited properties. It is designed for object property enumeration. ```javascript const car = { brand: "Tesla", model: "Model 3", year: 2024 }; for (let key in car) { console.log(key + " = " + car[key]); } ``` **Why not for arrays:** - It iterates over **indices as strings** ("0", "1", "2"), not numeric values. - It includes **inherited properties** from the Array prototype, which can cause unexpected behavior. - **Iteration order is not strictly guaranteed** by the specification. - Better alternatives: `for…of` (for values), `Array.prototype.forEach()`, or traditional `for` loop with numeric index. --- ## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the syntax and execution flow of the `for` loop in JavaScript with a suitable example. Include a trace table. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a JavaScript program using a `for` loop to find the sum of all even numbers between 1 and 100. Explain the logic. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Syntax:** ```javascript for (initialization; condition; update) { // body } ``` **Execution Flow Phases:** 1. **Initialization** — executed only once at the start. 2. **Condition Test** — evaluated before every iteration; if false → exit. 3. **Body Execution** — runs when condition is true. 4. **Update Expression** — runs at the end of every iteration. 5. Loop returns to step 2. **Example:** ```javascript for (let i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { console.log("Value: " + i); } ``` **Trace Table:** | Iteration | i | i <= 5 | Output | i++ after | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1 | true | Value: 1 | i = 2 | | 2 | 2 | true | Value: 2 | i = 3 | | 3 | 3 | true | Value: 3 | i = 4 | | 4 | 4 | true | Value: 4 | i = 5 | | 5 | 5 | true | Value: 5 | i = 6 | | 6 | 6 | false | (exit) | — | --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Problem:** Sum of all even numbers from 1 to 100. **Logic Explanation:** - Even numbers have the form `2, 4, 6, ..., 100`. - We initialize `sum = 0`. - Loop from `i = 2` to `i <= 100`, incrementing by 2 (so only even values are processed). - Add each `i` to `sum`. **Code:** ```javascript let sum = 0; for (let i = 2; i <= 100; i += 2) { sum = sum + i; } console.log("Sum = " + sum); ``` **Mathematical verification (Arithmetic Progression):** $$ S_n = \frac{n}{2}(a + l) $$ Where $a = 2$, $l = 100$, $n = 50$. $$ S = \frac{50}{2} \times (2 + 100) = 25 \times 102 = 2550 $$ **Output: `Sum = 2550`** ✅ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the `break` and `continue` statements in JavaScript with examples. How does a **labeled `break`** differ from an ordinary `break`? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Predict the output of the following JavaScript code and justify each line: **(7 marks)** ```javascript let result = ""; for (let i = 1; i <= 4; i++) { if (i === 3) continue; if (i === 4) break; result += i + " "; } console.log(result); ``` --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): **`break` Statement:** - Terminates the **innermost enclosing loop or switch** immediately. - Control transfers to the statement immediately after the loop. ```javascript for (let i = 1; i <= 10; i++) { if (i === 5) break; console.log(i); } // Output: 1 2 3 4 ``` **`continue` Statement:** - Skips the **remainder of the current iteration**. - Proceeds to the next iteration's condition test (and update in `for`). ```javascript for (let i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { if (i === 3) continue; console.log(i); } // Output: 1 2 4 5 ``` **Labeled `break` vs Ordinary `break`:** | Feature | Ordinary `break` | Labeled `break` | |---|---|---| | Scope | Exits the **innermost** loop only | Exits the **labeled** (outer) loop | | Syntax | `break;` | `break labelName;` | | Use case | Single-loop exit | Nested loop early termination | **Labeled Break Example:** ```javascript outer: for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) { for (let j = 0; j < 3; j++) { if (i === 1 && j === 1) break outer; console.log(i + "," + j); } } // Output: 0,0 0,1 0,2 1,0 1,1 // (terminates both loops at i=1, j=1) ``` --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Code under analysis:** ```javascript let result = ""; for (let i = 1; i <= 4; i++) { if (i === 3) continue; if (i === 4) break; result += i + " "; } console.log(result); ``` **Step-by-Step Trace:** | i | i === 3? | i === 4? | Action | result after | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | No | No | append `"1 "` | `"1 "` | | 2 | No | No | append `"2 "` | `"1 2 "` | | 3 | **Yes** | — | `continue` (skip rest) | `"1 2 "` (unchanged) | | 4 | No | **Yes** | `break` (exit loop) | `"1 2 "` (unchanged) | **Final Output:** ``` 1 2 ``` **Justification:** When `i = 3`, the `continue` statement skips the `result +=` and `if (i === 4)` lines, jumping directly to `i++` and the next condition test. When `i = 4`, the `break` terminates the loop before any further append occurs. Hence the result string contains only "1 2 ". --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing `for…in` with `for…of`** — they are not interchangeable. `for…in` is for object **keys**, not array values. > 2. **Forgetting the trailing semicolon in `do…while`** — the only loop where this is mandatory. > 3. **Assuming `break` exits all nested loops** — it only exits the innermost enclosing loop unless using a label. > 4. **Writing `i = i + 1` inside a `for` header that already has `i++`** — causes double-increment bugs. > 5. **Using `let` vs `var` confusion** — `let` provides block scope; `var` is function-scoped. The loop variable in a `for` header with `let` is not accessible outside the loop. > 6. **Forgetting the update statement in `while` loops** — leads to infinite loops and is a common reason for failing practical exams. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Iterative Statements in JavaScript** - ✅ **`for` loop** has three header expressions: **initialization; condition; update**. Use for **definite (counted)** iteration. - ✅ **`for…in`** iterates over **enumerable property keys** of an object. Best for objects, not arrays. - ✅ **`while`** is **pre-test**; the body may execute **zero** times if the condition is initially false. - ✅ **`do…while`** is **post-test**; the body executes **at least once**. **Trailing semicolon is mandatory**. - ✅ **`break`** exits the **innermost** loop immediately. Use **labeled `break`** for multi-level exit. - ✅ **`continue`** skips the rest of the **current iteration** and proceeds to the next. - ✅ Infinite loops are formed when the **update expression is missing** in `while` / `do…while` or when the condition never becomes false. - ✅ Iteration count formula for `for (let i = a; i < b; i += k)` is: $$ N_{iter} = \left\lceil \frac{b - a}{k} \right\rceil $$ - ✅ In **nested loops**, `break` / `continue` apply to the **innermost** loop only (unless labeled). - ✅ `let` inside the `for` header is **block-scoped** and not visible outside the loop. - ✅ **Modern best practice:** prefer `for…of` for arrays, `.forEach()` / `.map()` for functional iteration, and `for…in` strictly for object key enumeration.

topics.title: Introduction to jQuery: Syntax, Selectors, Events, Traversing, AJAX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to jQuery: Syntax, Selectors, Events, Traversing, AJAX # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Standard):** > jQuery is a **fast, lightweight, feature-rich client-side JavaScript library** that simplifies HTML Document Object Model (DOM) manipulation, event handling, animation, and Asynchronous JavaScript And XML (AJAX) interactions while abstracting away cross-browser inconsistencies through a uniform API. ## 1.1 What is jQuery? jQuery is essentially a single JavaScript file (`jquery.min.js`) that you include in your HTML page. Once loaded, it exposes the global function **`$`** (or equivalently `jQuery`) that lets you query the DOM using CSS-like selectors and chain operations on the matched elements. | Attribute | Specification | | :--- | :--- | | Initial Release | **August 2006** | | Original Author | **John Resig** | | Official Motto | *"Write less, do more"* | | Current Stable (3.x) | ~**30 KB minified** / ~**90 KB uncompressed** | | License | MIT License (Open Source) | | Latest Major Branch | **jQuery 3.7.x** (modern KTU 2024 references) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Remote Control" Imagine the HTML DOM as a **huge wall of light switches**, where every switch controls one bulb, fan, or appliance. Without jQuery (vanilla JavaScript), you have to: - Walk to each switch - Identify it by its physical position - Pull it down differently for every brand (IE, Chrome, Firefox quirks) **jQuery acts as a Universal Remote Control**: - You press one button labeled `"p"` → it toggles every paragraph light - You press `".menu"` → it controls every switch labeled "menu" - You press `"#login"` → it controls one specific switch labeled "login" - One remote works uniformly across all TV brands (cross-browser) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why jQuery in Modern Web Apps (KTU Context):** > Even with modern frameworks (React, Angular, Vue), jQuery remains heavily used in **legacy enterprise apps, WordPress ecosystem, plugin development, and progressive enhancement** scenarios. PECBT633 Module 4 specifically targets this pragmatic, industry-realistic web layer. ## 1.3 The Core jQuery Philosophy 1. **DOM Abstraction** — Hide browser-specific DOM API differences. 2. **Chainability** — Return the jQuery object from every method so you can chain. 3. **CSS-Style Selectors** — Reuse the selector knowledge students already have. 4. **Event Normalization** — One unified `event` object, regardless of browser. 5. **AJAX Simplification** — Replace 10+ lines of `XMLHttpRequest` boilerplate with one method call. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Whenever asked *"What is jQuery?"*, always mention the three keywords: **lightweight**, **"write less do more"**, and **cross-browser**. Examiners award marks for these exact phrases. --- ## 1.4 The jQuery Ecosystem Map - **jQuery Core** — DOM, events, AJAX (this module) - **jQuery UI** — Widgets, interactions, effects (sortable, draggable, datepicker) - **jQuery Mobile** — Touch-optimized mobile UI framework - **Sizzle** — The selector engine embedded inside jQuery - **Deferred / Promise Object** — For managing asynchronous chains (used by AJAX) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 jQuery Syntax — The Atomic Formula The **fundamental jQuery expression** is given by: $$ \text{jQuery Expression} \;=\; \$( \text{Selector} ).\text{Action}(\text{Optional Argument}) $$ Or in long form: $$ \text{jQuery Expression} \;=\; \text{jQuery}(\text{Selector}).\text{Action}(\text{Optional Argument}) $$ ### Step-by-step breakdown: | Token | Purpose | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `$` | Alias for the global `jQuery` function. Returns a jQuery object wrapping zero or more DOM nodes. | `$` | | `Selector` | CSS3-style query string used to find matching DOM elements. | `"div.menu"` | | `.Action()` | jQuery method that operates on the matched set. | `.hide()`, `.html("Hi")` | | `Argument` | Optional parameter (string, object, function). | `.css("color","red")` | ### Basic KTU Exam-Ready Program Skeleton ```html jQuery Demo

Hello

``` ## 2.2 Selectors — The Query Engine Selectors are categorized into the following functional families (this is the **KTU high-yield classification table**): | Category | Syntax Pattern | Returns | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **All / Universal** | `$("*")` | Every element in the DOM | | **Element / Type** | `$("p")`, `$("h1")` | All matching tag names | | **ID** | `$("#loginBtn")` | Element with that `id` (unique) | | **Class** | `$(".card")` | All elements with that class | | **Compound** | `$("div.highlight")` | `div` AND class `highlight` | | **Descendant** | `$("ul li")` | `li` anywhere inside `ul` | | **Child** | `$("ul > li")` | Direct children only | | **Sibling Adjacent** | `$("h1 + p")` | `p` immediately after `h1` | | **Sibling General** | `$("h1 ~ p")` | All `p` siblings after `h1` | | **Multiple** | `$("h1, h2, h3")` | Union of multiple selectors | | **First / Last** | `$("p:first")`, `$("p:last")` | First / last element | | **Even / Odd** | `$("tr:even")`, `$("tr:odd")` | Zero-indexed even / odd | | **Index (eq)** | `$("li:eq(2)")` | Element at index 2 | | **Greater / Less** | `$("li:gt(1)")`, `$("li:lt(1)")` | Index-based filtering | | **Not** | `$("p:not(.intro)")` | Excludes selector | | **Contains** | `$("p:contains('KTU')")` | Text-content filter | | **Has** | `$("div:has(p)")` | Has descendant matching | | **Empty** | `$(":empty")` | No children or text | | **Hidden / Visible** | `$(":hidden")`, `$(":visible")` | State filters | | **Attribute Exists** | `$("[href]")` | Has the attribute | | **Attribute Equals** | `$("[type='text']")` | Exact value match | | **Attribute Starts With** | `$("[href^='https']")` | Prefix match | | **Attribute Ends With** | `$("[href$='.pdf']")` | Suffix match | | **Attribute Contains** | `$("[href*='ktu']")` | Substring match | | **Form `:input`** | `$(":input")` | All form elements | | **`:checked`** | `$(":checked")` | Checked checkboxes/radios | | **`:selected`** | `$(":selected")` | Selected `

topics.title: Introduction to Bootstrap: Basics, Grids, Themes
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Bootstrap: Basics, Grids, Themes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Bootstrap** is a free, open-source, front-end CSS framework developed originally by Mark Otto and Jacob Thornton at Twitter in **2011**. It is the most popular HTML, CSS, and JavaScript framework for developing **responsive**, **mobile-first** web pages. Bootstrap provides a collection of pre-built CSS classes, JavaScript plugins, and typography rules that accelerate UI development by enforcing a consistent design language across browsers and devices. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PECBT633 - Modern Web Applications), Bootstrap is positioned as a **CSS Component Library / UI Toolkit** that abstracts the complexity of media queries, flexbox, and grid mathematics through semantic class names. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to demonstrate competency in *(a)* including Bootstrap in a project via CDN/local installation, *(b)* constructing layouts using the **12-column grid system**, and *(c)* customizing appearance using **themes, Bootstrap variables (Sass)**, or Bootswatch templates. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "IKEA Furniture Kit" Imagine you are building a house: - **Without Bootstrap:** You must cut wood, hammer nails, sand surfaces, paint walls, and weld hinges from scratch. (This is writing raw CSS for every margin, padding, media query, and hover state.) - **With Bootstrap:** You receive flat-pack furniture from IKEA — pre-measured planks, labeled screws (`.btn`, `.card`, `.row`), color swatches, and an instruction booklet. You assemble it, you don't manufacture it. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for exams:** A frequent 3-mark question is *"Why use Bootstrap?"*. The answer must always include three keywords: **Responsive**, **Mobile-First**, and **Cross-Browser Compatible**. KTU examiners award full marks only when these three appear. ### 1.3 Key Versions and Why They Matter | Version | Release Year | Key Change | Relevance | |---------|--------------|------------|-----------| | **Bootstrap 3** | 2013 | Introduced mobile-first philosophy | Legacy, KTU rarely asks | | **Bootstrap 4** | 2018 | Flexbox-based grid, Sass, rem units | Common in older labs | | **Bootstrap 5** | 2021 | **Dropped jQuery**, pure JS, RTL, offcanvas | **Current KTU standard** | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Note:** Bootstrap 5 removed **jQuery** entirely. If you write any jQuery syntax in a Bootstrap 5 answer, marks will be deducted. KTU 2024 expects answers aligned with **v5.x**. ### 1.4 Two Ways to Include Bootstrap 1. **CDN (Content Delivery Network) — Recommended for quick demos and exams** ```html ``` 2. **Local Download (npm / source files) — Recommended for production** ```bash npm install bootstrap ``` Then link from `node_modules/bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css`. > [!TIP] > **KTU Lab Tip:** Always use the CDN in your lab record. The `bootstrap.bundle.min.js` (not `bootstrap.min.js`) is required because it includes **Popper.js** for dropdowns and tooltips. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Bootstrap Architecture Bootstrap is logically divided into three technical layers: - **Layer 1 — Reboot.css (Normalization Layer):** A modern CSS reset that removes browser inconsistencies (default margins, line-heights, `box-sizing: border-box`). All Bootstrap components inherit this baseline. - **Layer 2 — Component Classes (Utility Layer):** Pre-styled class names such as `.btn`, `.alert`, `.navbar`, `.card`. Each class bundles dozens of CSS properties into a single semantic name. - **Layer 3 — JavaScript Plugins (Behaviour Layer):** Interactive components like **modals, dropdowns, carousels, offcanvas, tooltips**. These rely on Bootstrap's JS bundle and `data-bs-*` attributes. ### 2.2 The Bootstrap Container — The Root Box Every Bootstrap layout **must** begin with a container. Containers are the outermost wrappers that establish a **maximum width** and **horizontal centering**. - **`.container`** — Fixed-width responsive container. Width changes at each breakpoint. - **`.container-fluid`** — Spans the **full 100% width** of the viewport at all sizes. - **`.container-{breakpoint}`** — Becomes 100% wide *until* the specified breakpoint, then becomes fixed. ``` . Small (<576px) Medium (≥768px) Large (≥992px) X-Large (≥1200px) XXL (≥1400px) .container 100% 720px 960px 1140px 1320px .container-fluid 100% 100% 100% 100% 100% ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why containers matter:** Without a container, rows inside Bootstrap have no defined maximum width, and the content can stretch across ultra-wide monitors, ruining readability. ### 2.3 The 12-Column Grid System — The Heart of Bootstrap Bootstrap divides every row horizontally into **12 equal-width logical columns**. Every layout, no matter how complex, is constructed by summing column spans to exactly 12 (or less, with offsetting). #### 2.3.1 Grid Class Syntax The class name follows a strict structure: ``` col - {breakpoint} - {span} | | | | | └── Number of columns to occupy (1–12) | └──────────────── Device size (sm, md, lg, xl, xxl) └────────────────────────── "column" — marks a grid item ``` #### 2.3.2 Six Tiered Breakpoints | Class Infix | Breakpoint | Typical Device | Container Max-Width | |-------------|------------|----------------|---------------------| | (none) | < **576px** | Portrait phone | Auto (100%) | | `sm` | ≥ **576px** | Landscape phone | 540px | | `md` | ≥ **768px** | Tablet | 720px | | `lg` | ≥ **992px** | Desktop | 960px | | `xl` | ≥ **1200px** | Large desktop | 1140px | | `xxl` | ≥ **1400px** | Ultra-wide | 1320px | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The grid is **mobile-first**, meaning styles *cascade up*, not down. A `col-12 col-md-6` declaration means: full width on mobile, half width on medium and above. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question format. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Grid Mathematics The percentage width of any column span on a given device is calculated using this **canonical formula**: $$ \text{Column Width (\%)} = \left(\frac{\text{Column Span}}{12}\right) \times 100 $$ | Span | Calculation | Resulting Width | |------|-------------|-----------------| | 1 | (1/12) × 100 | **8.33%** | | 2 | (2/12) × 100 | **16.67%** | | 3 | (3/12) × 100 | **25.00%** | | 4 | (4/12) × 100 | **33.33%** | | 6 | (6/12) × 100 | **50.00%** | | 12 | (12/12) × 100 | **100.00%** | ### 2.5 Gutter Width The horizontal padding between columns is called a **gutter**. The default Bootstrap gutter is: $$ G_{default} = 24 \text{ px (1.5rem)} $$ This can be customized using the `.g-0`, `.g-1`, `.g-2`, ..., `.g-5` utility classes, where each unit equals **0.25rem (4px)**. ### 2.6 Bootstrap Themes — Customization Layer A "theme" in Bootstrap refers to a custom collection of **Sass variable overrides** or a pre-built **CSS skin**. The most popular ecosystem is **Bootswatch**, offering free themes (Cosmo, Flatly, Lux, Minty, Solar, etc.). **Theming approaches in increasing complexity:** 1. **CDN-only Theme (Easiest):** Replace the default CDN with a Bootswatch CDN: ```html ``` 2. **CSS Override Theme (Beginner):** Add your own ` ``` *Logic:* The default `.btn-primary` uses `$primary` (blue). By redefining the class with a hex code **dark green (#14532d)**, our rule overrides because of source-order priority. 3. **Sass Variable Customization (3 marks):** Modify variables in a custom SCSS file, then recompile. ```scss $primary: #14532d; $border-radius: 0.5rem; $font-family-base: 'Inter', sans-serif; @import "bootstrap/scss/bootstrap"; ``` *Logic:* The `$primary` variable controls button, link, and focus color globally. Recompiling produces a fully customized Bootstrap CSS bundle. **Comparison Table:** | Approach | Skill Level | Best For | Performance | |----------|-------------|----------|-------------| | CDN Theme | Beginner | Demos, prototypes | Same as default | | CSS Override | Intermediate | Small customizations | Adds ~few KB | | Sass Customization | Advanced | Production, brand consistency | Optimized build | --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas — Bootstrap Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting ``:** Without this tag, your responsive design *will not work on mobile*. Examiners explicitly test this — 1 to 2 marks lost. > 2. **Using `bootstrap.min.js` instead of `bootstrap.bundle.min.js`:** The bundle includes Popper.js, required for dropdowns/tooltips. Examiners will check the script tag. > 3. **Mentioning jQuery with Bootstrap 5:** Bootstrap 5 does NOT use jQuery. Using `$()` syntax is an instant 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Summing columns to more than 12 without using `offset`:** If your `col-md-8` + `col-md-6` = 14, the second column wraps. Examiners expect correct span math. > 5. **Confusing `container` with `container-fluid`:** Read the question carefully. "Full-width" = `container-fluid`. > 6. **Missing the closing `` for rows/containers:** Syntax errors cause layout collapse. Validate your HTML. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Bootstrap Basics, Grids, Themes** - **Bootstrap 5** is **mobile-first**, **jQuery-free**, and **Sass-based**. - Always include `` in ``. - Use the **CDN** for quick demos; the **bundle** script (not plain JS) is mandatory for components. - The **12-column grid** is the layout backbone. Always verify your column spans sum to **≤12** per row. - Formula: $$ W = \left(\frac{\text{span}}{12}\right) \times 100\% $$ - **Breakpoints** cascade upward: `col-12 col-md-6 col-lg-4` means full → half → one-third. - **Containers** come in three types: `.container` (responsive max-width), `.container-fluid` (100% always), `.container-{bp}` (100% up to breakpoint). - **Default gutter** = **24px (1.5rem)**; use `.g-0` to `.g-5` to adjust. - **Themes** can be applied via **Bootswatch CDN**, **CSS overrides**, or **Sass variable customization**. - The **`.row`** class uses **negative margins** to align flush with its parent container's padding. - **Offset classes** (`offset-md-2`) add left margin equal to `n` columns' worth of space. - **Order classes** (`order-md-2`) reorder columns visually using Flexbox `order`. - The `col-12 col-md-{span}` pattern is the **most-tested** Bootstrap construct in KTU exams. - **Six breakpoints** to memorize: **576, 768, 992, 1200, 1400** px (and the implicit 0px base). - Bootstrap components use `data-bs-*` attributes (e.g., `data-bs-toggle="modal"`) for JS behavior in v5. - KTU expects answers aligned with **Bootstrap 5** — avoid Bootstrap 3/4 syntax in 2024 scheme papers. - **Cross-browser compatibility**, **responsive design**, and **rapid prototyping** are the three biggest advantages to mention in any "Why Bootstrap?" question.

topics.title: Angular JS: Expressions, Modules, Data Binding, Scopes, Directives & Events, Controllers, Filters, Services, Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Angular JS: Expressions, Modules, Data Binding, Scopes, Directives & Events, Controllers, Filters, Services, Validation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of AngularJS ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **AngularJS** is a **structural, open-source JavaScript framework** maintained by Google, designed to extend HTML vocabulary for dynamic, single-page web applications (SPAs). It implements the **MVW (Model-View-Whatever) architectural pattern**, where the DOM is treated as the View, JavaScript objects as the Model, and the link between them (Controller / ViewModel) is the Whatever. It uses **declarative templates**, **two-way data binding**, **dependency injection**, and **directives** to decouple DOM manipulation from application logic. The framework operates by **bootstrapping** the application against a root HTML element (typically ``), after which it compiles directives and binds the runtime to the page lifecycle. | **Attribute** | **Specification** | |---|---| | **Initial Release** | **2010** (by Misko Hevery & Adam Abrons at Google) | | **Latest Stable Version** | **1.8.3** (LTS) | | **Language Core** | **JavaScript (ES5/ES6 compatible)** | | **Architecture** | **MVW (Model-View-Whatever)** | | **Module System** | **`angular.module()`** | | **Default Currency** | **$ (Dollar Symbol)** | | **Stable LTS Until** | **December 2021** (community forks continue) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Magic Translator of a Restaurant"** Imagine a **restaurant**: - The **Menu (HTML)** is static — it shows the layout (headings, tables, placeholders). - The **Chef's Recipe Book (Model / Data)** holds the actual ingredients (data). - The **Waiter (AngularJS)** constantly runs back and forth: when a customer (user) changes an order on the menu, the waiter informs the chef (Model → View update), and when the chef runs out of an ingredient, the waiter instantly updates the menu (Model → View synchronization). - The **Restaurant Manager (Controller)** decides the rules — "if vegetarian, suggest pasta." **AngularJS = The Waiter + The Manager + The Translation Rules.** It *binds* the static menu (HTML) to the live data (JavaScript objects) and keeps them magically in sync using **two-way data binding**. ## 1.3 Why AngularJS? — Core Design Goals 1. **Decouple DOM manipulation** from application logic. 2. **Extend HTML** with custom tags, attributes, and classes (directives). 3. **Bind data** bi-directionally between Model and View. 4. **Inject dependencies** automatically to improve testability. 5. **Modularize** large applications through `angular.module()`. 6. **Validate** user input declaratively using HTML attributes. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Although AngularJS (1.x) is officially deprecated in favor of Angular (2+), KTU PECBT633 includes it historically as the **conceptual foundation** for modern frameworks (React, Vue, Angular 17+). Examiners expect awareness of its architecture and terminology. --- ## 1.4 Core Building Blocks — At-a-Glance Map | **Building Block** | **Role in MVW** | **One-line Purpose** | |---|---|---| | **Expressions** | View-glue | Inline `{{ }}` syntax to output model data | | **Modules** | Container | Defines application boundaries & DI registration | | **Data Binding** | Sync | Auto-syncs Model ↔ View | | **Scopes** | Glue | Per-element execution context holding the Model | | **Directives** | Extenders | Augment HTML with custom behavior (`ng-model`, `ng-repeat`) | | **Events** | Interaction | Bind DOM events (`ng-click`, `ng-change`) | | **Controllers** | Logic | Houses presentation logic & binds to `$scope` | | **Filters** | Formatters | Transform displayed data (`currency`, `uppercase`) | | **Services** | Reusables | Singleton business logic (`$http`, `$location`) | | **Validation** | Integrity | Form-level input rules via `ng-model` + HTML5 | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The AngularJS Compilation & Runtime Pipeline When the browser loads an AngularJS page, the following lifecycle executes: 1. **Load Phase** — Browser fetches `angular.js` and HTML. 2. **Bootstrap Phase** — `ng-app` directive triggers `angular.bootstrap()`. 3. **Compile Phase** — Angular traverses the DOM, identifies directives, and produces a **linking function**. 4. **Link Phase** — The linking function attaches event listeners and registers watchers on the scope. 5. **Runtime Phase** — `$digest` cycle runs continuously to detect model mutations. 6. **Mutation Detection** — **Dirty-checking** compares `$$watchers`; if changed → DOM updates. 7. **Digest Cycle Limit** — Maximum of **10 iterations** per cycle (prevents infinite loops). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dirty-Checking Equation (Conceptual):** > > $$\text{Stable} \iff \forall w \in \text{Watchers},\; w.\text{getNewValue}() \equiv w.\text{getOldValue}()$$ > > If unstable after **10 iterations**, Angular throws: > **"$digest already in progress" / "$rootScope:infdig"** (Infinite Digest error). ## 2.2 High-Yield KTU Cheat Sheet ### 2.2.1 Expressions | **Aspect** | **Detail** | |---|---| | **Syntax** | `{{ expression }}` | | **Evaluation Context** | Current `$scope` of the element | | **Supported** | Variables, operators, function calls, filters, ternaries | | **NOT Supported** | `if/else`, `for/while`, declarations (`var`, `let`), comma operators | | **Example** | `{{ price * quantity | currency }}` | | **One-time binding** | `{{ ::user.name }}` — renders once, removes watcher | > [!NOTE] > **Anti-Pitfall:** Expressions are **NOT JavaScript**. They are parsed by Angular's own expression compiler (`$parse`) and run inside a sandboxed context that prohibits window/DOM access for security. ### 2.2.2 Modules | **Aspect** | **Detail** | |---|---| | **Getter Syntax** | `angular.module('myApp')` | | **Setter Syntax** | `angular.module('myApp', [])` — the `[]` defines dependencies | | **Common Dependencies** | `['ngRoute', 'ngAnimate', 'ui.router']` | | **Module Methods** | `.controller()`, `.service()`, `.factory()`, `.directive()`, `.filter()`, `.config()`, `.run()` | | **Bootstrap** | `` OR `angular.bootstrap(document, ['myApp'])` | ### 2.2.3 Data Binding | **Type** | **Syntax** | **Direction** | **Use Case** | |---|---|---|---| | **One-way Interpolation** | `{{ value }}` | Model → View | Displaying text | | **One-way (ng-bind)** | `` | Model → View | Avoid flicker (FOUC) | | **Two-way (ng-model)** | `` | Model ↔ View | Form inputs | | **One-time** | `{{ ::value }}` | Model → View (once) | Static optimization | ### 2.2.4 Scopes | **Property** | **Value** | |---|---| | **Definition** | An object that refers to the application Model | | **Hierarchy** | Nested scopes prototypally inherit from parents | | **Root** | `$rootScope` — accessible app-wide via `$injector` | | **Key Methods** | `$watch`, `$digest`, `$apply`, `$on`, `$broadcast`, `$emit` | | **Event Flow Down** | `$broadcast('eventName', data)` | | **Event Flow Up** | `$emit('eventName', data)` | | **Sibling-to-Sibling** | Use `$rootScope` or a shared service | ### 2.2.5 Directives (The Heart of AngularJS) > [!IMPORTANT] > A **directive** is a marker on a DOM element (`A`, `E`, `C`, `M` = attribute, element, class, comment) that tells Angular's HTML compiler (`$compile`) to attach specified behavior or transform the element/children. | **Built-in Directive** | **Purpose** | |---|---| | `ng-app` | Bootstrap root application | | `ng-init` | Initialize scope variables | | `ng-model` | Two-way bind input to scope | | `ng-bind` | One-way replace innerText | | `ng-repeat` | Iterate over a collection | | `ng-click`, `ng-dblclick` | Click event handlers | | `ng-mouseenter`, `ng-mouseleave` | Hover handlers | | `ng-change` | Fires on model change | | `ng-show` / `ng-hide` | Toggle `display: none` | | `ng-if` | Add/remove from DOM | | `ng-class` | Conditionally apply classes | | `ng-style` | Conditionally apply styles | | `ng-disabled`, `ng-readonly` | Boolean HTML attributes | | `ng-src`, `ng-href` | Safe binding of URLs | | `ng-include` | Inject partial templates | | `ng-view` | Route container (ngRoute) | | `ng-switch` | Conditional rendering block | ### 2.2.6 Events | **ng-Event** | **Native DOM Equivalent** | |---|---| | `ng-click` | `onclick` | | `ng-dblclick` | `ondblclick` | | `ng-mousedown`/`up` | `onmousedown`/`onmouseup` | | `ng-mouseenter`/`leave`/`move`/`over`/`out` | Same | | `ng-keydown`/`up`/`press` | Same | | `ng-focus`/`blur` | Same | | `ng-change` | `onchange` | | `ng-submit` | `onsubmit` | | `ng-copy`/`cut`/`paste` | Same | | `ng-drag`/`drop` | (HTML5 drag events) | ### 2.2.7 Controllers | **Aspect** | **Detail** | |---|---| | **Purpose** | Initialize scope and add presentation behavior | | **Registration** | `module.controller('Name', function($scope){...})` | | **DI Annotations** | String array `['$scope', function(s){}]` (minification-safe) | | **Modern Syntax** | `controller('Name', CtrlFn)` + `CtrlFn.$inject = ['$scope']` | | **DI in Detail** | Arrays preferred — string-injection is minification-unsafe | ### 2.2.8 Filters | **Built-in Filter** | **Syntax Example** | **Effect** | |---|---|---| | `currency` | `{{ price | currency:'₹' }}` | Adds currency symbol | | `uppercase` | `{{ name | uppercase }}` | ALL CAPS | | `lowercase` | `{{ name | lowercase }}` | all lowercase | | `number` | `{{ 3.14159 | number:2 }}` | 2 decimal places | | `date` | `{{ d | date:'dd/MM/yyyy' }}` | Date formatting | | `limitTo` | `{{ list | limitTo:5 }}` | Truncate array/string | | `orderBy` | `{{ list | orderBy:'age' }}` | Sort | | `filter` | `{{ list | filter:query }}` | Search | | `json` | `{{ obj | json }}` | Pretty-print | **Chaining Rule:** `{{ value | filter1 | filter2:arg }}` — left-to-right pipeline. ### 2.2.9 Services | **Built-in Service** | **Purpose** | |---|---| | `$http` | AJAX/REST client | | `$location` | URL parsing/rewriting | | `$route` / `$routeParams` | Client-side routing | | `$timeout` / `$interval` | Timers (digest-aware) | | `$q` | Promises (Angular's implementation) | | `$log` | Console wrapper | | `$sce` | Strict Contextual Escaping (security) | | `$rootScope` | Global scope singleton | | `$injector` | DI container | | `$animate` | CSS/JS animations | | `$cookies` / `$cookieStore` | Browser cookies | | `$resource` | RESTful higher-level wrapper | | `$sanitize` | HTML sanitization | **Custom Service Creation (3 patterns):** | **Pattern** | **Syntax** | **When to Use** | |---|---|---| | **`service()`** | `module.service('Name', CtorFn)` | Use `this` properties | | **`factory()`** | `module.factory('Name', fn)` | Return an object/value | | **`provider()`** | `module.provider('Name', fn)` | Configurable globally in `.config()` | ### 2.2.10 Validation | **Form Property** | **Meaning** | |---|---| | `$pristine` | User has not interacted yet | | `$dirty` | User has interacted | | `$valid` | All fields pass validation | | `$invalid` | At least one field fails | | `$submitted` | Form has been submitted | | `$error` | Object of failed validators per rule | | `$touched` | Field has lost focus | | `$untouched` | Field has not lost focus | **Built-in HTML5 + Angular Validators:** `required`, `minlength`, `maxlength`, `min`, `max`, `pattern` (regex), `email`, `number`, `url`, `date`. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Enterprise dashboards** — Real-time data binding powers monitoring UIs. - **E-commerce** — Dynamic forms, cart updates via `ng-model`. - **CMS / Admin Panels** — Form validation, CRUD via `$http`. - **SPA Migration** — AngularJS enabled the **first wave of SPAs** (Gmail, etc.) before React/Vue. > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** Expect at least one question that asks you to **list & explain 5 directives** or **distinguish between `service()` and `factory()`**. Memorize the cheat sheet above. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 End-to-End Working Application: "Student Registration System" The following example consolidates **all 10 building blocks** into one runnable application. Each block is annotated with its concept label. ### 3.1.1 Application Skeleton (HTML) ```html Student Registration

{{ ctrl.title }}

Welcome, {{ ctrl.student.name }}!

One-time display: {{ ::ctrl.student.name }}

Required!

Registered Students

  • {{ s.name | uppercase }} - {{ s.branch }} - Fee: {{ s.fee | currency:'₹' }}
``` ### 3.1.2 Application Logic (app.js) ```javascript // MODULE: Application container angular.module('studentApp', []) // CONTROLLER: Presentation logic + SCOPE binding .controller('StudentController', function($scope, $http) { var vm = this; vm.title = 'Student Registration Portal'; vm.student = { name: '', email: '', age: null, branch: '', fee: 0 }; vm.branches = ['CSE', 'ECE', 'ME', 'CE']; vm.students = []; // SCOPE.WATCH: Reactive computation $scope.$watch('ctrl.student.branch', function(newVal) { vm.student.fee = (newVal === 'CSE') ? 100000 : 75000; }); // SERVICE: $http for REST API call vm.submit = function(form) { if (form.$valid) { $http.post('/api/students', vm.student) .then(function(response) { vm.students.push(response.data); vm.student = {}; }); } }; }) // SERVICE: Custom factory for fee calculation .factory('FeeService', function() { return { calculate: function(branch) { var base = (branch === 'CSE') ? 100000 : 75000; return { base: base, gst: base * 0.18, total: base * 1.18 }; } }; }) // FILTER: Custom mark-grade filter .filter('grade', function() { return function(marks) { if (marks >= 90) return 'A+'; if (marks >= 80) return 'A'; if (marks >= 70) return 'B'; if (marks >= 60) return 'C'; return 'F'; }; }); ``` ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-through ### Step 1 — Bootstrap The browser parses the HTML and finds ``. Angular's compiler: 1. Detects `ng-app`. 2. Calls `angular.injector(['ng', 'studentApp'])`. 3. Creates the **injector**, **$rootScope**, and **$compile service**. ### Step 2 — Compile & Link `$compile` walks the DOM: $$ \text{DOM} \xrightarrow{\text{compile}} \text{Compile Fn} \xrightarrow{\text{link}} \text{Active DOM with watchers} $$ For each directive, Angular generates a **linking function** and registers watchers on `$scope`. ### Step 3 — Two-way Binding Activation (`ng-model`) `ng-model` registers **two watchers**: - Watcher A: scope → element (Model → View) - Watcher B: element event listener → scope (View → Model) $$ \text{View} \xrightarrow{\text{input event}} \text{ViewValue} \xrightarrow{\text{parser}} \text{Model} \xrightarrow{\text{formatter}} \text{ViewValue} \xrightarrow{\text{update}} \text{DOM} $$ ### Step 4 — Digest Cycle Every time `$apply()` is called (e.g., on `ng-click`): $$ \text{Digest Cycle} \Rightarrow \text{Traverse all watchers} \Rightarrow \text{Compare old vs new} \Rightarrow \text{Trigger listeners if changed} $$ Maximum iterations: $$ N_{\text{max}} = 10 $$ If unstable after 10 iterations → `$rootScope:infdig` thrown. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Feature** | **`service()`** | **`factory()`** | **`provider()`** | |---|---|---|---| | **Returns** | Singleton of `this` | Whatever the function returns | Whatever `$get` returns | | **Configurable** | No | No | Yes (via `.config()`) | | **Syntax** | `service('x', function(){this.y=1})` | `factory('x', function(){return {y:1}})` | `provider('x', function(){this.$get=function(){return {y:1}}})` | | **Use Case** | Stateful methods | Pure object/closure | Reusable library config | | **Event** | **Direction** | **Stops at** | |---|---|---| | `$broadcast` | Parent → Children | Receiving child scope | | `$emit` | Child → Ancestors | Root scope | | `$on` | Listener | (handler scope) | | **ng-repeat vs ng-options** | **ng-repeat** | **ng-options** | |---|---|---| | Speed | Slower for large lists | Faster (optimized) | | Use Case | Any HTML structure | `

Hello, !

Length: {{ username.length }}

``` ```javascript angular.module('bindApp', []) .controller('BindCtrl', function($scope) { $scope.username = ''; }); ``` **Explanation:** - When the user types in the input, the `input` event fires. - `ng-model` updates `$scope.username` (View → Model). - During the next digest cycle, `{{ username }}` and `ng-bind` re-evaluate (Model → View). **One-time Binding (`::`):** ```html

Welcome, {{ ::username }}!

``` After first render, the **watcher is removed** from the digest cycle, providing: - **Performance gain** in large lists (e.g., `{{ ::item.name }}` inside `ng-repeat`). - **Memory efficiency** — fewer watchers = faster digest. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting that `::` only works for **simple expressions**. If the value later changes, the binding will **not** update — by design. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > > ### **Question B (14 Marks)** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **scopes** in AngularJS. Describe the **scope hierarchy**, the **digest cycle**, and the difference between `$broadcast` and `$emit` with examples. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** What are **filters** and **services** in AngularJS? Write a custom filter and a custom service (using `factory()`) demonstrating their use. Also explain the differences between `service()`, `factory()`, and `provider()`. ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### (a) Scopes, Hierarchy, Digest, and Events **Scope Definition:** A `$scope` is a JavaScript object that holds the **Model** for a particular part of the UI. It is the **execution context** for expressions. **Scope Hierarchy:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A["$rootScope (app-wide)"] --> B["ParentCtrl $scope"] A --> C["OtherCtrl $scope"] B --> D["ngRepeat child scope"] B --> E["ngIf child scope"] D --> F["item scope (per iteration)"] style A fill:#1976d2,color:#fff style B fill:#388e3c,color:#fff style C fill:#388e3c,color:#fff style D fill:#f57c00,color:#fff style E fill:#f57c00,color:#fff style F fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff ``` Child scopes **prototypally inherit** from their parents. **Digest Cycle:** $$ \text{Trigger} \rightarrow \text{Dirty-check watchers} \rightarrow \text{Update DOM} \rightarrow \text{Repeat up to 10 times} $$ **`$broadcast` vs `$emit`:** | **Method** | **Direction** | **Stops At** | **Use Case** | |---|---|---|---| | `$broadcast` | Parent → Children | Receiving child | "Refresh list" from parent | | `$emit` | Child → Ancestors | `$rootScope` | "Item deleted" from list | **Example:** ```javascript // Parent controller $scope.$broadcast('refreshList', { id: 5 }); // Child controller $scope.$on('refreshList', function(event, data) { console.log('Refresh for', data.id); }); ``` ```javascript // Child scope $scope.$emit('itemDeleted', { id: 12 }); // Parent scope $scope.$on('itemDeleted', function(e, data) { $scope.totalItems--; }); ``` #### (b) Custom Filter and Custom Service **Custom Filter: `grade` (converts marks to letter grade)** ```javascript angular.module('app', []) .filter('grade', function() { return function(marks) { if (marks >= 90) return 'A+'; if (marks >= 75) return 'A'; if (marks >= 60) return 'B'; if (marks >= 50) return 'C'; return 'F'; }; }); ``` **Usage in HTML:** ```html {{ student.marks | grade }} ``` **Custom Service using `factory()` — `FeeService`:** ```javascript angular.module('app') .factory('FeeService', function() { var api = { calculate: function(branch) { var base = (branch === 'CSE') ? 100000 : 75000; return { base: base, gst: base * 0.18, total: base * 1.18 }; } }; return api; }); ``` **Usage in controller (via DI):** ```javascript .controller('FeeCtrl', ['$scope', 'FeeService', function($scope, FeeService) { $scope.fee = FeeService.calculate('CSE'); }]); ``` **Differences between `service()`, `factory()`, `provider()`:** | **Aspect** | **`service()`** | **`factory()`** | **`provider()`** | |---|---|---|---| | Receives | Constructor function | Factory function | Provider function with `$get` | | Returns | `new` instance with `this` properties | Whatever function returns | Whatever `$get` returns | | Configurable | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes via `module.config()` | | Complexity | Low | Medium | High | | When to use | Stateful services | Object/closure services | Reusable configurable modules | ```javascript // provider() with config angular.module('app', []) .provider('apiBase', function() { var baseUrl = 'http://default.com'; this.setBaseUrl = function(url) { baseUrl = url; }; this.$get = function() { return { url: baseUrl }; }; }) .config(function(apiBaseProvider) { apiBaseProvider.setBaseUrl('https://api.ktu.ac.in'); }); ``` > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall — Examiner's Warning:** > 1. Confusing `service()` and `factory()`: `service` is invoked with `new`; `factory` is invoked as a normal function whose return value is used. > 2. Forgetting that `provider()` is the **only** type that can be configured in `.config()`. > 3. Writing `ng-model` without `ng-app` ancestor → no binding occurs. > 4. Using `{{ }}` for binding in `` instead of `ng-model` — only works for display, not input. > 5. Modifying `$scope` from outside Angular (e.g., jQuery) without `$apply` → silent failure. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Save This for Exam Eve):** - **AngularJS** is a **JavaScript MVW framework** that extends HTML with directives. - **Expression** = `{{ }}` syntax evaluated on `$scope`. **NOT JavaScript**. - **One-time binding** = `{{ ::value }}` removes the watcher after first render. - **Module** = `angular.module('name', [deps])` registers all components. - **Two-way binding** uses `ng-model`; **one-way display** uses `{{ }}` or `ng-bind`. - **Scope hierarchy** is prototypal; child inherits from parent up to `$rootScope`. - **Digest cycle** has a hard limit of **10 iterations**; uses dirty-checking on watchers. - **Directives** are markers (`A`, `E`, `C`, `M`); `ng-app`, `ng-model`, `ng-repeat`, `ng-show`, `ng-hide`, `ng-click`, `ng-change`, `ng-class`, `ng-style` are the most common. - **Events** use `ng-click`, `ng-change`, etc. — DOM events are wrapped for digest triggering. - **Controllers** attach presentation logic to `$scope`; prefer `controller as` syntax in modern code. - **Filters** transform displayed data; chainable with `|` operator. - **Services** are **singletons** created by `service()`, `factory()`, or `provider()`. - **Validation** uses `NgModelController` properties: `$pristine`, `$dirty`, `$valid`, `$invalid`, `$touched`, `$error`. - **Custom validators** are added via `ngModel.$validators.name` inside a directive. - **`$broadcast`** = parent-to-child; **`$emit`** = child-to-ancestor. - **DI** is minification-unsafe with plain function arguments — use array annotation. - **AngularJS 1.x** is in **LTS** (legacy) — KTU includes it for foundational concepts. - **Modern Angular (2+)** is a complete rewrite in TypeScript, NOT a version upgrade of AngularJS. > **End of Module 4 Notes — PECBT633 Modern Web Applications**

topics.title: Back End Technologies: Introduction to RESTful Services, Resources, Messages (Request, Response), Addressing, Methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT633 - MODERN WEB APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Back End Technologies: Introduction to RESTful Services, Resources, Messages (Request, Response), Addressing, Methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of RESTful Services > [!IMPORTANT] > **REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an **architectural style** for distributed hypermedia systems, formally defined by **Roy Fielding** in his doctoral dissertation (2000). A service that conforms to the REST constraints is called a **RESTful Web Service** (or RESTful API). A **RESTful Web Service** exposes business data and functionality as **resources** that are identified by **URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers)** and manipulated via standard **HTTP methods**. The state of a resource is transferred between client and server in a standardized **representation** (typically **JSON** or **XML**). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** RESTful services are the backbone of modern back-end communication. They are **language-agnostic**, **platform-independent**, and operate over standard **HTTP/HTTPS** protocols on **port 80/443**. ### Key Terms at a Glance | Term | One-Line Definition | |---|---| | **Resource** | A named conceptual entity (e.g., a user, an order, a product). | | **URI** | The unique address that identifies a resource. | | **Representation** | The current state of a resource (JSON, XML, HTML). | | **Message** | The HTTP request or response exchanged between client and server. | | **Method** | The HTTP verb (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) describing the action. | | **Statelessness** | Each request contains all information needed; server holds no client session. | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍽️ Imagine a **restaurant**: - **You (the Client)** sit at a table and want food. - **The Kitchen (the Server)** has all the food (resources). - You don't go into the kitchen yourself. Instead, you give your order to a **waiter (the REST API)**. - The waiter takes your **order slip (HTTP Request)** to the kitchen. - The kitchen prepares the dish and the waiter brings back the **plate of food (HTTP Response with representation)**. - You never need to know *how* the kitchen cooked the meal — you only know the **menu items (URIs)** and the **standard actions** (place order, cancel order, modify order). > [!TIP] > **Mapping the Analogy to REST** > > | Restaurant | REST Equivalent | > |---|---| > | Menu Card | URI List (Resources) | > | Order Slip | HTTP Request (Method + URI + Body) | > | Dish Served | HTTP Response (Status + Representation) | > | Waiter | RESTful API Layer | > | Kitchen | Back-end Server / Database | > | You | Client Application (Browser, Mobile App) | **Why is this design powerful?** Because the waiter speaks a *standard language* that every customer understands — just like HTTP is a standard protocol understood by every client.
