The customer can be from any country, and the kitchen can be from any culture. --- ## 1.3 Why RESTful Services? (Engineering Motivation) > [!IMPORTANT] > RESTful services are the **de-facto industry standard** for building scalable, loosely-coupled web APIs. They are used by **Google, Amazon, Facebook, Twitter, GitHub, and virtually every modern web/mobile application**. ### Core Advantages 1. **Statelessness** → High scalability (any server can handle any request). 2. **Uniform Interface** → Simpler client code, decoupled from server internals. 3. **Cacheability** → Improved performance and reduced server load. 4. **Layered System** → Load balancers, proxies, and CDNs can be inserted transparently. 5. **Standard HTTP** → Reuses existing infrastructure (routers, firewalls, monitoring). 6. **Lightweight Representations** → JSON is far smaller than legacy XML/SOAP payloads. ### SOAP vs REST vs GraphQL (High-Level Comparison) > [!NOTE] > This is a **favourite KTU comparison question**. Memorize the distinctions below. | Feature | SOAP | REST | GraphQL | |---|---|---|---| | **Type** | Protocol | Architectural Style | Query Language | | **Data Format** | XML only | JSON, XML, HTML, Plain | JSON only | | **Transport** | HTTP, SMTP, JMS | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP/HTTPS | | **Statefulness** | Stateful or Stateless | Stateless | Stateless | | **Bandwidth** | Heavy | Lightweight | Lightweight (single endpoint) | | **Contract** | WSDL required | No formal contract | Schema (SDL) optional | | **Error Handling** | SOAP Faults | HTTP Status Codes | Single 200 OK with errors array | | **KTU Use Case** | Enterprise / Banking | Modern Web APIs | Mobile apps, complex nested data | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The 6 REST Architectural Constraints (Fielding's Dissertation) > [!IMPORTANT] > These 6 constraints define what makes a service **truly RESTful**. Examiners frequently ask students to list and explain them. **Memorize all six.** | # | Constraint | Meaning | Engineering Benefit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Client–Server** | Separation of UI (client) from data storage (server). | Independent evolution of front-end and back-end. | | 2 | **Stateless** | Each request is independent; server stores no client context. | Scalability, simplicity, fault-tolerance. | | 3 | **Cacheable** | Responses must declare themselves cacheable or non-cacheable. | Reduced latency, lower server load. | | 4 | **Uniform Interface** | Standardized resource identification, manipulation, and self-descriptive messages. | Decoupling, simplicity, visibility. | | 5 | **Layered System** | Client cannot tell if it is talking to the end server or an intermediary. | Security, load balancing, legacy encapsulation. | | 6 | **Code on Demand (Optional)** | Server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) to the client. | Extensibility, reduced client complexity. | ### The 4 Sub-Constraints of *Uniform Interface* This is a **sub-topic that often appears in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions**: 1. **Identification of Resources** — Each resource has a unique URI. 2. **Manipulation of Resources through Representations** — Client receives a representation (JSON) and uses it to modify the resource. 3. **Self-Descriptive Messages** — Each message contains enough information to describe how to process it (e.g., `Content-Type: application/json`). 4. **Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State (HATEOAS)** — Responses contain links to related resources, guiding the client. --- ## 2.2 Anatomy of an HTTP Request Message > [!NOTE] > Understanding the **exact structure** of an HTTP message is essential for KTU 14-mark questions. An HTTP **Request** consists of: ``` ``` ### 2.2.1 Example HTTP Request ``` POST /api/v1/students/2045/enroll HTTP/1.1 Host: www.ktu-university.ac.in User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0) Accept: application/json Content-Type: application/json Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9... Content-Length: 78 Connection: keep-alive { "courseId": "CST301", "semester": 4, "academicYear": "2024-2025" } ``` ### 2.2.2 Component Breakdown | Component | Example | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Request Line** | `POST /api/v1/students/2045/enroll HTTP/1.1` | Method + URI + HTTP version | | **Host Header** | `Host: www.ktu-university.ac.in` | Domain of the server | | **Accept Header** | `Accept: application/json` | Desired response format | | **Content-Type** | `Content-Type: application/json` | Format of the request body | | **Authorization** | `Authorization: Bearer ` | Authentication credentials | | **Body** | `{"courseId": "CST301", ...}` | The actual data (for POST/PUT/PATCH) | --- ## 2.3 Anatomy of an HTTP Response Message ### 2.3.1 Example HTTP Response ``` HTTP/1.1 201 Created Date: Mon, 12 Aug 2024 10:30:00 GMT Server: Apache/2.4.52 (Ubuntu) Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8 Content-Length: 142 Location: /api/v1/students/2045/enrollments/887 Cache-Control: no-store { "enrollmentId": 887, "studentId": 2045, "courseId": "CST301", "semester": 4, "status": "CONFIRMED", "enrolledOn": "2024-08-12T10:30:00Z" } ``` ### 2.3.2 Component Breakdown | Component | Example | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Status Line** | `HTTP/1.1 201 Created` | HTTP version + status code + reason phrase | | **Date** | `Date: Mon, 12 Aug 2024 ...` | When the response was generated | | **Content-Type** | `Content-Type: application/json` | Format of the response body | | **Location** | `Location: /api/v1/.../enrollments/887` | URI of the newly created resource | | **Body** | `{"enrollmentId": 887, ...}` | The representation of the resource | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield HTTP Status Code Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiners love status-code questions.** A student who confuses `200 OK` with `201 Created` loses easy marks. Memorize the five categories below. | Category | Range | Meaning | Common Codes You MUST Know | |---|---|---|---| | **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Request received, continuing process | `100 Continue` | | **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request successfully received & accepted | `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content` | | **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action needed (e.g., redirect) | `301 Moved Permanently`, `304 Not Modified` | | **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Malformed request / unauthorized | `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `403 Forbidden`, `404 Not Found`, `405 Method Not Allowed`, `409 Conflict` | | **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfill valid request | `500 Internal Server Error`, `502 Bad Gateway`, `503 Service Unavailable` | ### Critical Status Code Mappings for REST Methods | HTTP Method | Typical Success Codes | Typical Failure Codes | |---|---|---| | `GET` | `200 OK` | `404 Not Found` | | `POST` (Create) | `201 Created` (+ `Location` header) | `400`, `409` | | `PUT` (Full Update) | `200 OK` or `204 No Content` | `404`, `400` | | `PATCH` (Partial Update) | `200 OK` | `404`, `400` | | `DELETE` | `204 No Content` or `200 OK` | `404`, `403` | --- ## 2.5 HTTP Methods — The "CRUD Verb" Deep Dive > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the four primary HTTP methods with their idempotency and safety properties."* This is a **14-mark question** waiting to happen. | Method | CRUD | Idempotent? | Safe? | Request Body? | Response Body? | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **GET** | Read | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Retrieve resource(s) | | **POST** | Create | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Create new resource / trigger action | | **PUT** | Update (Full) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Optional | Replace entire resource | | **PATCH** | Update (Partial) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Modify specific fields | | **DELETE** | Delete | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | Optional | Remove a resource | | **HEAD** | Read (headers only) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | Check if resource exists | | **OPTIONS** | Meta | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Discover allowed methods (CORS) | ### Definitions of the Two Critical Properties > [!NOTE] > **Idempotent:** Performing the same operation multiple times produces the **same result** on the server state. > > **Safe:** The operation **does not modify** the server state (read-only). **Examples for clarity:** - `GET /students/2045` called 100 times → Server still has the same student → **Safe & Idempotent** ✅ - `PUT /students/2045` called 100 times with the same body → Student data is the same → **Idempotent** (not safe) ⚠️ - `POST /students` called 100 times with the same body → Creates 100 different students (different IDs) → **Neither safe nor idempotent** ❌ - `DELETE /students/2045` called 100 times → First deletes; rest return 404 but state is unchanged → **Idempotent** (not safe) ⚠️ --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Resource Identification & URI Design (Step-by-Step) **Problem:** Design RESTful URIs for a university's *Student Management* subsystem. ### Step 1 — Identify the Nouns (Resources) Resources are **nouns**, not verbs. Strip away all action words from the requirements. > Common KTU trap: Students write URIs like `/getStudent` or `/createStudent`. **This is wrong** — the verb belongs in the HTTP method, not the URI. ### Step 2 — Build a Resource Hierarchy Use a **collection → item** pattern: | # | Resource Type | URI Pattern | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Collection** | `/api/v1/students` | List all students | | 2 | **Item** | `/api/v1/students/{id}` | One specific student | | 3 | **Sub-collection** | `/api/v1/students/{id}/courses` | Courses of one student | | 4 | **Sub-item** | `/api/v1/students/{id}/courses/{cid}` | One specific course of one student | | 5 | **Filter / Search** | `/api/v1/students?dept=CSE&sem=4` | Filtered collection | ### Step 3 — Apply REST URI Design Rules 1. **Use plural nouns** → `/students` not `/student`. 2. **Use lowercase** → `/students/2045` not `/Students/2045`. 3. **Use hyphens, not underscores** → `/academic-records` not `/academic_records`. 4. **No file extensions in URI** → `/students/2045` not `/students/2045.json` (use `Accept` header instead). 5. **Version the API** → `/api/v1/...` (allows future `/api/v2/...`). 6. **Use path variables for hierarchy** and **query parameters for filtering**. ### Step 4 — Final URI Catalog for the Student Management API ``` GET /api/v1/students → List students POST /api/v1/students → Create new student GET /api/v1/students/{id} → Retrieve a student PUT /api/v1/students/{id} → Update entire student record PATCH /api/v1/students/{id} → Update partial student record DELETE /api/v1/students/{id} → Remove a student GET /api/v1/students/{id}/courses → List courses of a student POST /api/v1/students/{id}/courses → Enroll student in a course GET /api/v1/departments GET /api/v1/departments/{code}/students ``` --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Complete CRUD Request/Response Cycle > [!TIP] > **Worked Example Scenario:** A mobile app for KTU students manages course enrollments. Walk through the full lifecycle of enrolling a student in a course. ### Step 1 — `GET` Request: View Available Courses **Request:** ``` GET /api/v1/courses?semester=4&department=CSE HTTP/1.1 Host: api.ktuapp.in Accept: application/json Authorization: Bearer abc123token ``` **Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: application/json ETag: "courses-v3-2024" Cache-Control: max-age=300 ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"courses": [} \\ & \quad \left\{ \text{"id": "CST301", "title": "Operating Systems", "credits": 4\right\}, \\ & \quad \left\{ \text{"id": "CST303", "title": "DBMS", "credits": 4\right\}, \\ & \quad \left\{ \text{"id": "CST305", "title": "Web Technologies", "credits": 3\right\} \\ & \left. \right] \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** The client requests the catalogue. The server returns `200 OK` with a JSON array. The `Cache-Control: max-age=300` tells the client it may cache this for 5 minutes — fulfilling the **Cacheable** constraint. ### Step 2 — `POST` Request: Enroll in a Course **Request:** ``` POST /api/v1/students/2045/enrollments HTTP/1.1 Host: api.ktuapp.in Content-Type: application/json Authorization: Bearer abc123token ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"courseId": "CST301",} \\ & \text{"semester": 4,} \\ & \text{"academicYear": "2024-2025"} \\ & \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ **Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 201 Created Location: /api/v1/students/2045/enrollments/887 Content-Type: application/json ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"enrollmentId": 887,} \\ & \text{"studentId": 2045,} \\ & \text{"courseId": "CST301",} \\ & \text{"status": "CONFIRMED",} \\ & \text{"enrolledOn": "2024-08-12T10:30:00Z"} \\ & \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** A **new** resource (enrollment #887) is created. The server returns `201 Created` (not `200 OK` — this is a KTU favourite distinction) and includes the new resource's URI in the `Location` header. **Idempotency is NOT guaranteed** — calling this twice would create two enrollments. ### Step 3 — `PUT` Request: Update Grade **Request:** ``` PUT /api/v1/students/2045/enrollments/887 HTTP/1.1 Host: api.ktuapp.in Content-Type: application/json ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"courseId": "CST301",} \\ & \text{"semester": 4,} \\ & \text{"academicYear": "2024-2025",} \\ & \text{"status": "COMPLETED",} \\ & \text{"grade": "A"} \\ & \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ **Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: application/json ``` **Explanation:** The full enrollment resource is replaced. Calling `PUT` again with the **same body** yields the same final state — therefore **idempotent**. ### Step 4 — `DELETE` Request: Drop the Course **Request:** ``` DELETE /api/v1/students/2045/enrollments/887 HTTP/1.1 Host: api.ktuapp.in Authorization: Bearer abc123token ``` **Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 204 No Content ``` **Explanation:** No body is needed. The server returns `204 No Content` to confirm success. Calling `DELETE` again returns `404 Not Found` but the server state is unchanged — therefore **idempotent**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each HTTP Method > [!NOTE] > This decision matrix is the **single most important KTU revision table** for Module 4. | Decision Criterion | Use `GET` | Use `POST` | Use `PUT` | Use `PATCH` | Use `DELETE` | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Client wants to **read** a resource? | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | Client wants to **create** a new resource? | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | Client wants to **replace** entire resource? | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | Client wants to **modify** few fields only? | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | | Client wants to **remove** a resource? | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Should be **idempotent**? | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | Should be **safe**? | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | Can be **cached** by browsers? | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | Can be **bookmarked** in browser? | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 REST Client–Server Communication Flow > The following Mermaid block illustrates the **complete request–response lifecycle** of a RESTful interaction, including cache lookup, server processing, and response delivery. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant CL as CLIENT APP participant CA as CACHE LAYER participant LB as LOAD BALANCER participant API as REST API SERVER participant DB as DATABASE CL->>CA: GET /api/v1/students/2045 CA-->>CL: Cache MISS CL->>LB: Forward request (HTTP) LB->>API: Route to available instance API->>DB: SELECT * FROM students WHERE id=2045 DB-->>API: Result row API-->>LB: 200 OK + JSON representation LB-->>CA: Store response with Cache-Control CA-->>CL: Return final response Note over CL,DB: Entire flow is STATELESS
No session data retained ``` --- ## 4.2 REST Resource Hierarchy (Tree View) > Visual representation of a typical RESTful URI hierarchy for a university information system. ```mermaid graph TD ROOT[API Root /api/v1] STU[students Collection] STUID[students Item id] STUCRS[student Courses Sub Collection] STUCRSID[student Course Item] DEPT[departments Collection] DEPTID[department Item] DEPTSTU[department Students Sub Collection] ROOT --> STU ROOT --> DEPT STU --> STUID STUID --> STUCRS STUCRS --> STUCRSID DEPT --> DEPTID DEPTID --> DEPTSTU ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a RESTful Service > For complex topics, this block diagram shows the **internal layers** of a REST API and how a request traverses them. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENT_LAYER UI_A[Web Browser] UI_B[Mobile App] UI_C[Third Party Service] end subgraph EDGE_LAYER FW[Firewall] LB[Load Balancer] CDN[CDN Cache] end subgraph APP_LAYER RT[Request Router] AUTH[Auth Middleware] CTRL[Controllers] SVC[Business Logic Services] end subgraph DATA_LAYER ORM[ORM Mapper] DB1[(Primary Database)] DB2[(Replica Database)] end UI_A --> FW UI_B --> FW UI_C --> FW FW --> LB LB --> CDN CDN --> RT RT --> AUTH AUTH --> CTRL CTRL --> SVC SVC --> ORM ORM --> DB1 ORM --> DB2 ``` --- ## 4.4 HTTP Method Decision Flowchart > A quick-reference flowchart a student can reproduce in the exam to **justify method selection**. ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Client wants to interact with a resource] --> Q1{What is the intent?} Q1 -->|Read| GET[Use GET Method
Safe, Idempotent, Cacheable] Q1 -->|Create| POST[Use POST Method
Not Idempotent] Q1 -->|Replace Fully| PUT[Use PUT Method
Idempotent] Q1 -->|Update Partially| PATCH[Use PATCH Method] Q1 -->|Delete| DEL[Use DELETE Method
Idempotent] GET --> R1[Returns 200 OK + Representation] POST --> R2[Returns 201 Created + Location Header] PUT --> R3[Returns 200 OK or 204 No Content] PATCH --> R4[Returns 200 OK + Patched Representation] DEL --> R5[Returns 204 No Content] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > *Each Part A question carries 3 marks. Answer in 4–6 sentences. Definitions should be precise.* --- ### Q1. Define RESTful Web Services. List any four REST architectural constraints. **[Model Answer — KTU University Exam Pattern, Dec 2023 Style]** **Definition (≈1.5 marks):** A **RESTful Web Service** is a service that conforms to the architectural constraints of **REST (Representational State Transfer)**, an architectural style proposed by **Roy Fielding** in 2000. It uses **HTTP** as the transport protocol, **URIs** to identify **resources**, and standard **HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)** to perform **CRUD** operations. Data is exchanged as **representations**, typically in **JSON** or **XML** format. **Four REST Constraints (≈1.5 marks):** 1. **Client–Server** — Separation of concerns between the UI (client) and data storage (server). 2. **Stateless** — Each request is self-contained; the server stores no client session. 3. **Cacheable** — Responses must define whether they can be cached to improve performance. 4. **Uniform Interface** — Standardized way of communicating using URIs, methods, and representations. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between `PUT` and `POST` HTTP methods. **[Model Answer]** | Aspect | `POST` | `PUT` | |---|---|---| | **CRUD Operation** | Create a new resource | Create or **replace** an existing resource | | **URI Used** | The **collection** URI (e.g., `/students`) | The **specific item** URI (e.g., `/students/2045`) | | **Idempotency** | ❌ Not idempotent | ✅ Idempotent | | **Side Effects** | Creates a new sub-resource each time | Repeated calls yield the same final state | | **Typical Response** | `201 Created` + `Location` header | `200 OK` or `204 No Content` | | **Example** | `POST /students` to register a new student | `PUT /students/2045` to update that student | **Example (1 mark):** - `POST /api/v1/students` creates a new student with server-assigned ID. - `PUT /api/v1/students/2045` replaces the student with ID 2045. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** in 14-mark REST questions due to these common mistakes: > > 1. **Confusing `200 OK` with `201 Created`** — POST must return `201`; PUT may return `200` or `204`. > 2. **Writing URIs with verbs** (`/getStudent`, `/createStudent`) — URIs must contain **nouns only**. > 3. **Forgetting idempotency** — A common 3-mark sub-question asks for the idempotent property of DELETE. > 4. **Mixing up PATCH and PUT** — PATCH = partial update; PUT = full replacement. > 5. **Not including the `Location` header** in `201 Created` responses. > 6. **Omitting JSON request/response examples** — Always show at least one example. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### (a) Explain the six architectural constraints of REST. Why is *statelessness* considered the most important constraint for scalability? **(7 marks)** **[Model Answer]** **Introduction (1 mark):** REST is defined by six constraints. A system that violates any of the mandatory five is **not** considered truly RESTful. **The Six Constraints (4 marks):** 1. **Client–Server:** The user-interface concerns are separated from the data-storage concerns. The client and server evolve independently — for example, a mobile app can be redesigned without changing the server API. 2. **Stateless:** Each request from the client must contain **all** the information necessary to be understood by the server. The server does not store any client session between requests. Example: Authentication credentials are sent in **every** request via the `Authorization` header. 3. **Cacheable:** Responses must explicitly mark themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable using headers like `Cache-Control` and `ETag`. This allows clients and intermediaries to reuse responses, reducing network traffic. 4. **Uniform Interface:** This is the cornerstone of REST and is itself decomposed into four sub-constraints: - *Identification of resources* (via URIs), - *Manipulation through representations* (client acts on JSON/XML, not the resource itself), - *Self-descriptive messages* (`Content-Type` describes format), - *HATEOAS* (responses contain links to next possible actions). 5. **Layered System:** The architecture is composed of hierarchical layers (client → cache → load balancer → server → database). Each layer only "sees" the immediate layer it interacts with. 6. **Code on Demand (Optional):** Servers can temporarily extend client functionality by transferring executable code (e.g., JavaScript applets, scripts). **Why Statelessness Enables Scalability (2 marks):** Statelessness is the **key enabler of horizontal scaling**. Because the server retains no client state: - **Any server instance** in a server farm can handle **any incoming request** — there is no "session affinity" requirement. - New servers can be **added or removed** dynamically (auto-scaling) without disrupting users. - Failure recovery is trivial — a request can be **retried on a different server** without data loss. - **Caching and load balancing** become far more effective, since no per-user state lookup is needed. > **Example:** Amazon's e-commerce API handles billions of stateless requests per day. The state (cart, user preferences) is stored in a centralized database or sent in the request itself, allowing thousands of identical web servers to scale elastically. --- #### (b) Design RESTful URIs and illustrate the HTTP request–response cycle for the following operations on a *Library Management System*: **(7 marks)** 1. List all books 2. Get details of a specific book 3. Add a new book 4. Update the price of a book 5. Delete a book **[Model Answer]** **Step 1 — URI Design (2 marks):** | # | Operation | HTTP Method | URI | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | List all books | `GET` | `/api/v1/books` | | 2 | Get one book | `GET` | `/api/v1/books/{isbn}` | | 3 | Add a new book | `POST` | `/api/v1/books` | | 4 | Update price | `PATCH` | `/api/v1/books/{isbn}` | | 5 | Delete a book | `DELETE` | `/api/v1/books/{isbn}` | **Step 2 — Sample Request/Response (5 marks):** **Operation 1 — List all books:** ``` GET /api/v1/books?author=Tolkien HTTP/1.1 Host: library.ktu.ac.in Accept: application/json ``` **Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: application/json ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"books": [} \\ & \quad \left\{ \text{"isbn": "9780547928227", "title": "The Hobbit", "price": 499\right\}, \\ & \quad \left\{ \text{"isbn": "9780261103573", "title": "Fellowship of the Ring", "price": 599\right\} \\ & \left. \right] \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Operation 3 — Add a new book:** ``` POST /api/v1/books HTTP/1.1 Host: library.ktu.ac.in Content-Type: application/json ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"isbn": "9780547928227",} \\ & \text{"title": "The Silmarillion",} \\ & \text{"author": "J.R.R. Tolkien",} \\ & \text{"price": 699, "stock": 25} \\ & \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ **Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 201 Created Location: /api/v1/books/9780547928227 Content-Type: application/json ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"message": "Book created successfully",} \\ & \text{"isbn": "9780547928227", "createdAt": "2024-08-12"} \\ & \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Operation 4 — Update price (PATCH for partial update):** ``` PATCH /api/v1/books/9780547928227 HTTP/1.1 Host: library.ktu.ac.in Content-Type: application/json ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"price": 649} \\ & \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ **Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: application/json ``` **Operation 5 — Delete a book:** ``` DELETE /api/v1/books/9780547928227 HTTP/1.1 Host: library.ktu.ac.in ``` **Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 204 No Content ``` **Conclusion:** The URIs are **noun-based, hierarchical, and versioned**. The HTTP methods correctly map to CRUD operations, and the responses use the proper status codes (`200`, `201`, `204`). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### (a) Explain the four primary HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) with their properties: *safe*, *idempotent*, *cacheable*, and supported request/response bodies. Provide one example URI for each. **(7 marks)** **[Model Answer]** **Introduction (1 mark):** The four primary HTTP methods map directly to the **CRUD** (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations in persistent storage. **1. `GET` — Retrieve a Resource (1.5 marks):** - **Purpose:** Read or fetch a representation of a resource. - **Safe:** ✅ Yes (does not modify server state). - **Idempotent:** ✅ Yes (repeated calls return the same result). - **Cacheable:** ✅ Yes. - **Body in request:** ❌ No. - **Body in response:** ✅ Yes (the resource representation). - **Example:** `GET /api/v1/students/2045` → Returns the student with ID 2045. **2. `POST` — Create a New Resource (1.5 marks):** - **Purpose:** Submit data to create a new sub-resource. - **Safe:** ❌ No. - **Idempotent:** ❌ No (each call creates a new resource with a new ID). - **Cacheable:** ❌ No. - **Body in request:** ✅ Yes. - **Body in response:** ✅ Yes (typically the new resource). - **Example:** `POST /api/v1/students` with a JSON body → Returns `201 Created` + `Location: /api/v1/students/2046`. **3. `PUT` — Create or Replace a Resource (1.5 marks):** - **Purpose:** Replace the **entire** resource at the given URI. - **Safe:** ❌ No. - **Idempotent:** ✅ Yes (sending the same body multiple times yields the same final state). - **Cacheable:** ❌ No. - **Body in request:** ✅ Yes (complete replacement representation). - **Body in response:** Optional (often empty for `204`). - **Example:** `PUT /api/v1/students/2045` with full student JSON → Updates/replaces the student. **4. `DELETE` — Remove a Resource (1.5 marks):** - **Purpose:** Delete the resource at the given URI. - **Safe:** ❌ No. - **Idempotent:** ✅ Yes (the first call deletes; subsequent calls return `404` but the final state is the same — no resource). - **Cacheable:** ❌ No. - **Body in request:** ❌ No. - **Body in response:** Optional. - **Example:** `DELETE /api/v1/students/2045` → Returns `204 No Content`. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"GET is the Gentle One"** — it is the only method that is both **Safe** AND naturally **Idempotent** AND **Cacheable**. --- #### (b) Compare SOAP and RESTful web services across any seven parameters. Describe the structure of an HTTP request and response message with a labelled diagram. **(7 marks)** **[Model Answer]** **Comparison Table (3.5 marks):** | # | Parameter | SOAP | REST | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Type** | Protocol with strict specification | Architectural style with loose guidelines | | 2 | **Data Format** | XML only | JSON, XML, HTML, YAML, plain text | | 3 | **Statefulness** | Can be stateful (uses WS-*) | Strictly stateless | | 4 | **Transport** | HTTP, SMTP, JMS, TCP | HTTP/HTTPS only | | 5 | **Bandwidth** | Heavy (verbose XML envelopes) | Lightweight (compact JSON) | | 6 | **Contract Definition** | WSDL required (machine-readable) | Optional (OpenAPI/Swagger) | | 7 | **Security** | WS-Security, SSL | HTTPS, OAuth2, JWT, API keys | | 8 | **Error Handling** | `` blocks | HTTP status codes (4xx, 5xx) | | 9 | **Caching** | Not supported by default | Native HTTP caching support | | 10 | **Use Case** | Banking, enterprise integration | Public APIs, mobile, IoT, web apps | **HTTP Message Structure (3.5 marks):** An **HTTP Request** has four parts: ``` +---------------------------------------+ | REQUEST LINE | | Method | URI | HTTP-Version | +---------------------------------------+ | HEADERS (key: value pairs) | | Host: ... | | Content-Type: ... | | Authorization: ... | +---------------------------------------+ | | +---------------------------------------+ | BODY (optional) | | JSON / XML / form data | +---------------------------------------+ ``` An **HTTP Response** has four parts: ``` +---------------------------------------+ | STATUS LINE | | HTTP-Version | Code | Reason-Phrase| +---------------------------------------+ | HEADERS | | Content-Type: ... | | Date: ... | | Location: ... | +---------------------------------------+ | | +---------------------------------------+ | BODY (optional) | | JSON / XML representation | +---------------------------------------+ ``` **Example Response:** ``` HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found Content-Type: application/json Content-Length: 58 ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Body} = \left\{ \right. & \text{"error": "StudentNotFound",} \\ & \text{"message": "No student with ID 9999"} \\ & \left. \right\} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4** ### 🔑 Core REST Concepts - **REST** = **R**epresentational **S**tate **T**ransfer (Roy Fielding, 2000). - A **RESTful service** exposes **resources** (nouns) via **URIs** and is manipulated via **HTTP methods** (verbs). - A **resource** is any named concept: user, order, book, etc. - A **representation** is a snapshot of a resource's state in JSON, XML, etc. ### 🔑 The 6 REST Constraints (Memorize Order) 1. Client–Server 2. Stateless 3. Cacheable 4. Uniform Interface (with 4 sub-constraints) 5. Layered System 6. Code on Demand (optional) ### 🔑 HTTP Methods (CRUD + Properties) - **`GET`** → Read → Safe ✅, Idempotent ✅, Cacheable ✅, No request body. - **`POST`** → Create → Not safe, Not idempotent, Has request body → `201 Created` + `Location` header. - **`PUT`** → Full update/replace → Idempotent ✅, Has request body. - **`PATCH`** → Partial update → Not idempotent. - **`DELETE`** → Remove → Idempotent ✅, `204 No Content` typical. ### 🔑 URI Design Golden Rules - Use **plural nouns** (`/students`). - Use **lowercase + hyphens** (`/academic-records`). - **No verbs** in URIs. - Use **path variables** for hierarchy and **query parameters** for filters. - **Version the API** (`/api/v1/`). - No file extensions (use `Accept` header). ### 🔑 Status Code Categories - **1xx** — Informational - **2xx** — Success (`200`, `201`, `204`) - **3xx** — Redirection (`301`, `304`) - **4xx** — Client error (`400`, `401`, `403`, `404`, `409`) - **5xx** — Server error (`500`, `503`) ### 🔑 Key Distinctions to Survive the Exam | Question | Correct Answer | |---|---| | After successful `POST`? | `201 Created` + `Location` header | | After successful `DELETE`? | `204 No Content` | | After successful `GET`? | `200 OK` + resource in body | | Is `GET` idempotent? | ✅ Yes | | Is `POST` idempotent? | ❌ No | | Is `DELETE` safe? | ❌ No (it modifies state) | | SOAP vs REST data format? | SOAP = XML only; REST = JSON/XML/HTML | | Stateless means? | Server stores **no** client session data | ### 🔑 Advantages of REST Scalability, simplicity, language-independence, lightweight payloads (JSON), standard HTTP infrastructure, browser-friendly, easy to test (Postman, cURL, Swagger). > **Best of luck for your KTU University Examination! 🎓**
